passage: A study of 500 employees found that those who took regular breaks during work hours were 30% more productive than those who worked continuously. The company's management concluded that implementing mandatory break times would increase overall productivity.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the conclusion drawn above?

options.text: The study was conducted during the summer months when workloads are typically lighter.
Employees who chose to take regular breaks were already among the most motivated workers.
Some companies that have implemented mandatory breaks reported mixed results.
The study measured productivity using output per hour rather than total daily output.
Not all employees in the study took the same number of breaks.

explanation: Premise: Break-takers are 30% more productive. Conclusion: Mandatory breaks will increase productivity. Gap: Assumes breaks CAUSED productivity. B introduces selection bias — motivated workers chose breaks AND were productive. The breaks may not be the cause.

passage: Sales of organic produce at GreenMart have doubled over the past year. The store manager attributes this increase to the store's new advertising campaign promoting the health benefits of organic food.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the manager's explanation?

options.text: Several competing stores in the area closed during the past year, redirecting customers to GreenMart.
The advertising campaign cost more than the additional revenue from organic produce sales.
Organic produce is generally more expensive than conventionally grown produce.
GreenMart also sells a wide variety of non-organic products.
Some customers prefer to buy organic produce at farmers' markets.

explanation: Premise: Organic sales doubled. Conclusion: Ad campaign caused the increase. Gap: Assumes no alternative explanation. A provides an alternative cause — competitor closures drove customers to GreenMart.

passage: A city council notes that neighborhoods with more public libraries tend to have lower crime rates. The council has therefore proposed building new libraries in high-crime neighborhoods to reduce criminal activity.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, would most weaken the council's proposal?

options.text: Libraries require significant ongoing funding for staffing and maintenance.
Wealthier neighborhoods, which can already afford more community resources, tend to have both more libraries and lower crime rates.
Some high-crime neighborhoods already have community centers.
The city has successfully reduced crime through increased police patrols in the past.
Public libraries offer programs that are popular with residents of all ages.

explanation: Premise: More libraries correlate with less crime. Conclusion: Building libraries will reduce crime. Gap: Correlation assumed to be causation. B identifies a confounding variable — wealth explains both libraries and lower crime.

passage: After a new speed limit was implemented on Highway 12, the number of traffic accidents on that highway decreased by 20%. Transportation officials concluded that lower speed limits are an effective way to reduce traffic accidents.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the officials' conclusion?

options.text: The speed limit change was accompanied by the installation of new guardrails and improved road lighting.
Accidents on other highways in the region also decreased during the same period.
Some drivers on Highway 12 were observed exceeding the new speed limit.
The new speed limit added an average of five minutes to commuters' travel time.
Highway 12 is one of the busiest highways in the state.

explanation: Premise: Accidents fell 20% after speed limit change. Conclusion: Lower speed limits reduce accidents. Gap: Assumes only the speed limit caused the decrease. A provides alternative causes (guardrails, lighting) that could explain the drop.

passage: A pharmaceutical company found that patients who took its new vitamin supplement reported feeling more energetic. The company concluded that the supplement effectively boosts energy levels.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the company's conclusion?

options.text: The supplement contains vitamins that are already present in most multivitamins.
Patients in the study were not compared with a group taking a placebo.
Energy levels can be influenced by sleep quality and exercise.
The supplement has not been approved by regulatory authorities.
Some patients reported mild side effects from the supplement.

explanation: Premise: Patients reported more energy. Conclusion: Supplement boosts energy. Gap: No control group to rule out placebo effect. B directly exposes this flaw — without a placebo comparison, the energy boost may be psychological.

passage: Riverside School District switched to a new math curriculum last year, and average test scores rose by 15 points. The district superintendent concluded that the new curriculum is superior to the old one.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the superintendent's conclusion?

options.text: Teachers received 40 hours of additional training to implement the new curriculum.
The new curriculum covers fewer topics than the old one.
The standardized test used this year was redesigned to be easier than previous versions.
Some parents expressed dissatisfaction with the new curriculum.
Math scores in neighboring districts remained unchanged.

explanation: Premise: Scores rose 15 points after curriculum change. Conclusion: New curriculum is better. Gap: Assumes nothing else changed. C — the test itself got easier, so the score increase doesn't reflect curriculum quality.

passage: A restaurant chain introduced a loyalty rewards program and saw a 25% increase in repeat customers over the following quarter. Management concluded that the loyalty program was responsible for the increase in repeat business.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the management's conclusion?

options.text: The loyalty program offers relatively modest rewards compared to competitors' programs.
The restaurant chain also significantly lowered its menu prices at the beginning of the same quarter.
Not all repeat customers enrolled in the loyalty program.
The restaurant chain has locations in over 30 states.
Repeat customers tend to spend more per visit than first-time customers.

explanation: Premise: Repeat customers increased 25%. Conclusion: Loyalty program caused it. Gap: Assumes no other factor drove increase. B provides a strong alternative cause — lower prices independently explain more repeat visits.

passage: A survey found that people who read at least one book per month score higher on vocabulary tests than those who read less frequently. An educator argued that schools should require students to read one book per month to improve their vocabulary.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the educator's argument?

options.text: Many students already read more than one book per month voluntarily.
People with larger vocabularies tend to enjoy reading more and therefore read more frequently.
Vocabulary tests do not measure all aspects of language ability.
Some schools already have required reading programs.
Reading digital content such as articles can also improve vocabulary.

explanation: Premise: Frequent readers have better vocabulary. Conclusion: Required reading will improve vocabulary. Gap: Assumes reading causes vocabulary growth. B reverses the causation — larger vocabulary leads to more reading.

passage: A town installed solar-powered streetlights on Elm Street and found that nighttime pedestrian accidents dropped by 40% that year. The town council concluded that solar-powered streetlights are safer than the traditional streetlights used elsewhere in town.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the council's conclusion?

options.text: Solar-powered streetlights are more expensive to install than traditional ones.
The new streetlights on Elm Street are twice as bright as the traditional lights they replaced, regardless of power source.
Pedestrian traffic on Elm Street decreased significantly that year due to a road construction detour.
Some residents reported that the new lights were too bright at night.
The traditional streetlights in the rest of town are over twenty years old.

explanation: Premise: Accidents dropped 40% after solar lights. Conclusion: Solar lights are safer. Gap: Assumes lights caused the drop. C — fewer pedestrians means fewer accidents, regardless of lighting.

passage: Company X switched from individual offices to an open floor plan and saw collaboration among teams increase. The CEO concluded that open floor plans foster better teamwork.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the CEO's conclusion?

options.text: Several employees have complained about noise levels in the new open floor plan.
The switch to an open floor plan coincided with the introduction of new team-building initiatives and collaborative software tools.
Open floor plans are increasingly popular among technology companies.
The company also hired several new employees when the floor plan changed.
Some teams reported that they preferred working in individual offices.

explanation: Premise: Collaboration increased after open floor plan. Conclusion: Open plan fosters teamwork. Gap: Assumes open plan caused the increase. B provides alternative causes — team-building initiatives and new software.

passage: Since the city introduced free public Wi-Fi in its downtown parks, the number of visitors to those parks has increased by 35%. City planners argue that free Wi-Fi is an effective strategy for increasing park usage.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the planners' argument?

options.text: The city also renovated the park playgrounds and added new walking trails during the same period.
Free public Wi-Fi is available in many coffee shops and restaurants near the parks.
Not all park visitors use the Wi-Fi service.
Maintaining the Wi-Fi infrastructure is costly for the city.
Parks in other cities without free Wi-Fi have also seen increased visitation.

explanation: Premise: Park visits up 35% after Wi-Fi. Conclusion: Wi-Fi drove the increase. Gap: Assumes Wi-Fi is the cause. A provides strong alternative causes — renovated playgrounds and new trails.

passage: Employees at Techno Inc. who participate in the company's wellness program have 30% fewer sick days than non-participants. The HR department concluded that the wellness program reduces employee illness.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the HR department's conclusion?

options.text: The wellness program includes gym membership, nutrition counseling, and stress management workshops.
Employees who are generally healthier and rarely get sick are more likely to sign up for the wellness program.
Techno Inc. spends a significant portion of its budget on the wellness program.
Some employees who participate in the wellness program still take sick days occasionally.
Other companies in the industry have similar wellness programs.

explanation: Premise: Wellness participants have fewer sick days. Conclusion: Program reduces illness. Gap: Assumes participation causes better health. B shows self-selection bias — healthy people join the program.

passage: After a hospital introduced a new hand-washing protocol, the rate of hospital-acquired infections dropped by 35%. The hospital's chief medical officer concluded that stricter hand-washing protocols are the most effective method for reducing hospital infections.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the officer's conclusion?

options.text: The hospital simultaneously implemented new air filtration systems and upgraded its sterilization equipment.
Hand-washing compliance among staff had been low before the new protocol.
Some hospital-acquired infections are caused by viruses that are not eliminated by hand-washing.
The hospital is one of the largest in the region.
Other hospitals have also seen decreases in infection rates.

explanation: Premise: Infections dropped 35% after hand-washing protocol. Conclusion: Hand-washing is the most effective method. Gap: Assumes hand-washing caused the drop. A provides simultaneous changes that could account for improvement.

passage: MegaCorp's research division found that teams using its proprietary project management software completed projects 15% faster than teams using competitors' software. MegaCorp claims that its software makes teams more efficient.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens MegaCorp's claim?

options.text: MegaCorp's software is more expensive than most competing project management tools.
The teams using MegaCorp's software were composed of senior employees, while competitor-software teams included more junior staff.
MegaCorp's software has more features than most competitors' offerings.
Project completion time is only one measure of team efficiency.
Some of the teams using competitors' software completed their projects ahead of schedule.

explanation: Premise: MegaCorp teams finished 15% faster. Conclusion: Software makes teams efficient. Gap: Assumes fair comparison. B shows the groups weren't comparable — senior vs. junior employees — so the difference reflects skill, not software.

passage: A recent study found that regions with higher coffee consumption per capita have significantly lower rates of Type 2 diabetes. Researchers suggested that moderate coffee consumption may help prevent Type 2 diabetes.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the researchers' suggestion?

options.text: Coffee contains several compounds that have been shown to affect insulin sensitivity in laboratory settings.
Regions with higher coffee consumption also tend to have higher average incomes and greater access to healthcare.
Some individuals who consume large amounts of coffee still develop Type 2 diabetes.
Tea, which also contains caffeine, has not been similarly associated with lower diabetes rates.
The study did not track participants over an extended period.

explanation: Premise: Higher coffee consumption correlates with lower diabetes. Conclusion: Coffee may prevent diabetes. Gap: Correlation vs. causation. B identifies confounding variables — income and healthcare access.

passage: An electric car manufacturer notes that countries offering substantial tax incentives for electric vehicles have seen faster adoption. The manufacturer argues that governments worldwide should introduce similar tax incentives to accelerate the transition to electric vehicles.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the manufacturer's argument?

options.text: Electric vehicles have lower operating costs than gasoline-powered vehicles.
The countries with the highest adoption rates also have extensive charging infrastructure built by private companies before the incentives were introduced.
Some consumers prefer electric cars for environmental reasons regardless of price.
Tax incentives for electric vehicles can be expensive for governments to sustain.
Gasoline prices have been rising steadily in many countries.

explanation: Premise: Countries with EV tax incentives have faster adoption. Conclusion: Incentives should be introduced globally. Gap: Assumes incentives caused adoption. B shows pre-existing charging infrastructure could explain adoption instead.

passage: A pharmaceutical company argues that its new drug is effective at lowering cholesterol because in clinical trials, patients who took the drug showed a 25% reduction in LDL cholesterol after six months. Patients in the control group showed only a 5% reduction.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the company's argument?

options.text: The patients in the drug group were also required to follow a strict low-fat diet and exercise regimen, while the control group received no such guidance.
LDL cholesterol is only one of several indicators of cardiovascular health.
The drug was tested on patients with extremely high cholesterol at the start of the trial.
A few patients in the drug group experienced mild side effects.
The company has other cholesterol drugs already on the market.

explanation: Premise: Drug group had 25% LDL reduction vs. 5% for control. Conclusion: Drug lowers cholesterol. Gap: Assumes drug caused the difference. A shows the drug group had additional interventions (diet, exercise), violating experimental design.

passage: A technology company found that developers who use its new code editor complete coding tasks 20% faster. The company claims this proves that its editor is the most productive development tool available.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the company's claim?

options.text: The new editor has features specifically designed to autocomplete common coding patterns.
The developers testing the new editor were selected from the company's own highly skilled engineering team, while those using other editors were drawn from a general survey.
The tasks used in the test were relatively simple and may not represent complex real-world projects.
The company's editor is only available for certain operating systems.
Speed of task completion is one of several metrics used to evaluate developer productivity.

explanation: Premise: Developers with the new editor are 20% faster. Conclusion: Editor is most productive. Gap: Assumes fair comparison. B shows groups weren't comparable — elite engineers vs. general population.

passage: A study of urban communities found that neighborhoods with more green spaces have lower rates of respiratory illness. City health officials proposed increasing green spaces in all neighborhoods to improve respiratory health.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the officials' proposal?

options.text: Green spaces can help filter air pollutants.
Neighborhoods with more green spaces tend to be farther from major highways and industrial areas that produce air pollution.
Respiratory illness can be caused by indoor factors such as mold and poor ventilation.
The study measured respiratory illness using hospital admission records.
Some neighborhoods lack available land for creating new green spaces.

explanation: Premise: More green space correlates with less respiratory illness. Conclusion: Adding green space will improve respiratory health. Gap: Correlation vs. causation. B shows distance from pollution sources is the confounding variable.

passage: A management consultant found that companies that invest more than 5% of revenue in employee training have higher profit margins than those that invest less. The consultant recommends that all companies increase training budgets to at least 5% of revenue.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the consultant's recommendation?

options.text: Employee training can take various forms, including classroom instruction, online courses, and on-the-job mentoring.
Companies with higher profit margins have more available funds to invest in employee training.
The consultant's study included companies from various industries.
Some companies that invest heavily in training experience high employee turnover.
Training budgets as a percentage of revenue vary widely across industries.

explanation: Premise: Higher training spend correlates with higher profits. Conclusion: Increase training to boost profits. Gap: Assumes training causes profits. B shows reverse causation — profitable companies can afford more training.

passage: Country Z banned the use of a common pesticide, and bee populations in the country increased by 40% over the following two years. Environmental advocates concluded that the pesticide was the primary cause of bee population decline in Country Z.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the advocates' conclusion?

options.text: The banned pesticide had been in use in Country Z for over two decades.
During the same two-year period, an unusually favorable climate resulted in an abundance of wildflowers that serve as bee food sources.
Some bee species in Country Z are still declining despite the ban.
Other countries that have not banned the pesticide have also seen some bee recovery.
The pesticide was used primarily on crops that are not pollinated by bees.

explanation: Premise: Bees increased after pesticide ban. Conclusion: Pesticide was the primary cause of decline. Gap: Assumes ban caused recovery. B provides a strong alternative — favorable climate and abundant food could explain the increase.

passage: A school district implemented a later start time for high school, moving from 7:30 AM to 8:45 AM. In the following semester, students' average grades improved by half a letter grade. The school board concluded that later start times lead to better academic performance.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the school board's conclusion?

options.text: Adolescents' circadian rhythms naturally shift, making it difficult for them to fall asleep before 11 PM.
The semester with later start times also introduced a new tutoring program and smaller class sizes.
Students reported feeling less tired with the later start time.
Schools with earlier start times in other districts did not see grade improvements.
The later start time required shortening the lunch period by fifteen minutes.

explanation: Premise: Grades improved after later start time. Conclusion: Later start times improve academics. Gap: Assumes the start time caused improvement. B provides concurrent changes (tutoring, smaller classes) that could explain the grade increase.

passage: A health insurance company found that policyholders who use its telemedicine service have 20% lower annual medical costs than those who do not. The company promotes telemedicine as a cost-saving solution for all policyholders.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the company's promotion?

options.text: Telemedicine consultations are less expensive than in-person doctor visits.
Policyholders who use telemedicine tend to be younger and healthier than those who prefer in-person visits.
The telemedicine service is available to all policyholders at no additional charge.
Some medical conditions require in-person examination and cannot be addressed via telemedicine.
The number of policyholders using telemedicine has been growing each year.

explanation: Premise: Telemedicine users have 20% lower costs. Conclusion: Telemedicine saves money for all. Gap: Assumes telemedicine causes lower costs. B shows selection bias — younger, healthier people use telemedicine and naturally have lower costs.

passage: An art museum increased its admission fee from $15 to $25 and saw a 10% increase in revenue the following year. The museum director concluded that raising prices is an effective strategy for increasing museum revenue.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the director's conclusion?

options.text: The museum hosted a blockbuster exhibition of a world-famous artist during the year the price was raised.
The museum offers free admission on the first Sunday of each month.
Some visitors reported that the higher admission fee made them perceive the museum as more prestigious.
The museum's operating costs also increased during the year.
Other museums in the region did not change their admission fees.

explanation: Premise: Revenue increased 10% after price hike. Conclusion: Raising prices increases revenue. Gap: Assumes price caused revenue increase. A provides an alternative — a blockbuster exhibition drove attendance and revenue regardless of price.

passage: A study found that employees who participate in mindfulness meditation programs at work report 40% less stress than non-participants. The HR director concluded that the meditation program is effective at reducing workplace stress.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the director's conclusion?

options.text: The meditation program sessions last only 15 minutes per day.
Employees who were already less stressed and more interested in wellness were the ones who volunteered for the program.
Some participants in the meditation program also engage in physical exercise regularly.
Workplace stress has been linked to higher turnover rates.
The study relied on participants' self-reported stress levels rather than physiological measurements.

explanation: Premise: Meditation participants report 40% less stress. Conclusion: Program reduces stress. Gap: Assumes meditation caused the reduction. B shows self-selection — less-stressed, wellness-oriented employees volunteered.

passage: After introducing a four-day workweek trial, a software firm saw productivity increase by 12% and employee satisfaction rise significantly. The CEO concluded that a permanent four-day workweek would maintain these benefits indefinitely.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the CEO's conclusion?

options.text: During the trial, employees knew they were being evaluated and worked especially hard to ensure the trial's success.
Several other technology companies have also experimented with shorter workweeks.
The four-day trial period lasted only eight weeks.
Employees reported using their extra day off for personal errands and family time.
The company's revenue was not significantly affected during the trial.

explanation: Premise: Productivity and satisfaction rose during trial. Conclusion: Permanent four-day week will maintain benefits. Gap: Assumes trial results will persist. A shows the Hawthorne effect — employees tried harder because they knew they were being watched, an unsustainable behavior.

passage: A consumer report showed that electric toothbrushes remove 21% more plaque than manual toothbrushes. Dentists are therefore recommending that all patients switch to electric toothbrushes for better oral hygiene.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the dentists' recommendation?

options.text: Electric toothbrushes are significantly more expensive than manual toothbrushes.
The study participants using manual toothbrushes were not given instruction on proper brushing technique, while electric toothbrush users received detailed guidance.
Plaque removal is the most important factor in preventing gum disease.
Some manual toothbrush users in the study already had good oral hygiene.
Electric toothbrushes come in a variety of models with different features.

explanation: Premise: Electric toothbrushes removed 21% more plaque. Conclusion: Everyone should switch. Gap: Assumes fair comparison. B shows the test was biased — electric users got instruction while manual users did not.

passage: A university observed that students who live on campus have a graduation rate 20 percentage points higher than commuter students. The university president argued that building more dormitories would increase the overall graduation rate.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the president's argument?

options.text: On-campus housing is more expensive than typical off-campus apartments.
Students who can afford on-campus housing tend to come from wealthier families that also provide stronger academic support systems.
The university requires first-year students to live on campus.
Living on campus provides students with easier access to libraries and tutoring centers.
Some commuter students live very close to the university.

explanation: Premise: On-campus students graduate at higher rates. Conclusion: More dorms will increase graduation. Gap: Assumes housing causes higher graduation. B shows a confounding variable — wealth provides both on-campus housing and academic support.

passage: After a popular food blog rated Restaurant Maris as its top pick, the restaurant's reservations tripled within a month. The restaurant owner attributes the increase entirely to the blog review.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the owner's attribution?

options.text: The blog has a readership of over 500,000 food enthusiasts.
Restaurant Maris hired a new award-winning chef and redesigned its menu two weeks before the blog review was published.
Several other restaurants were also highly rated in the same blog post.
Reservations at other restaurants mentioned in the blog also increased.
The restaurant had previously received positive reviews from local newspapers.

explanation: Premise: Reservations tripled after blog review. Conclusion: Blog caused the increase. Gap: Assumes blog was the only factor. B provides a strong alternative — a new chef and menu could independently drive interest.

passage: A government report found that cities with more bicycle lanes have lower rates of traffic congestion. Transportation planners recommended building bicycle lanes on major roads in congested cities to alleviate traffic.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the planners' recommendation?

options.text: Building bicycle lanes on major roads would require eliminating one lane of vehicle traffic.
Cities that already have extensive bicycle lane networks tend to be smaller and more compact than cities with severe congestion.
Cycling has health benefits for commuters who switch from driving.
Some studies suggest that bicycle lanes can increase property values along their routes.
The report measured congestion using average commute times.

explanation: Premise: More bike lanes correlate with less congestion. Conclusion: Build bike lanes to reduce congestion. Gap: Correlation vs. causation. B shows a confound — smaller, compact cities naturally have both more bike lanes and less congestion.

passage: A cosmetics company found that its products sold better in stores where sales associates received its in-house training program. The company's executives concluded that the training program is the key to increasing sales.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the executives' conclusion?

options.text: The training program takes three days to complete.
Stores selected for the training program were already the company's highest-performing retail locations.
The training program covers product knowledge, customer engagement, and sales techniques.
Sales associates who completed the training reported feeling more confident in their product recommendations.
Competing cosmetics companies also offer training to retail sales associates.

explanation: Premise: Trained-store sales are higher. Conclusion: Training drives sales. Gap: Assumes training caused higher sales. B shows selection bias — already top-performing stores were chosen for training.

passage: Researchers observed that children who learn to play a musical instrument before age seven score higher on standardized math tests in middle school. A parenting magazine advised that parents should enroll young children in music lessons to improve their future math ability.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the magazine's advice?

options.text: Learning music requires pattern recognition skills that are also used in mathematics.
Parents who enroll children in early music lessons also tend to provide more academic enrichment activities and homework support at home.
Not all children enjoy playing musical instruments.
The study controlled for the type of musical instrument learned.
Music education funding has been cut in many school districts.

explanation: Premise: Early musicians score higher on math. Conclusion: Music lessons will improve math. Gap: Assumes music causes math ability. B shows confounding — parents who invest in music also invest in academic support.

passage: A ride-sharing company reported that riders who use its premium service rate their experience 4.8 out of 5 on average, compared to 4.2 for its standard service. The company concluded that the premium service provides a superior customer experience.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the company's conclusion?

options.text: The premium service costs approximately three times as much as the standard service.
Customers who choose the premium service have higher expectations and are known to rate all services they use more generously.
Premium service vehicles are newer and more comfortable than standard service vehicles.
The company recently updated its rating system from a 3-point to a 5-point scale.
Standard service riders take more trips per month on average than premium riders.

explanation: Premise: Premium riders rate higher (4.8 vs 4.2). Conclusion: Premium service is superior. Gap: Assumes the rating difference reflects service quality. B shows a rating bias — premium customers rate everything more generously, so the difference may not reflect actual experience quality.

passage: A national study found that states that increased funding for public preschool programs saw improved third-grade reading scores five years later. Education policy analysts recommended that all states increase preschool funding to boost elementary reading achievement.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the analysts' recommendation?

options.text: Reading proficiency by third grade is a strong predictor of future academic success.
The states that increased preschool funding were also those that simultaneously raised teacher certification requirements and adopted evidence-based elementary reading curricula.
Some children who did not attend preschool also achieved high reading scores in third grade.
The amount of funding increase varied significantly from state to state.
Private preschool programs in those states did not show similar improvements in outcomes.

explanation: Premise: States with more preschool funding had better 3rd-grade reading. Conclusion: Increase preschool funding everywhere. Gap: Assumes funding caused the improvement. B introduces concurrent reforms (teacher certification, new curricula) that could explain the reading gains independently.

passage: A technology firm that adopted an artificial intelligence system for screening job applicants found that new hires made through the AI system had 15% higher performance ratings after one year than those hired through traditional methods. The firm concluded that AI-based screening produces better employees.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the firm's conclusion?

options.text: The AI system uses algorithms trained on data from the firm's past highest-performing employees.
Managers who knew a new hire was selected by the AI system rated that employee more favorably in performance reviews due to their trust in the technology.
The firm received more applications during the period when the AI system was in use.
AI-based hiring systems have been adopted by many large corporations.
Some employees hired through traditional methods outperformed those hired through AI.

explanation: Premise: AI-selected hires have 15% higher performance ratings. Conclusion: AI produces better employees. Gap: Assumes the ratings are objective. B shows rater bias — managers who know about AI selection give higher ratings because of trust in the technology, inflating scores regardless of actual performance.

passage: A health research institute found that people who regularly consume probiotic supplements have a 30% lower incidence of gastrointestinal disorders compared to non-users. The institute recommends probiotic supplementation as a preventive measure against digestive diseases.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the institute's recommendation?

options.text: Probiotics contain live bacteria that are similar to beneficial microorganisms found in the human digestive tract.
People who take probiotic supplements also tend to maintain healthier overall diets, exercise regularly, and avoid processed foods.
Some probiotic supplements contain different strains of bacteria than others.
The study tracked participants over a three-year period.
Gastrointestinal disorders have multiple causes, including genetic factors.

explanation: Premise: Probiotic users have fewer GI disorders. Conclusion: Probiotics prevent digestive diseases. Gap: Assumes probiotics cause the lower incidence. B shows confounding — probiotic users have an overall healthier lifestyle that independently protects against GI disorders.

passage: In a double-blind trial, patients receiving a new antidepressant showed a 45% improvement in depression scores after 12 weeks, compared to 30% improvement in the placebo group. The pharmaceutical company argues this demonstrates clear clinical effectiveness of the drug.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the company's argument?

options.text: The improvement scale used in the study ranges from 0 to 100 points.

Clinical guidelines typically require that the drug-placebo difference exceed 20 percentage points to be considered clinically meaningful, as smaller differences are often attributable to side effects unblinding participants.
The new drug costs significantly more than existing antidepressants on the market.
Some patients in the drug group reported feeling better within the first two weeks.
The placebo group's 30% improvement is consistent with rates seen in other antidepressant trials.

explanation: Premise: Drug group improved 45% vs. placebo 30%. Conclusion: Drug is clinically effective. Gap: Assumes the 15-point difference is meaningful. B shows that clinical guidelines require >20-point differences for meaningful effect, and smaller gaps may result from side-effect unblinding rather than therapeutic benefit.

passage: Countries that have privatized their national railway systems have seen an increase in the number of routes served and a decline in average ticket prices. Free-market advocates argue that privatization is the best approach for improving rail service in any country.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the advocates' argument?

options.text: Privatized railways often receive government subsidies to maintain unprofitable rural routes.
The countries that privatized their railways were those with the most outdated and inefficient state-run systems, while countries with already high-performing public railways did not privatize.
Private railway companies must generate profits to satisfy shareholders.
Some privatized railways have experienced safety concerns due to cost-cutting measures.
Rail travel competes with air travel for long-distance routes.

explanation: Premise: Privatized railways show more routes and lower prices. Conclusion: Privatization is universally best. Gap: Assumes what worked in those countries will work everywhere. B shows selection bias — only poorly run systems privatized, so improvement was likely regardless, and already well-run systems wouldn't benefit.

passage: A longitudinal study following 10,000 adults over 20 years found that those who maintained social networks of more than 15 close relationships had a 50% lower risk of developing dementia. Neurologists have suggested that actively expanding one's social circle could help prevent cognitive decline.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the neurologists' suggestion?

options.text: Social interaction stimulates multiple brain regions involved in memory and executive function.
Early-stage dementia, often undiagnosed, causes subtle personality changes that gradually shrink a person's social network years before clinical symptoms appear.
The study controlled for education level and socioeconomic status.
Participants defined 'close relationships' differently.
Physical exercise has also been linked to reduced dementia risk.

explanation: Premise: Larger social networks correlate with lower dementia risk. Conclusion: Expanding social circles prevents cognitive decline. Gap: Assumes social networks cause protection. B shows reverse causation — early dementia itself reduces social networks, so smaller networks are a symptom, not a cause.

passage: An investment firm found that its portfolios managed by algorithms outperformed those managed by human analysts by 3% annually over the past decade. The firm's CEO argued that algorithm-managed portfolios are inherently superior to human-managed ones.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the CEO's argument?

options.text: Algorithmic trading has become increasingly common in the financial industry.
The decade in question featured a prolonged bull market with steady growth patterns that are particularly well-suited to algorithmic trend-following strategies but rarely occur historically.
The firm employs some of the most talented software engineers in the financial sector.
Past performance is generally acknowledged not to guarantee future results.
Some human-managed portfolios at the firm outperformed the algorithms in individual years.

explanation: Premise: Algorithms outperformed humans by 3% over a decade. Conclusion: Algorithms are inherently superior. Gap: Assumes past performance generalizes. B shows the decade was atypical — steady bull market suited algorithms specifically, so the outperformance may not persist under different conditions.

passage: A city that installed extensive surveillance camera networks in its downtown area saw a 30% decrease in reported street crime over two years. The police chief argued that surveillance cameras are highly effective at deterring crime.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the chief's argument?

options.text: The surveillance cameras are monitored by a team of trained security personnel.
Reported crime in residential neighborhoods just outside the camera zone increased by 25% during the same period, suggesting criminals relocated rather than stopped offending.
The cameras have also helped police identify suspects in several serious crimes.
The city allocated additional funding for lighting improvements in the downtown area.
Public surveys show that residents feel safer knowing cameras are present.

explanation: Premise: Downtown street crime fell 30% after cameras. Conclusion: Cameras deter crime. Gap: Assumes crime actually decreased rather than moved. B shows crime displacement — offenders relocated to areas without cameras, meaning cameras didn't deter crime but shifted it.

passage: A biotech company's study showed that a genetically modified crop variety produces 40% higher yields per acre than the conventional variety under laboratory conditions. The company recommends farmers adopt this GM crop to increase food production significantly.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the company's recommendation?

options.text: The GM crop has been approved for commercial cultivation by regulatory agencies.
Laboratory growing conditions provide optimal temperature, water, and nutrient levels that are rarely achievable on actual farms, where the GM variety has shown yield advantages of only 2-3%.
Some consumers prefer to buy non-GMO food products.
The GM crop is resistant to a common plant disease.
Farmers who adopt GM crops may need to purchase new seeds each growing season.

explanation: Premise: GM crop yields 40% more in labs. Conclusion: Farmers should adopt it for significant gains. Gap: Assumes lab results transfer to real farms. B directly shows the real-world advantage is negligible (2-3%), undermining the promise of significant yield improvement.

passage: A comprehensive meta-analysis of 50 studies involving over 100,000 participants found that people who work remotely report significantly higher job satisfaction than office workers. However, the researchers also noted that remote workers log an average of 7 more hours per week than their office counterparts. Based on the satisfaction findings, a corporate strategy consultant advises her clients that transitioning to remote work will improve employee morale and reduce turnover costs.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the consultant's advice?

options.text: The meta-analysis included studies from various industries, including technology, finance, and healthcare.

Workers who reported the highest satisfaction were also those whose roles had been specifically redesigned to suit remote collaboration, a costly and time-consuming organizational change that most companies in the meta-analysis undertook before the transition.
Job satisfaction is one of the strongest predictors of employee retention.
Some remote workers reported difficulty maintaining work-life boundaries due to the additional hours worked.
Companies that have adopted remote work policies have also seen reductions in office space costs.

explanation: Premise: Remote workers have higher satisfaction (meta-analysis). Conclusion: Transitioning to remote will improve morale and reduce turnover. Gap: Assumes the transition alone produces results. B reveals that the satisfaction gains required extensive role redesign — an intervening variable. Without this costly restructuring, merely allowing remote work may not yield the same morale benefits.

passage: Economists studying developing nations found that countries where microfinance institutions expanded rapidly between 2005 and 2015 showed greater reductions in poverty rates than comparable countries without microfinance growth. Based on this evidence, an international development organization recommends aggressive microfinance expansion as the primary tool for poverty reduction in Sub-Saharan Africa.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the organization's recommendation?

options.text: Microfinance institutions provide small loans to entrepreneurs who lack access to traditional banking.

The countries where microfinance expanded had also received significantly more foreign direct investment and infrastructure aid during the same period, and statistical models cannot reliably disentangle these overlapping effects.
Some microfinance borrowers default on their loans.
Sub-Saharan Africa has a growing population of young entrepreneurs.
Microfinance institutions charge interest rates that are higher than traditional banks.

explanation: Premise: Countries with microfinance growth reduced poverty more. Conclusion: Microfinance should be primary poverty tool in Sub-Saharan Africa. Gap: Assumes microfinance caused the poverty reduction and that results transfer. B shows confounding — foreign investment and infrastructure aid coincided, and their effects can't be separated statistically.

passage: A prestigious medical journal published a 15-year cohort study showing that individuals who consumed a Mediterranean diet had a 40% lower risk of cardiovascular events compared to those following a standard Western diet. The study's lead researcher argues that public health campaigns should promote the Mediterranean diet as the optimal approach for heart disease prevention across all populations.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the researcher's argument?

options.text: The Mediterranean diet emphasizes olive oil, fish, whole grains, and fresh vegetables.

The study participants who followed the Mediterranean diet were predominantly from southern European populations with genetic variants that enhance the metabolism of olive oil's beneficial compounds, and these variants are rare outside Mediterranean-ancestry populations.
Some participants who followed a Western diet did not experience any cardiovascular events.
The study accounted for differences in participants' exercise habits and smoking status.
Several shorter studies have also shown benefits of the Mediterranean diet.

explanation: Premise: Mediterranean diet linked to 40% lower cardiovascular risk. Conclusion: Promote it as optimal for ALL populations. Gap: Assumes benefits generalize universally. B identifies a critical limitation — genetic variants in the study population that enhance olive oil metabolism. Without these variants, other populations may not see the same benefit, undermining the universal recommendation.

passage: A retail chain observed that stores playing classical music had 18% higher sales per customer than stores playing pop music. The chain's regional manager ordered all stores to switch to classical music to boost sales.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the manager's decision?

options.text: Classical music is preferred by many shoppers over the age of 50.
The stores playing classical music were located in affluent neighborhoods where customers have higher disposable incomes.
Playing copyrighted music in stores requires paying licensing fees.
Some customers shop with personal headphones and do not hear the store's music.
The study was conducted over a three-month period.

explanation: Premise: Classical-music stores had higher per-customer sales. Conclusion: Classical music boosts sales. Gap: Assumes music caused higher spending. B shows a confound — affluent locations have wealthier customers who spend more regardless of music.

passage: An environmental group noted that bird populations near wind farms declined by 15% over five years and concluded that wind turbines are a significant threat to bird species in the region.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the group's conclusion?

options.text: Wind turbines generate clean energy that reduces reliance on fossil fuels.
Bird populations across the entire region, including areas far from wind farms, declined by a similar percentage due to habitat loss from urban development.
Some bird species have been observed avoiding wind turbine areas.
The wind farm operators have installed bird-deterrent devices.
The wind farms are located along known bird migration routes.

explanation: Premise: Birds near wind farms declined 15%. Conclusion: Wind turbines are a significant threat. Gap: Assumes wind farms caused the decline. B shows the decline is region-wide from urban development — wind farms aren't uniquely responsible.

passage: An auto insurance company found that drivers who completed its online defensive driving course had 25% fewer accident claims than those who did not. The company concluded that its course makes drivers significantly safer.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the company's conclusion?

options.text: The online course covers topics such as hazard awareness and proper following distances.
Drivers who voluntarily complete defensive driving courses tend to be more cautious and safety-conscious to begin with.
The company offers a discount on premiums for drivers who complete the course.
The accident claims data was collected over a two-year period.
Some states require new drivers to complete a defensive driving course.

explanation: Premise: Course completers had 25% fewer claims. Conclusion: Course makes drivers safer. Gap: Assumes the course caused fewer accidents. B shows self-selection — safety-conscious drivers chose the course and already drive more carefully.

passage: A multinational study found that children who attend bilingual schools outperform monolingual-school peers on tests of cognitive flexibility and problem-solving by age 12. Educational reformers argue that converting all schools to bilingual instruction would enhance students' cognitive development nationwide.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the reformers' argument?

options.text: Bilingual education requires teachers who are fluent in two languages, a relatively scarce qualification.
Parents who choose bilingual schools for their children tend to be more educated and to provide cognitively stimulating home environments that independently enhance cognitive development.
Cognitive flexibility is associated with better academic outcomes in secondary school.
The study measured cognitive flexibility using well-validated neuropsychological tests.
Some monolingual-school students also demonstrated high cognitive flexibility.

explanation: Premise: Bilingual-school kids score higher on cognitive tests. Conclusion: All schools should go bilingual. Gap: Assumes bilingual instruction caused the cognitive advantage. B shows a confounding variable — more educated parents who choose bilingual schools also provide richer cognitive stimulation at home.

passage: A large-scale randomized controlled trial found that patients with chronic lower back pain who received acupuncture twice weekly for six months reported a 45% reduction in pain scores, compared to 40% for those receiving sham acupuncture (needles inserted at non-therapeutic points) and 20% for those receiving no treatment. The acupuncture practitioners' association argues that these results demonstrate that real acupuncture has specific therapeutic effects beyond placebo.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the association's argument?

options.text: The trial used validated pain assessment scales to measure outcomes.

The 5-percentage-point difference between real and sham acupuncture was not statistically significant, whereas the difference between both acupuncture groups and the no-treatment group was highly significant.
Acupuncture has been practiced for over 2,000 years in traditional Chinese medicine.
Some patients in the real acupuncture group reported complete elimination of their pain.
The trial was funded by a government health research agency.

explanation: Premise: Real acupuncture reduced pain 45% vs. sham 40% vs. no treatment 20%. Conclusion: Acupuncture has specific therapeutic effects beyond placebo. Gap: Assumes the difference between real and sham is meaningful. B shows the 5-point real vs. sham difference wasn't statistically significant — meaning the specific needle placement doesn't matter, and the benefit is likely from the ritual/placebo of needle insertion, not the specific acupuncture technique.

passage: A fitness app company reports that users who track their meals on the app lose an average of 10 pounds more over six months than users who only track exercise. The company claims that meal tracking is the key feature driving weight loss.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the company's claim?

options.text: The app's meal tracking feature uses a comprehensive database of over 500,000 foods.
Users who choose to track meals are generally more committed to their weight loss goals and also follow stricter diets independently of the app.
Exercise tracking is the most popular feature of the app.
Weight loss depends on creating a caloric deficit.
The company offers premium and free versions of the app.

explanation: Premise: Meal trackers lose more weight. Conclusion: Meal tracking drives weight loss. Gap: Assumes tracking causes the loss. B shows self-selection — more committed users choose meal tracking and independently follow stricter diets.

passage: Research at a large corporation found that departments with the highest employee engagement scores also had the lowest error rates in their work output. The Chief Operations Officer recommended investing heavily in employee engagement programs to reduce errors across all departments.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the COO's recommendation?

options.text: Employee engagement can be measured through surveys and performance metrics.
Departments with well-designed processes and clear task instructions naturally produce fewer errors, and employees in these well-organized departments tend to feel more engaged as a result.
The company has invested in engagement programs before with positive employee feedback.
Error rates vary widely depending on the complexity of each department's work.
Engaged employees are less likely to leave the company.

explanation: Premise: High engagement correlates with low errors. Conclusion: Engagement programs will reduce errors. Gap: Assumes engagement causes fewer errors. B shows reverse causation — good processes reduce errors AND increase engagement.

passage: A criminal justice study found that regions with longer mandatory minimum sentences for drug offenses have lower rates of drug-related crime than regions with more lenient sentencing. Law-and-order advocates argue that this proves stricter sentencing deters drug crime effectively.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the advocates' argument?

options.text: Mandatory minimum sentences have been criticized by some legal scholars as disproportionate.
Regions with stricter sentencing also allocate significantly more funding to law enforcement and undercover drug operations, which suppress drug activity independently of sentencing policy.
Drug-related crime can include trafficking, possession, and manufacturing.
Some individuals convicted under mandatory minimums are first-time offenders.
The study analyzed data from over 200 jurisdictions.

explanation: Premise: Stricter sentencing correlates with less drug crime. Conclusion: Strict sentencing deters crime. Gap: Assumes sentencing causes the lower crime. B shows a confound — more enforcement funding — that independently suppresses drug activity.

passage: A social media platform found that users who engage with its fact-checking labels on news posts share 30% fewer false stories. The platform argues that its fact-checking system effectively reduces the spread of misinformation.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the platform's argument?

options.text: The platform employs third-party organizations to verify the accuracy of news stories.
Users who pay attention to fact-checking labels are those who were already skeptical of unverified news and shared false stories less frequently before the labels were introduced.
Not all false stories on the platform receive fact-checking labels.
The platform's user base has grown significantly over the past year.
Other social media platforms have also implemented fact-checking features.

explanation: Premise: Users engaging with labels share 30% fewer false stories. Conclusion: Fact-checking reduces misinformation spread. Gap: Assumes labels change behavior. B shows self-selection — skeptical users engage with labels and were already cautious sharers.

passage: An agricultural study spanning 10 years found that farms using integrated pest management (IPM) had higher crop yields and lower pesticide costs than conventional farms. The agriculture ministry recommends that all farms transition to IPM to improve productivity and reduce chemical usage.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the ministry's recommendation?

options.text: IPM combines biological control, habitat manipulation, and targeted pesticide use.
Farms that adopted IPM were primarily large commercial operations with access to agronomists and advanced monitoring technology that smaller farms cannot afford or implement.
Some conventional farms in the study also achieved high yields.
IPM has been promoted by environmental organizations for decades.
The cost of transitioning to IPM varies depending on the crop type.

explanation: Premise: IPM farms outperform conventional farms. Conclusion: All farms should adopt IPM. Gap: Assumes what works for some farms will work for all. B shows that IPM adopters had unique advantages (size, expertise, tech) unavailable to smaller farms, limiting generalizability.

passage: A survey of 1,000 consumers found that 80% of those who shop at farmers' markets report better overall health than those who shop primarily at supermarkets. A public health official concluded that shopping at farmers' markets leads to better health outcomes.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the official's conclusion?

options.text: Farmers' market produce is often fresher than supermarket produce.
People who shop at farmers' markets tend to have higher incomes and more access to healthcare, gym memberships, and personal trainers.
Not all farmers' markets sell exclusively organic products.
Supermarkets offer a wider variety of products than farmers' markets.
The survey relied on self-reported health assessments.

explanation: Premise: Farmers' market shoppers report better health. Conclusion: Farmers' markets lead to better health. Gap: Correlation vs. causation. B shows confounding — wealthier shoppers have better health resources independent of where they shop.

passage: After introducing standing desks for all employees, a tech startup noticed that short-term sick leave decreased by 20%. The CEO concluded that standing desks improve employee health.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the CEO's conclusion?

options.text: Standing desks allow workers to alternate between sitting and standing positions.
The startup also introduced a new comprehensive health insurance plan with free preventive care visits during the same period.
Some employees use the standing desk feature only occasionally.
The startup employs mostly young workers in their twenties and thirties.
Standing for extended periods can cause leg and foot discomfort.

explanation: Premise: Sick leave decreased after standing desks. Conclusion: Standing desks improve health. Gap: Assumes standing desks caused the decrease. B provides an alternative — new health insurance with preventive care likely reduced illness.

passage: A language learning app found that users who practice for at least 30 minutes daily achieve conversational fluency in a new language within six months. The app advertises that its method guarantees fluency in six months.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the basis for the app's advertising claim?

options.text: The app uses spaced repetition and immersive audio exercises.
Users who maintain a 30-minute daily practice routine tend to supplement their learning with language exchange meetups, foreign media consumption, and private tutors.
Some users achieve fluency in less than six months.
The app is available in over 30 languages.
Many users who download the app discontinue use within the first month.

explanation: Premise: Daily app users achieve fluency in 6 months. Conclusion: The app guarantees fluency. Gap: Assumes the app alone causes fluency. B shows that dedicated users also use other learning methods, so the app isn't solely responsible.

passage: A large-scale educational experiment in three developing countries provided free laptops loaded with educational software to primary school students in rural areas. After two years, students who received laptops scored 15% higher on digital literacy tests than control group students. The international education agency that funded the program argues that providing laptops is an effective way to improve educational outcomes in developing countries.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the agency's argument?

options.text: The laptops came preloaded with applications covering math, science, and reading.

While digital literacy scores improved, the laptop recipients showed no improvement — and in some cases slight declines — in math, reading, and science scores compared to the control group, suggesting the devices merely taught students how to use computers rather than improving core academic learning.
Some students shared their laptops with family members.
The agency plans to expand the program to additional countries.
Internet connectivity in the rural areas was limited and unreliable.

explanation: Premise: Laptop recipients scored 15% higher on digital literacy tests. Conclusion: Laptops improve educational outcomes. Gap: Equates digital literacy with broader educational outcomes. B shows that core academic skills (math, reading, science) didn't improve — students merely learned to use computers, which is a much narrower outcome than the agency claims.

passage: Economists found that cities which invested heavily in sports stadiums experienced an average 12% increase in local employment over the following decade. City officials in Greenvale, which has high unemployment, are proposing a $500 million public stadium investment, arguing it will create a significant number of jobs.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the officials' argument?

options.text: Sports stadiums can attract tourism and major events to a city.

Independent analyses show that the employment growth in stadium cities was driven by concurrent tech industry booms in those regions, and cities with similar economic profiles that did not build stadiums experienced comparable employment gains during the same period.
The proposed stadium would host a professional football team.
Some residents oppose spending public funds on stadium construction.
Construction of the stadium itself would provide temporary jobs during the building phase.

explanation: Premise: Stadium cities saw 12% employment growth. Conclusion: Stadium investment will create jobs in Greenvale. Gap: Assumes stadiums caused employment growth. B shows the growth was driven by concurrent tech booms, and cities without stadiums saw the same gains — stadiums were incidental.

passage: A school principal noticed that students who eat in the school cafeteria, which serves balanced meals, have fewer absences than students who bring lunch from home. The principal concluded that the school's balanced meals contribute to better student health and attendance.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the principal's conclusion?

options.text: Students who bring lunch from home often pack high-sugar, low-nutrient foods.
The school cafeteria was recently renovated with a modern kitchen.
Some students who eat in the cafeteria still get sick occasionally.
The school has approximately 800 students.
Attendance rates have been stable over the past five years.

explanation: Premise: Cafeteria eaters have fewer absences. Conclusion: Balanced meals improve health/attendance. The conclusion is strengthened if home lunches are nutritionally inferior (A), supporting the idea that the cafeteria's balanced meals make the difference.

passage: A car manufacturer claims that its new hybrid vehicle reduces fuel costs for drivers. The hybrid model costs $5,000 more than the standard gasoline version but uses 40% less fuel.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the manufacturer's claim?

options.text: Most drivers keep their vehicles for an average of eight years, during which the fuel savings significantly exceed the $5,000 price premium.
The hybrid model has a slightly smaller trunk than the gasoline version.
Some drivers prefer the driving feel of gasoline engines.
The hybrid model requires specialized maintenance that is available at most dealerships.
Gasoline prices have remained relatively stable over the past decade.

explanation: Premise: Hybrid costs $5,000 more but uses 40% less fuel. Conclusion: Reduces fuel costs for drivers. A strengthens by showing that over typical ownership, savings exceed the premium, making the cost claim valid.

passage: A city installed new LED streetlights to replace older sodium vapor lights, predicting that the change would reduce electricity costs. In the first year, the city's streetlighting electricity bill decreased by 50%.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the conclusion that the LED streetlights caused the cost reduction?

options.text: Electricity rates in the city remained unchanged during the year.
LED lights are manufactured by many different companies.
The city also replaced some traffic signals during the year.
Some residents preferred the warmer color of the old sodium lights.
The city purchased the LED lights from a local supplier.

explanation: Premise: Electricity bill fell 50% after LED switch. Conclusion: LEDs caused the reduction. A strengthens by ruling out an alternative explanation — stable electricity rates confirm the savings came from the lights, not rate changes.

passage: A nonprofit organization provides free tutoring to underperforming elementary school students. The organization claims that its tutoring program improves students' math scores.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the organization's claim?

options.text: Students who received tutoring for at least three months improved their math scores by an average of 25%, while a matched comparison group of similar students who did not receive tutoring improved by only 5%.
The tutors are all certified teachers.
The program operates in 15 states.
Some students in the program also improved their reading scores.
The organization has been operating for over ten years.

explanation: Premise: Organization claims tutoring improves math scores. A strengthens by providing a controlled comparison — tutored students improved dramatically more than matched non-tutored peers, supporting the causal claim.

passage: A company implemented a new ergonomic chair design in its offices and found that employee complaints about back pain decreased by 45% over six months. The company concluded that the new chairs reduce back pain.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the company's conclusion?

options.text: No other changes were made to the office environment, work schedules, or employee wellness programs during the six-month period.
The new chairs are available in several different colors.
Ergonomic chairs are generally more expensive than standard office chairs.
Some employees did not notice a difference in comfort.
The company surveyed employees about their pain levels before and after the change.

explanation: Premise: Back pain complaints dropped 45% after new chairs. Conclusion: Chairs reduce pain. A strengthens by ruling out alternative explanations — nothing else changed, isolating the chairs as the likely cause.

passage: A fire department noticed that homes with smoke detectors were 60% less likely to experience a fatality during a fire. The department launched a campaign to install free smoke detectors in homes without them.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the reasoning behind the department's campaign?

options.text: The presence of working smoke detectors gives occupants an average of 3 extra minutes to evacuate during a fire, and most fire fatalities occur because occupants are unaware of the fire in time.
Smoke detectors require regular battery replacement.
Some home fires are caused by cooking accidents.
The campaign costs the department approximately $200,000 per year.
Homes with fire extinguishers also have lower fatality rates.

explanation: Premise: Homes with detectors have 60% fewer fatalities. Conclusion: Install detectors to save lives. A strengthens by providing the causal mechanism — detectors provide early warning, and fatalities result from late detection.

passage: A pharmaceutical company tested a new allergy medication and found that 75% of participants experienced significant symptom relief. However, skeptics argue the relief could be a placebo effect.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the claim that the medication is genuinely effective?

options.text: In the same trial, only 25% of participants who received a placebo reported significant symptom relief.
The medication was developed after ten years of research.
Allergy symptoms can vary significantly from person to person.
The trial included participants from diverse geographic regions.
Some participants in the medication group experienced mild side effects.

explanation: Premise: 75% got relief from medication; skeptics say placebo. Conclusion (to strengthen): Medication is genuinely effective. A directly addresses the skeptics by showing the placebo group had much lower relief (25%), demonstrating the drug outperforms placebo.

passage: A technology company found that after implementing a mentorship program, the promotion rate for junior employees increased from 15% to 30% annually. The company attributes this improvement to the mentorship program.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the company's attribution?

options.text: Junior employees who did not participate in the mentorship program continued to be promoted at the original 15% rate during the same period.
The mentorship program pairs each junior employee with a senior executive.
The company hires highly qualified junior employees from top universities.
Promotion criteria at the company include performance reviews and peer assessments.
The mentorship program was voluntary.

explanation: Premise: Promotion rate doubled after mentorship. Conclusion: Mentorship caused the improvement. A strengthens by showing a control — non-participants stayed at 15%, isolating the mentorship effect.

passage: A state government introduced a tax credit for homeowners who install solar panels. Over the following three years, residential solar panel installations in the state increased by 300%. The government claims the tax credit drove the increase.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the government's claim?

options.text: Neighboring states without similar tax credits saw only a 20% increase in solar installations over the same period, despite similar decreases in solar panel prices.
Solar panel prices have been decreasing globally due to improved manufacturing.
The tax credit covers 30% of installation costs.
Some homeowners installed solar panels for environmental reasons.
Electricity rates in the state have remained relatively stable.

explanation: Premise: Solar installations up 300% after tax credit. Conclusion: Tax credit drove it. A strengthens by ruling out price drops as the primary cause — neighboring states saw only 20% growth despite the same price trends.

passage: Researchers found that a new vaccine reduced the incidence of a tropical disease by 80% in a trial population. Some critics argue that the trial population may not have been representative of the general population at risk.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the researchers' claim that the vaccine is effective for the general at-risk population?

options.text: The trial population was demographically diverse, including participants of various ages, sexes, genetic backgrounds, and health conditions that mirror the characteristics of the general at-risk population.
The vaccine was developed using the latest mRNA technology.
The tropical disease is a leading cause of death in affected regions.
The researchers published their findings in a peer-reviewed journal.
No serious adverse effects were reported during the trial.

explanation: Premise: Vaccine reduced disease 80% in trial. Critics: Trial may not be representative. A strengthens by directly addressing the critics' concern — the trial population closely matched the general at-risk population.

passage: A delivery company switched its fleet from diesel trucks to electric vans and reported that delivery times improved by 10%. The company attributes the improvement to the electric vehicles' better acceleration in urban stop-and-go traffic.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the company's attribution?

options.text: The delivery routes, driver assignments, package volumes, and traffic conditions remained the same before and after the fleet change.
Electric vehicles have fewer moving parts than diesel trucks.
The electric vans have a range of 200 miles per charge.
Diesel fuel costs have been increasing over the past year.
The company plans to expand its electric fleet.

explanation: Premise: Delivery times improved 10% after switching to electric. Conclusion: Electric vehicles' acceleration caused improvement. A strengthens by ruling out alternative explanations — everything else remained constant, isolating the vehicle change.

passage: After a city planted 5,000 trees in a previously treeless commercial district, summer temperatures in the district measured 3 degrees Fahrenheit lower than before. Urban planners attributed the cooling effect to the new trees.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the planners' attribution?

options.text: Regional weather data showed that overall summer temperatures for the metropolitan area were slightly higher than previous years, ruling out broader climate variation as the cause of the district's cooling.
The trees were a mix of deciduous and evergreen species.
The commercial district has many large parking lots.
Some of the newly planted trees did not survive their first year.
The city also planted trees in residential neighborhoods.

explanation: Premise: District cooled 3°F after tree planting. Conclusion: Trees caused cooling. A strengthens by eliminating alternative explanation — the broader region was warmer, so the district's cooling must be local (i.e., the trees).

passage: A clinical trial tested a new therapy for reducing PTSD symptoms in military veterans. After 12 weeks, 65% of participants in the therapy group showed clinically significant improvement, compared to 20% in the control group. Critics note that the therapy requires a strong patient-therapist relationship and suggest the results may reflect general social support rather than the specific therapeutic technique.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the claim that the specific technique, not social support alone, is responsible for the improvement?

options.text: A third group in the trial received equal-duration sessions of supportive counseling without the specific therapeutic technique and showed only 25% improvement, comparable to the control group.
The therapists in the trial were highly experienced professionals.
PTSD is recognized as a serious mental health condition affecting many veterans.
The specific technique involves structured exposure to trauma memories.
Some participants in the therapy group also took medication.

explanation: Premise: Therapy group improved more (65% vs 20%). Critics: Maybe it's just social support. A strengthens by adding a control that isolates the variable — supportive counseling alone (same social contact) only helped 25%, matching the control. So the specific technique, not general support, drove the improvement.

passage: Economists found that cities that invested in high-speed rail connections to other major cities experienced 8% GDP growth over a decade. Critics argue that these cities were already economically strong and would have grown regardless.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the claim that high-speed rail investment contributed to the GDP growth?

options.text: Econometric modeling that controlled for pre-existing economic trends, industry composition, and population growth still found a statistically significant 5% GDP boost attributable to the rail connections.
High-speed rail reduces travel time between cities significantly.
Some cities that did not invest in rail also experienced GDP growth during the decade.
The rail connections were funded through a mix of public and private investment.
Passenger ridership on the new rail lines increased each year.
