explanation: Premise: Rail cities grew 8% GDP. Critics: They were already strong. A strengthens by directly addressing the critique — controlling for pre-existing trends still shows a significant rail effect (5%), supporting the causal claim.

passage: A tech company claims its AI-powered resume screening tool reduces hiring bias because the tool does not consider candidates' names, ages, or photographs. Civil rights groups remain skeptical, arguing that algorithmic systems can encode existing biases in other ways.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the company's claim?

options.text: An independent audit found that the tool's selection rates were statistically equivalent across all demographic groups, and the audit verified that no proxy variables correlated with protected characteristics were used in the algorithm.
The company employs a diverse workforce.
AI systems are increasingly used in HR processes across industries.
The tool evaluates candidates based on skills, experience, and qualifications.
The company offers bias training to all its employees.

explanation: Premise: Tool doesn't consider names/ages/photos. Conclusion: Reduces bias. Skeptics: Bias can be encoded otherwise. A strengthens by directly addressing the skeptics — an independent audit confirmed equal selection rates and no proxy variables.

passage: A pet food company launched a new organic dog food line and saw sales increase by 50% in the first quarter. The company attributes the increase to growing consumer demand for organic pet products.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the company's attribution?

options.text: A recent industry survey showed that 70% of dog owners expressed a willingness to pay more for organic pet food, and no new competing organic pet food brands entered the market during the same period.
The company has been in the pet food industry for over 20 years.
Organic ingredients cost more to source than conventional ingredients.
The company's non-organic dog food sales remained flat during the quarter.
The new organic line is available in three flavors.

explanation: Premise: Organic dog food sales up 50%. Conclusion: Demand for organic drove it. A strengthens by confirming the demand exists (70% willing to pay more) and ruling out competition as the explanation.

passage: A dentist recommends that patients switch to an electric toothbrush to reduce gum disease. She bases this recommendation on research showing that patients who use electric toothbrushes have healthier gums.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the dentist's recommendation?

options.text: A randomized controlled trial assigned patients to electric or manual toothbrushes and found significantly less gum inflammation in the electric toothbrush group after six months.
Electric toothbrushes come in a variety of price ranges.
Good brushing technique is important regardless of toothbrush type.
Gum disease is a common oral health problem.
Some patients prefer the convenience of a manual toothbrush.

explanation: Premise: Electric toothbrush users have healthier gums. Conclusion: Switch to electric to reduce gum disease. A strengthens by providing an RCT — the gold standard — that confirms the causal relationship.

passage: A city's transportation department reports that after converting several major intersections from traffic signals to roundabouts, injury accidents at those intersections fell by 75%. The department recommends expanding the roundabout program.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the department's recommendation?

options.text: National data from over 1,000 signal-to-roundabout conversions confirms an average 72-80% reduction in injury accidents, and the intersections converted in the city had accident rates typical of signalized intersections before conversion.
Roundabouts require vehicles to slow down when entering.
Some drivers initially find roundabouts confusing.
The converted intersections are located in both commercial and residential areas.
The cost of converting an intersection to a roundabout varies depending on the location.

explanation: Premise: Injury accidents fell 75% after roundabout conversion. Conclusion: Expand the program. A strengthens by showing the result is consistent with national data and the converted intersections were typical, supporting the expectation that future conversions will yield similar results.

passage: Researchers developed a machine learning model that predicts which hospital patients are at high risk for readmission within 30 days. When the model's predictions were used to target at-risk patients with additional follow-up care, readmission rates dropped by 20%. Critics argue that the additional follow-up care, not the model's predictions, deserves the credit.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the claim that the model's predictive ability was essential to the outcome?

options.text: When the same follow-up care resources were provided to randomly selected patients rather than model-identified patients, readmission rates decreased by only 3%.
The model uses data from electronic health records to make predictions.
Hospital readmissions are costly for both patients and healthcare systems.
The model was trained on data from over 50,000 patient records.
Some patients identified as high risk by the model were not readmitted.

explanation: Premise: Model-targeted care reduced readmissions 20%. Critics: Care itself deserves credit. A strengthens by showing that giving care randomly (without model targeting) barely helped (3%), proving the model's ability to identify the right patients was essential.

passage: A controversial policy paper argues that universal basic income (UBI) does not reduce workforce participation, citing data from two pilot programs where employment rates remained stable during the program. Critics note that pilot participants knew the program was temporary and therefore had no reason to quit working, making the pilots uninformative about permanent UBI effects.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the policy paper's argument against the critics' objection?

options.text: A natural experiment occurred when a permanent UBI-like program was unexpectedly extended indefinitely in one region; longitudinal analysis showed employment rates remained stable even five years after participants learned the payments would continue permanently.
The pilot programs provided monthly payments equivalent to the local poverty line.
UBI has been debated by economists for several decades.
Some pilot participants used the payments to start small businesses.
The pilots were conducted in both urban and rural communities.

explanation: Premise: Pilots show stable employment with UBI. Critics: Pilots are temporary so people wouldn't quit. A strengthens by providing evidence from a permanent program — when people learned payments would continue indefinitely, employment still didn't drop, directly countering the critics' objection.

passage: A clothing retailer launched an online store and saw total revenue increase by 30% while in-store revenue remained unchanged. The CEO concluded that the online store attracted new customers rather than shifting existing customers from the physical store.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the CEO's conclusion?

options.text: Analysis of customer data showed that 95% of online purchasers had never made a purchase at the physical store.
The online store offers free shipping on orders over $50.
The retailer's physical stores are located in three shopping malls.
Online retail has been growing across the entire clothing industry.
The online store and physical stores sell the same products.

explanation: Premise: Online revenue added, in-store stable. Conclusion: New customers, not shifted ones. A strengthens by directly showing 95% of online buyers were new customers.

passage: A water filtration company claims its new filter removes 99% of lead from tap water. Some consumers doubt the claim because previous filters by other companies failed to meet similar claims.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the company's claim?

options.text: Independent laboratory tests by three different certified testing agencies each confirmed that the filter removes at least 99% of lead from water samples with varying lead concentrations.
The filter uses a proprietary technology developed over five years.
Lead contamination in drinking water has become a significant public health concern.
The company offers a 30-day money-back guarantee.
The filter needs to be replaced every six months.

explanation: Premise: Company claims 99% lead removal. A strengthens by providing independent third-party verification across multiple labs and conditions.

passage: A software company introduced a bug bounty program, offering cash rewards to external researchers who discover security vulnerabilities. Since the program began, the company reports 60% fewer security breaches. The company claims the bounty program has significantly improved its security posture.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the company's claim?

options.text: The company did not make any other changes to its security infrastructure, staffing, or policies during the same period.
Bug bounty programs are common in the technology industry.
The cash rewards range from $500 to $50,000 depending on the severity of the vulnerability.
Some discovered vulnerabilities were rated as critical by security experts.
The company's competitor also experienced a major data breach.

explanation: Premise: Breaches down 60% after bug bounty. Conclusion: Bounty program improved security. A strengthens by ruling out alternative causes — no other security changes were made.

passage: An agricultural county switched from flood irrigation to drip irrigation and saw crop yields increase by 25% while water usage decreased by 40%. The county's water authority concluded that drip irrigation is more efficient than flood irrigation for the county's crops.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the water authority's conclusion?

options.text: The same crop varieties were planted in the same fields with the same fertilizer application rates before and after the switch, and weather conditions were comparable across both periods.
Drip irrigation delivers water directly to plant roots.
The initial installation cost of drip irrigation systems is higher than flood irrigation.
Some farmers in the county still use flood irrigation.
Drip irrigation is widely used in arid regions around the world.

explanation: Premise: Better yields and less water after drip irrigation. Conclusion: Drip is more efficient. A strengthens by ruling out alternative explanations — same crops, fields, fertilizer, and weather.

passage: A new reading program claims to be more effective at teaching first-graders to read than the current standard method. In a pilot study, students in the new program scored 20% higher on reading assessments than students in the standard program.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the claim that the new program is more effective?

options.text: Students were randomly assigned to the new program or the standard program, and both groups had similar demographics, prior reading readiness scores, and classroom resources.
The new program uses phonics-based instruction.
Reading is a foundational skill for all other academic subjects.
The pilot study was conducted in a suburban school district.
Teachers in the new program group expressed enthusiasm about the curriculum.

explanation: Premise: New program students scored 20% higher. Conclusion: New program is more effective. A strengthens by confirming random assignment and equivalent groups, supporting the causal claim.

passage: A ride-hailing company claims that its service reduces drunk driving incidents. In cities where the company operates, drunk driving arrests have decreased by 15%.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the company's claim?

options.text: The decrease in drunk driving arrests occurred primarily during late-night hours on weekends, precisely when the ride-hailing service sees its highest usage, and cities without ride-hailing services saw no such decrease.
The company provides rides in over 200 cities.
Police departments have not changed their enforcement strategies regarding drunk driving.
The company offers discounted rides on major holidays.
Some users of the service do not consume alcohol.

explanation: Premise: Drunk driving arrests down 15% in service cities. Conclusion: Ride-hailing reduced drunk driving. A strengthens by showing the decrease matches the service's peak usage times and doesn't occur in non-service cities.

passage: A research team claims that a newly discovered compound in green tea can slow the progression of Alzheimer's disease. Their in-vitro study showed the compound reduced the formation of amyloid plaques by 60%.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the claim that the compound could be effective in actual patients?

options.text: A subsequent animal study using mice genetically engineered to develop Alzheimer's-like symptoms showed that oral administration of the compound at achievable dietary doses reduced brain amyloid plaque buildup by 50% and improved performance on memory tasks.
The compound is a type of polyphenol found in several plant species.
Alzheimer's disease affects millions of people worldwide.
In-vitro studies provide a controlled environment for testing hypotheses.
Green tea has been consumed for centuries in many cultures.

explanation: Premise: Compound reduced plaques 60% in vitro. Conclusion: Could slow Alzheimer's in patients. A strengthens by demonstrating the effect translates from lab to living organisms — animal studies show both biological and functional improvement at realistic doses.

passage: A nonprofit claims that its microfinance program empowers women in rural areas by providing small business loans. The program reports that 80% of women who received loans started or expanded businesses.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the claim that the program specifically drives this empowerment?

options.text: In comparable villages without the program, only 15% of women started or expanded businesses during the same period, and baseline economic conditions, education levels, and cultural norms were similar across both sets of villages.
The average loan amount is $500.
Many of the women who received loans had no prior business experience.
The program also provides financial literacy training.
Microfinance has been endorsed by several Nobel laureates.

explanation: Premise: 80% of loan recipients started/expanded businesses. Conclusion: Program empowers women. A strengthens by providing a controlled comparison — similar villages without the program had only 15% business activity, isolating the program's impact.

passage: A cleaning product company claims its new disinfectant spray kills 99.9% of common household bacteria. Consumers are skeptical because many cleaning products make similar claims.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the company's claim?

options.text: The disinfectant was tested by an independent laboratory certified by the Environmental Protection Agency, and the lab confirmed 99.9% kill rates across ten common bacterial species under standard household conditions.
The spray has a pleasant citrus scent.
The company has been manufacturing cleaning products for over 50 years.
The spray is available at most major retailers.
The product costs less than comparable disinfectant sprays.

explanation: Premise: Claims 99.9% bacteria killed. A strengthens with independent EPA-certified lab verification across multiple species and real conditions.

passage: A highway patrol reports that after installing rumble strips on the shoulder of a dangerous stretch of highway, single-vehicle run-off-road accidents decreased by 40%. The patrol attributes the decrease to the rumble strips.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the patrol's attribution?

options.text: The speed limit, lane width, and traffic volume on that stretch of highway remained the same, and run-off-road accidents on similar stretches without rumble strips did not decrease during the same period.
Rumble strips produce a loud vibration when a tire crosses them.
Run-off-road accidents are often caused by drowsy or distracted driving.
The rumble strips cost approximately $30,000 to install.
Some drivers find the noise from rumble strips annoying.

explanation: Premise: Run-off-road accidents down 40% after rumble strips. Conclusion: Strips caused decrease. A strengthens by controlling for other factors and showing comparable stretches without strips didn't improve.

passage: Because the company's new ergonomic keyboard has reduced wrist strain complaints by 60% among employees who use it, the company plans to purchase the keyboard for all 500 office employees to eliminate wrist strain problems in the workplace.
questionStem: Which of the following is an assumption on which the company's plan depends?

options.text: The ergonomic keyboard costs less than treating wrist strain injuries.
The employees who have not yet tried the keyboard will also experience reduced wrist strain from using it.
Wrist strain is the most common workplace injury at the company.
All employees spend the same amount of time typing each day.
The keyboard is available in sufficient quantities from the manufacturer.

explanation: Premise: Keyboard reduced wrist complaints 60% in users. Conclusion: Buy for all 500 to eliminate wrist strain. The plan assumes what worked for current users will also work for remaining employees (B). Without this, buying for all is unjustified.

passage: A farmer argues that planting a cover crop during the off-season will improve his soil quality by next spring, since a neighboring farmer who planted cover crops saw significant soil improvement after one season.
questionStem: Which of the following is an assumption on which the farmer's argument relies?

options.text: Cover crops are inexpensive to plant.
The farmer's soil and growing conditions are similar enough to the neighbor's that cover crops would produce comparable results.
Cover crops prevent soil erosion during heavy rainfall.
The farmer has not tried cover crops before.
The neighbor used a specific type of cover crop.

explanation: Premise: Neighbor's cover crop improved soil. Conclusion: Mine will too. Assumption: My conditions are similar enough (B). Without this, the neighbor's success doesn't predict the farmer's outcome.

passage: A city official argues that building a new community swimming pool will reduce childhood obesity rates in the neighborhood, since data shows that children who swim regularly have lower obesity rates.
questionStem: Which of the following is an assumption underlying the official's argument?

options.text: The swimming pool will be accessible and affordable for neighborhood families.
Swimming is more effective at reducing obesity than other forms of exercise.
Childhood obesity is a significant health concern in the neighborhood.
The neighborhood does not currently have any recreational facilities.
Children who swim regularly also tend to eat healthier diets.

explanation: Premise: Swimming children have lower obesity. Conclusion: Build pool to reduce obesity. Assumption: Families will actually be able to access and use the pool (A). If it's inaccessible or unaffordable, building it won't matter.

passage: A marketing firm argues that television advertising is the best way to reach consumers, since a survey found that 85% of adults watch at least one hour of television per day.
questionStem: Which of the following is an assumption on which the firm's argument depends?

options.text: Television advertising is more expensive than online advertising.
Adults who watch television actually pay attention to the advertisements rather than skipping or ignoring them.
Television viewership has remained stable over the past decade.
The survey was conducted across a representative sample of the population.
Online advertising has a higher click-through rate than television advertising.

explanation: Premise: 85% of adults watch 1+ hour daily. Conclusion: TV ads are the best way to reach consumers. Assumption: Viewers pay attention to ads (B). Watching TV doesn't mean watching ads if people skip them.

passage: The city council argues that converting the abandoned rail line into a cycling and walking path will boost property values for homes near the path, since studies in other cities have shown that proximity to recreational paths increases property values by 10-15%.
questionStem: Which of the following is an assumption required by the council's argument?

options.text: The rail line runs through a residential area.

The characteristics of the neighborhoods along this rail line and the local real estate market are sufficiently similar to those in the other cities' studies that a comparable property value increase can be expected.
Converting the rail line will cost less than the expected increase in property tax revenue.
Some residents may oppose the conversion.
The rail line has been abandoned for more than ten years.

explanation: Premise: Other cities saw 10-15% property value increase near paths. Conclusion: This city will see a boost too. Assumption: Conditions here are comparable to the other cities (B).

passage: A software startup argues that hiring remote workers from lower-cost regions will allow it to maintain product quality while significantly reducing payroll expenses, since the startup has found that remote developers produce code of equal quality to its in-house team.
questionStem: Which of the following is an assumption necessary to the startup's argument?

options.text: Remote workers are less expensive to hire than in-house workers in all cases.
The quality of code produced by remote developers in lower-cost regions specifically — not just remote developers in general — will be comparable to that of the current in-house team.
The startup's current in-house team is highly skilled.
Remote work arrangements have become more common in the technology industry.
Communication challenges will not arise with remote workers.

explanation: Premise: Remote developers match in-house quality. Conclusion: Hiring from lower-cost regions will maintain quality at lower cost. Assumption: Remote developers in lower-cost regions specifically will match in-house quality (B). The startup tested remote developers generally, not specifically those from low-cost regions.

passage: A public health campaign argues that banning indoor tanning for minors will reduce the incidence of skin cancer among young adults, since indoor tanning during adolescence significantly increases the risk of developing melanoma later in life.
questionStem: Which of the following is an assumption on which the campaign's argument depends?

options.text: Indoor tanning salons are profitable businesses.
Minors who are prevented from indoor tanning will not substitute equally harmful tanning methods, such as prolonged unprotected sun exposure.
Melanoma is the most dangerous type of skin cancer.
Some adults also use indoor tanning facilities.
The ban would be enforceable in all jurisdictions.

explanation: Premise: Adolescent indoor tanning increases melanoma risk. Conclusion: Banning it for minors will reduce skin cancer. Assumption: Minors won't substitute with equally harmful alternatives (B). If they switch to unprotected sunbathing, the ban won't help.

passage: A company decided to replace its annual employee performance reviews with quarterly feedback sessions, arguing that more frequent feedback will improve employee performance, since research shows employees who receive regular feedback perform better.
questionStem: Which of the following is an assumption required by the company's argument?

options.text: Annual reviews are considered outdated by most management experts.
The quality and usefulness of feedback provided in quarterly sessions will be comparable to the feedback quality shown to improve performance in the research.
Quarterly feedback sessions will cost less to administer than annual reviews.
All managers are capable of providing constructive feedback.
Employee performance is the company's top priority.

explanation: Premise: Regular feedback improves performance (research). Conclusion: Quarterly sessions will improve performance. Assumption: The feedback quality in the company's sessions will match that in the research (B). Frequency alone doesn't help if feedback quality is poor.

passage: An environmental group argues that imposing a carbon tax on industrial emissions will significantly reduce the country's greenhouse gas output, since countries that have implemented carbon taxes have seen their emissions decline.
questionStem: Which of the following is an assumption on which the environmental group's argument depends?

options.text: Carbon taxes generate revenue that can be used for environmental programs.
The country's industrial sector will respond to a carbon tax by reducing emissions rather than simply passing the cost to consumers and maintaining current production levels.
All greenhouse gas emissions come from industrial sources.
The public supports the implementation of a carbon tax.
Greenhouse gas emissions contribute to climate change.

explanation: Premise: Countries with carbon taxes saw emissions decline. Conclusion: A carbon tax here will reduce emissions. Assumption: Industry will reduce emissions rather than absorb costs (B). If companies just pass costs to consumers, output and emissions stay the same.

passage: A city planner argues that building a new light rail line connecting the downtown business district to the southern suburbs will reduce highway congestion along that corridor, since traffic surveys indicate that 40% of southern-corridor commuters would consider using rail transit if it were available.
questionStem: Which of the following is an assumption required by the planner's argument?

options.text: The light rail line will be completed on time and within budget.

A substantial portion of commuters who say they would consider using rail transit will actually switch from driving, and the resulting reduction in vehicle traffic will not be offset by new drivers filling the freed-up highway capacity.
Light rail is more environmentally friendly than automobile travel.
The southern suburbs have a growing population.
Highway congestion costs the city millions of dollars per year in lost productivity.

explanation: Premise: 40% of commuters would consider rail. Conclusion: Rail will reduce congestion. Two assumptions in B: (1) stated interest translates to actual behavior, and (2) reduced traffic doesn't induce new demand. Both must hold for congestion to decrease.

passage: A museum director argues that replacing the museum's traditional audio guides with a smartphone app will enhance visitors' experiences, since surveys at museums that have adopted apps show higher visitor satisfaction scores.
questionStem: Which of the following is an assumption required by the director's argument?

options.text: Smartphone apps are less expensive to maintain than traditional audio guides.
The museum's visitor demographics, including age distribution and technological comfort, are similar enough to those of the surveyed museums that the satisfaction results can be expected to transfer.
Most museum visitors own smartphones.
Some visitors prefer not to use technology during museum visits.
The app will include information about all exhibits in the museum.

explanation: Premise: Museums with apps have higher satisfaction. Conclusion: Replacing audio guides with an app here will enhance experience. Assumption: This museum's visitors are comparable to those surveyed (B). If demographics differ (e.g., older visitors uncomfortable with technology), results may not transfer.

passage: An investment advisor argues that her client should invest in renewable energy stocks because the global transition to renewable energy is accelerating, and companies in this sector will see sustained revenue growth over the next decade.
questionStem: Which of the following is an assumption necessary for the advisor's argument?

options.text: Renewable energy is better for the environment than fossil fuels.
The anticipated revenue growth in the renewable energy sector will translate into stock price appreciation rather than being already reflected in current stock prices.
Some renewable energy companies have experienced losses in recent years.
Government subsidies for renewable energy will continue.
The client's portfolio does not currently include any energy stocks.

explanation: Premise: Renewable energy growth is accelerating. Conclusion: Client should buy these stocks. Assumption: Growth isn't already priced in (B). If the market has already incorporated expected growth into current prices, buying now won't yield above-market returns.

passage: The dean of a law school argues that requiring all students to complete a pro bono legal clinic will produce more ethically minded lawyers, since students who have participated in voluntary pro bono work at the school have been rated as more ethically aware by their professors.
questionStem: Which of the following is an assumption underlying the dean's argument?

options.text: Pro bono legal work benefits the communities that receive free legal services.
Making the clinic mandatory will produce the same ethical development as voluntary participation, even though the students' motivations for participating will differ.
Most law schools do not currently require pro bono work.
Ethical awareness is an important quality for practicing lawyers.
Professors are qualified to assess students' ethical awareness.

explanation: Premise: Voluntary pro bono students are rated more ethically aware. Conclusion: Mandatory pro bono will produce more ethical lawyers. Assumption: Mandatory participation yields the same benefits as voluntary (B). Voluntarily choosing pro bono may reflect pre-existing ethics rather than developing them.

passage: A political strategist argues that a candidate's proposal to implement universal pre-kindergarten education will win support among suburban voters, since polls show that education is ranked as the top priority by 65% of suburban voters. The strategist recommends making universal pre-K the centerpiece of the candidate's suburban outreach campaign.
questionStem: Which of the following is an assumption that the strategist's reasoning requires?

options.text: Universal pre-K education has been successfully implemented in other states.

Suburban voters who prioritize education generally favor expanding government-funded early childhood programs specifically, rather than prioritizing other education issues such as school choice, teacher pay, or reducing class sizes, which might be addressed differently.
The candidate has experience in education policy.
Suburban voters make up a significant portion of the electorate.
The opposing candidate does not have a strong education platform.

explanation: Premise: 65% of suburban voters rank education as top priority. Conclusion: Universal pre-K will win suburban support. Assumption: 'Education priority' means support for expanded government pre-K specifically (B). Voters could prioritize education but prefer very different policies (school choice, etc.).

passage: A hospital system CEO argues that implementing AI-assisted diagnostic tools in emergency departments will reduce misdiagnosis rates and improve patient outcomes, since clinical trials have shown that the AI tools correctly identify conditions that human physicians miss 30% of the time.
questionStem: Which of the following is an assumption required by the CEO's argument?

options.text: AI diagnostic tools have been approved for clinical use.

Emergency department physicians will trust, integrate, and act upon the AI system's recommendations rather than dismissing or overriding them, and the AI system will perform as well in the chaotic, time-pressured ED environment as it did under controlled clinical trial conditions.
Misdiagnosis is a significant problem in emergency departments.
The AI tools can process patient data quickly.
The hospital system has the budget to implement AI diagnostic tools.

explanation: Premise: AI catches conditions humans miss 30% of the time in trials. Conclusion: Implementing AI in EDs will reduce misdiagnosis. Assumption: (1) Doctors will actually use the AI's recommendations, and (2) the AI will work in real ED conditions as well as in trials (B). Both must hold for the conclusion to follow.

passage: A bakery owner plans to increase profits by switching from imported flour to locally sourced flour, which is 20% cheaper. The owner expects the quality of baked goods to remain the same.
questionStem: Which of the following is an assumption on which the owner's plan depends?

options.text: Locally sourced flour is available in sufficient quantities to meet the bakery's needs.
The locally sourced flour produces baked goods of comparable quality to the imported flour.
The bakery's customers prefer locally sourced ingredients.
The bakery currently uses a large volume of imported flour.
Flour is the bakery's most expensive ingredient.

explanation: Premise: Local flour is 20% cheaper. Conclusion: Switch to increase profits while maintaining quality. Assumption: Local flour will produce equal-quality goods (B). If quality drops, customers may leave, offsetting savings.

passage: A school board decided to extend the school day by one hour to improve students' academic performance, reasoning that more instructional time will lead to higher achievement.
questionStem: Which of the following is an assumption underlying the school board's decision?

options.text: Parents support the extended school day.
Students will remain attentive and engaged during the additional hour rather than experiencing fatigue that negates any learning gains.
The school board has the budget to fund the longer school day.
Other school districts have extended their school days.
Teachers will be compensated for the additional hour.

explanation: Premise: More time = more learning. Conclusion: Extend day to boost performance. Assumption: Students will actually benefit from the extra time — that fatigue won't cancel gains (B).

passage: A tech company plans to reduce customer support costs by replacing human agents with an AI chatbot, arguing that the chatbot can handle most customer inquiries at a fraction of the cost.
questionStem: Which of the following is an assumption necessary for the plan to succeed?

options.text: AI chatbots are becoming more common in customer service.
Customers will find the chatbot's service acceptable enough that they do not abandon the company's products due to frustration with automated support.
The chatbot will be available 24 hours a day.
The company receives a high volume of customer inquiries.
Human customer support agents are difficult to recruit.

explanation: Premise: Chatbot handles inquiries cheaply. Conclusion: Switching will reduce costs. Assumption: Customer satisfaction won't drop enough to cause lost revenue exceeding the savings (B).

passage: A nutritionist recommends that her clients eat more fermented foods to improve their gut health, citing studies showing that people who regularly consume fermented foods have more diverse gut microbiomes.
questionStem: Which of the following is an assumption on which the nutritionist's recommendation relies?

options.text: Fermented foods are widely available in most grocery stores.

The diverse microbiomes observed in fermented-food consumers are caused by the consumption of those foods rather than by other dietary or lifestyle factors common among people who choose to eat fermented foods.
A diverse gut microbiome is associated with better overall health.
Fermented foods include yogurt, kimchi, and sauerkraut.
The nutritionist's clients do not currently eat fermented foods.

explanation: Premise: Fermented food eaters have diverse microbiomes. Conclusion: Eat fermented foods to improve gut health. Assumption: Fermented foods CAUSE the diversity (B), not that health-conscious people who eat these foods also do other things that improve gut health.

passage: The CEO of a publishing company argues that shifting entirely to digital textbooks will reduce costs and improve access for students, since digital production eliminates printing, shipping, and warehousing expenses. The CEO plans to discontinue all physical textbook production within two years.
questionStem: Which of the following is an assumption required for the CEO's plan to achieve its stated goals?

options.text: Digital textbooks can be updated more easily than physical ones.

All students in the company's target market have reliable access to the digital devices and internet connectivity needed to use digital textbooks, and the company will not lose significant revenue from schools or districts that require or strongly prefer physical books.
Some students prefer digital formats for studying.
The publishing company currently spends a large portion of its budget on printing.
Digital textbook piracy can be effectively prevented.

explanation: The plan assumes both that students can access digital materials AND that the company won't lose sales from physical-book-preferring customers. Both elements of B must hold for the plan to reduce costs AND improve access.

passage: A gym owner plans to attract more members by adding a juice bar, reasoning that gyms with juice bars in nearby cities have higher membership numbers.
questionStem: Which of the following is an assumption on which the owner's plan depends?

options.text: Juice bars are profitable businesses on their own.
The higher membership at other gyms is at least partly due to the juice bar rather than other factors like location or pricing.
The gym owner has experience running a food service business.
Gym members prefer juice over other beverages.
The gym has sufficient space to add a juice bar.

explanation: Premise: Gyms with juice bars have more members. Conclusion: Adding one will attract members. Assumption: The juice bar is contributing to membership, not just correlated with it (B).

passage: A parent argues that her child should take piano lessons because a study found that children who play piano score higher on spatial reasoning tests.
questionStem: Which of the following is an assumption on which the parent's argument relies?

options.text: Piano lessons are available at an affordable price in the area.
Learning piano actually develops spatial reasoning ability rather than spatially gifted children simply being more likely to choose piano.
Spatial reasoning is important for academic success.
The parent's child has shown an interest in music.
The study included a large number of participants.

explanation: Premise: Piano players score higher on spatial tests. Conclusion: Lessons will improve spatial reasoning. Assumption: Piano causes the ability rather than gifted kids choosing piano (B).

passage: A hospital administrator argues that hiring nurse practitioners (NPs) instead of additional physicians for its walk-in clinic will reduce costs without compromising patient care quality, citing a study showing that NPs achieve patient outcomes comparable to physicians for common conditions.
questionStem: Which of the following is an assumption necessary for the administrator's argument?

options.text: Nurse practitioners earn lower salaries than physicians.

The conditions treated at the walk-in clinic are predominantly the common conditions for which NPs were shown to achieve comparable outcomes, rather than complex conditions requiring physician-level expertise.
Nurse practitioners are currently employed in many healthcare settings.
The hospital has difficulty recruiting physicians.
Patient satisfaction scores are higher for NPs than for physicians.

explanation: Premise: NPs match physicians on common conditions. Conclusion: NPs in walk-in clinic won't compromise quality. Assumption: The clinic mostly handles common conditions where NPs are equivalent (B).

passage: A real estate developer argues that building luxury condominiums on the waterfront will generate substantial tax revenue for the city, since luxury developments in other waterfront cities have yielded high property tax returns.
questionStem: Which of the following is an assumption on which the developer's argument depends?

options.text: Waterfront property is desirable in many markets.
Demand for luxury condominiums in this city is strong enough that the units will sell at premium prices rather than sitting vacant or requiring steep discounts.
The developer has experience building luxury properties.
The city's current tax revenue is insufficient to fund public services.
The waterfront is currently underutilized.

explanation: Premise: Luxury waterfront condos generate high taxes elsewhere. Conclusion: They will here too. Assumption: There's sufficient demand in this market to sustain luxury prices (B). Without demand, units won't sell and tax revenue won't materialize.

passage: A wildlife conservation group argues that reintroducing wolves into a national park will restore the park's ecological balance, since wolves were part of the original ecosystem and their removal led to an overpopulation of deer that damaged vegetation.
questionStem: Which of the following is an assumption underlying the conservation group's argument?

options.text: Wolves are apex predators in many ecosystems.
The park's current ecological conditions, including habitat availability and prey populations, can still support a viable wolf population that will effectively control deer numbers.
The reintroduction of wolves has been successful in other national parks.
Local ranchers may oppose the reintroduction of wolves.
Deer overpopulation has caused significant damage to the park's vegetation.

explanation: Premise: Wolf removal → deer overpopulation → vegetation damage. Conclusion: Reintroduce wolves to restore balance. Assumption: Current conditions can support wolves that will control deer (B). If the park has changed too much, wolves may not thrive or may not impact deer.

passage: An education technology company argues that its adaptive learning platform will close achievement gaps between high- and low-performing students, since the platform personalizes instruction to each student's level and pace. Pilot data shows the platform accelerated learning for students who were behind grade level.
questionStem: Which of the following is an assumption required by the company's argument?

options.text: Adaptive learning technology represents an advancement over traditional instruction.

The achievement gaps are primarily caused by instructional pacing mismatches that the platform addresses, rather than by factors outside the platform's scope such as socioeconomic disadvantage, family instability, or lack of access to technology at home.
Teachers in the pilot program were enthusiastic about the platform.
The platform covers all major academic subjects.
The company has secured funding for further development.

explanation: Premise: Platform personalizes pace; pilot accelerated behind-grade students. Conclusion: Will close achievement gaps. Assumption: Gaps are mainly about pacing, not deeper factors (B). If poverty or home environment drive gaps, personalized pacing alone won't close them.

passage: A financial analyst argues that Company X should acquire Company Y to achieve significant cost synergies, since the two companies have overlapping operations in supply chain management and administration that could be consolidated after the merger.
questionStem: Which of the following is an assumption necessary for the analyst's argument?

options.text: Company Y is currently profitable.

The projected cost savings from consolidating overlapping operations will not be outweighed by the integration costs, cultural clashes, operational disruptions, and potential employee attrition that commonly accompany mergers.
Company X has successfully acquired other companies in the past.
The two companies operate in the same industry.
Shareholders of both companies will approve the acquisition.

explanation: Premise: Overlapping operations can be consolidated. Conclusion: Acquisition will achieve significant cost synergies. Assumption: Net savings will be positive after accounting for all integration costs and risks (B).

passage: A climate policy advisor argues that implementing a cap-and-trade system for carbon emissions will reduce a country's emissions by 30% within a decade, citing the success of the EU Emissions Trading System, which achieved a comparable reduction. The advisor recommends adopting a virtually identical policy framework.
questionStem: Which of the following is an assumption required by the advisor's argument?

options.text: Cap-and-trade systems are considered market-based approaches to reducing emissions.

The country's industrial composition, energy infrastructure, regulatory enforcement capacity, and political stability are sufficiently comparable to the EU's that the same policy design will produce similar emission reductions, and the initial cap and allowance allocation will be set at levels stringent enough to drive meaningful reductions.
The country's current emissions are similar in magnitude to the EU's.
Cap-and-trade is more effective than a carbon tax.
The country has committed to international climate agreements.

explanation: Premise: EU cap-and-trade reduced emissions ~30%. Conclusion: Same policy here will produce same result. Assumption: Conditions here are comparable to the EU AND the policy will be stringently implemented (B). Structural differences could prevent comparable outcomes.

passage: All of Brightown's public parks are maintained by the city's Parks Department. The Parks Department has announced that due to budget cuts, it will reduce maintenance to biweekly instead of weekly for all parks it oversees. Greenfield Park is a public park in Brightown.
questionStem: If the statements above are true, which of the following must also be true?

options.text: Greenfield Park will close due to budget cuts.
Greenfield Park will receive biweekly maintenance instead of weekly maintenance.
Brightown's residents will be dissatisfied with the reduced maintenance.
The Parks Department will eventually restore weekly maintenance.
Greenfield Park is the largest park in Brightown.

explanation: All public parks → maintained by Parks Dept. Parks Dept → biweekly. Greenfield = public park in Brightown. Therefore: Greenfield → biweekly maintenance (B). A is too extreme. C, D, E are unsupported.

passage: Every employee at Sanderson Inc. who completes the advanced leadership program receives a promotion within one year. Maria completed the advanced leadership program at Sanderson Inc. six months ago. No employee at Sanderson Inc. has ever been promoted more than once in a single year.
questionStem: If the statements above are true, which of the following must be true?

options.text: Maria has already received a promotion.
Maria will receive a promotion within the next six months.
Maria is the most qualified employee at Sanderson Inc.
The advanced leadership program guarantees a senior management position.
Maria will receive exactly one promotion this year.

explanation: Every completer → promotion within 1 year. Maria completed 6 months ago → promotion due within 6 more months (B). A isn't necessarily true yet. C, D are unsupported. E goes beyond — she'll get at least one, but 'exactly' can't be confirmed.

passage: FreshCo supermarket sells only produce that has been harvested within the past 48 hours. All produce harvested within the past 48 hours is classified as 'ultra-fresh' by the National Food Quality Board. Today, FreshCo has tomatoes, lettuce, and strawberries available for sale.
questionStem: If the statements above are true, which of the following must be true?

options.text: FreshCo's prices are higher than other supermarkets because of its freshness policy.
The tomatoes, lettuce, and strawberries at FreshCo are classified as ultra-fresh.
FreshCo has the best produce in the region.
All ultra-fresh produce is sold at FreshCo.
FreshCo's produce will remain fresh for at least a week.

explanation: FreshCo sells only ≤48-hour produce. ≤48 hours → ultra-fresh. FreshCo's items → ≤48 hours → ultra-fresh (B). A is unsupported assumption. C is subjective. D reverses the logic. E is unsupported.

passage: All flights departing from Terminal B at Metro Airport are international flights. All international flights at Metro Airport require passengers to clear customs. James has a flight departing from Terminal B at Metro Airport tomorrow.
questionStem: If the statements above are true, which of the following must be true?

options.text: James is traveling to Europe.
James will need to clear customs before his flight.
Terminal B is the only terminal at Metro Airport for international flights.
James has a valid passport.
James's flight will be delayed.

explanation: Terminal B → international. International → customs. James → Terminal B → international → customs (B). A specifies destination without basis. C says 'only terminal' which isn't stated. D and E are unsupported.

passage: Profit margins at Henderson Electronics decreased by 12% last quarter even though total sales revenue increased by 8%. The company did not change the prices of any of its products during the quarter.
questionStem: If the statements above are true, which of the following can be properly inferred?

options.text: Henderson Electronics will be unprofitable next quarter.
Henderson Electronics' costs increased at a rate that more than offset its revenue gains last quarter.
Henderson Electronics sold fewer units last quarter than the previous quarter.
Henderson Electronics' competitors lowered their prices last quarter.
Henderson Electronics should raise its prices to restore profit margins.

explanation: Revenue up 8% but margins down 12%, prices unchanged → costs must have risen faster than revenue to compress margins (B). A is a prediction. C contradicts revenue increase at same prices. D and E are unsupported.

passage: Greenleaf Corporation's employee handbook states that any employee who receives three consecutive 'outstanding' performance ratings becomes eligible for the Executive Development Program. The handbook also states that only employees eligible for the Executive Development Program may apply for director-level positions. Thomas has received two consecutive outstanding ratings.
questionStem: If the statements above are true, which of the following must be true?

options.text: Thomas will receive an outstanding rating next period.
Thomas is not currently eligible to apply for a director-level position.
Thomas is the highest-rated employee at Greenleaf.
Thomas will eventually enter the Executive Development Program.
Thomas has been employed at Greenleaf for at least two years.

explanation: 3 consecutive outstandings → EDP eligible. Only EDP eligible → can apply for director. Thomas has 2, not 3 → not EDP eligible → cannot apply for director (B). A is prediction. C is comparative. D assumes future ratings. E assumes annual reviews.

passage: In Cedarville, all restaurants that have received a health inspection score below 85 are required to post a warning sign visible to customers. No restaurant with a health inspection score of 90 or above has ever been required to take any corrective action. Lucia's Trattoria in Cedarville recently scored 92 on its health inspection.
questionStem: If the statements above are true, which of the following must be true?

options.text: Lucia's Trattoria is the highest-rated restaurant in Cedarville.
Lucia's Trattoria has not been required to take any corrective action.
Lucia's Trattoria has the best food in Cedarville.
All restaurants in Cedarville with scores above 85 are exempt from warning signs.
Lucia's Trattoria will maintain its high score in future inspections.

explanation: Score ≥90 → no corrective action required. Lucia's scored 92 → no corrective action (B). A and C are comparative/subjective. D is about >85, but we only know <85 requires warning and ≥90 needs no action (scores 85-89 are unaddressed). E is prediction.

passage: A market research report states that among consumers aged 18-34, streaming service subscriptions have increased 45% in the past two years while cable television subscriptions have declined 30%. However, among consumers aged 55 and older, cable subscriptions have declined only 5% while streaming subscriptions have increased by just 10%.
questionStem: If the statements above are true, which of the following can be properly inferred?

options.text: Streaming services will eventually replace cable television entirely.
The shift away from cable television is occurring at different rates across age groups.
Cable television companies will go out of business within the next decade.
Consumers aged 55 and older do not use streaming services.
All consumers aged 18-34 prefer streaming over cable television.

explanation: Young consumers shifting fast (45% stream up, 30% cable down) vs. older consumers shifting slowly (10% stream up, 5% cable down) → the shift is happening at different rates by age (B). A and C are extreme predictions. D contradicts 10% increase. E uses 'all' too broadly.

passage: Every new drug approved by the Regulatory Health Authority must undergo at least three phases of clinical trials. Phase III trials require a minimum of 1,000 participants. NovaPharma's drug RX-7 has been approved by the Regulatory Health Authority.
questionStem: Which of the following must be true based on the information above?

options.text: RX-7 is the most effective drug in its category.
At least 1,000 people participated in Phase III trials of RX-7.
NovaPharma conducted more trials than required for RX-7.
RX-7 has no significant side effects.
RX-7 was the first drug approved for its intended condition.

explanation: Approved → at least 3 phases. Phase III → ≥1,000 participants. RX-7 approved → went through Phase III → ≥1,000 participants (B). A is comparative. C says 'more than required' which is unsupported. D and E are unsupported.

passage: All residents of Maple County who earn above $75,000 annually pay a local surtax. No resident who pays the local surtax is eligible for the county's subsidized housing program. Dr. Chen is a Maple County resident who earns $95,000 per year.
questionStem: Based on the information above, which of the following must be true?

options.text: Dr. Chen pays more taxes than most Maple County residents.
Dr. Chen is not eligible for the county's subsidized housing program.
Dr. Chen opposes the local surtax.
Dr. Chen earns more than any other resident of Maple County.
Dr. Chen could become eligible for subsidized housing by reducing work hours.

explanation: Income >$75K → surtax. Surtax → no subsidized housing. Dr. Chen earns $95K → surtax → no subsidized housing (B). A is comparative without data. C is opinion. D is extreme. E assumes a connection not stated.

passage: A company's quarterly earnings report showed that revenue from its cloud computing division grew by 35%, while revenue from its hardware division declined by 15%. The cloud division now represents 60% of total company revenue, up from 50% one year ago. The company's total revenue increased by 10% compared to the same quarter last year.
questionStem: Which of the following can be properly inferred from the information above?

options.text: The company should discontinue its hardware division.
The growth in cloud revenue more than compensated for the decline in hardware revenue in absolute dollar terms.
The hardware division will continue to decline in future quarters.
The company's operating costs decreased during the quarter.
All of the company's revenue growth came from new cloud customers.

explanation: Total revenue up 10%, but hardware down 15% → cloud's growth must have more than offset hardware's decline in absolute terms for the total to be positive (B). A is a recommendation. C is prediction. D is about costs (not discussed). E specifies source of growth without basis.

passage: In a clinical study, researchers found that Drug A reduces blood pressure by an average of 15 mmHg but causes drowsiness in 40% of patients. Drug B reduces blood pressure by an average of 10 mmHg and causes drowsiness in only 5% of patients. Both drugs are equally priced and have no other significant side effects. Current medical guidelines recommend using the drug with the best efficacy-to-side-effect ratio for patients who operate heavy machinery.
questionStem: Based on the information above, which of the following can be properly inferred?

options.text: Drug A is more dangerous than Drug B for all patients.
For patients who operate heavy machinery, Drug B would be recommended under the current guidelines.
Drug A should be taken off the market due to its side effects.
Drug B is more effective at reducing blood pressure than Drug A.
All patients should be prescribed Drug B instead of Drug A.

explanation: Guidelines: best efficacy-to-side-effect ratio for heavy-machinery operators. Drug A: 15/40% drowsiness ratio. Drug B: 10/5% drowsiness. For machinery operators, drowsiness is critical. Drug B has much less drowsiness with reasonable efficacy → better ratio for this group (B). A is overgeneralized. C is extreme. D contradicts (A reduces more). E ignores that A may be better for non-machinery patients.

passage: A technology company's internal memo reveals that software engineer salaries account for 45% of total operating expenses. The CEO has committed to reducing total operating expenses by 20% without any layoffs. The company's office lease, which accounts for 30% of operating expenses, is locked in for three more years at the current rate.
questionStem: If the statements above are true, which of the following must be true?

options.text: The company will reduce software engineer salaries.
Expense categories other than the office lease will need to bear disproportionately large cuts to achieve the 20% reduction.
The company will renegotiate its office lease.
The company will outsource some engineering work.
Total operating expenses will be reduced by exactly 20%.

explanation: Need to cut 20% of total. Office lease (30% of total) is fixed for 3 years → can't be cut. So the remaining 70% of expenses must absorb the entire 20% cut → disproportionately large cuts on non-lease categories (B). A is possible but not necessary. C contradicts the locked lease. D is speculative. E says 'exactly' without basis.

passage: All premium members of TechFit gym receive complimentary personal training sessions. Standard members must pay an additional fee for personal training. Jake is a standard member of TechFit gym and has been attending personal training sessions for the past month.
questionStem: Based on the information above, which of the following must be true?

options.text: Jake has been paying an additional fee for his personal training sessions.
Jake will upgrade to a premium membership soon.
Jake's personal training sessions are more effective than group classes.
Jake attends the gym more frequently than premium members.
Jake was required to pass a fitness test before attending personal training.

explanation: Standard → must pay for training. Jake is standard + attending training → Jake has been paying the additional fee (A). B is prediction. C, D, E are unsupported.

passage: The Martindale Library lends books for a maximum of three weeks. Any book not returned within three weeks incurs a late fee of $0.50 per day. Sarah checked out a book from Martindale Library and returned it 25 days later.
questionStem: Which of the following must be true?

options.text: Sarah enjoys reading books from the library.
Sarah was charged a late fee of $2.00.
Sarah has never returned a book late before.
The library will revoke Sarah's borrowing privileges.
Sarah forgot to return the book on time.

explanation: Max lending: 3 weeks = 21 days. Sarah returned at 25 days = 4 days late. 4 × $0.50 = $2.00 (B). A is subjective. C is unknown. D is not stated. E assumes reason for lateness.

passage: GlobaTech has offices in every country where it generates more than $10 million in annual revenue. GlobaTech does not have an office in Country X. Last year, GlobaTech's total global revenue was $5 billion, generated across 40 countries.
questionStem: Which of the following can be properly inferred from the information above?

options.text: GlobaTech generates no revenue in Country X.
GlobaTech generates $10 million or less in annual revenue in Country X.
Country X has unfavorable business conditions.
GlobaTech's average revenue per country is $125 million.
GlobaTech plans to open an office in Country X next year.

explanation: Revenue > $10M → office. No office in X → revenue ≤ $10M in X (contrapositive) (B). A is too extreme — could generate up to $10M. C is unsupported. D is mathematically correct ($5B/40) but the question asks what must be true about Country X. E is prediction.

passage: A manufacturing company reports that its defect rate for Product A is 2%, while its defect rate for Product B is 5%. Product A accounts for 70% of the company's total production volume, and Product B accounts for the remaining 30%. The company's quality assurance target is to maintain an overall defect rate below 3.5%.
questionStem: Based on the statements above, which of the following must be true?

options.text: The company should discontinue Product B.
The company is currently meeting its quality assurance target.
Product A is more profitable than Product B.
The company produces more units of Product B than Product A.
Product B's defect rate will decrease next quarter.

explanation: Overall defect rate = (0.70 × 2%) + (0.30 × 5%) = 1.4% + 1.5% = 2.9%. Target: below 3.5%. 2.9% < 3.5% → meeting target (B). A is a recommendation. C is about profit (not discussed). D contradicts 70/30 split. E is prediction.

passage: In Riverdale, all commercial buildings must pass a fire safety inspection annually. Buildings that fail the inspection are closed until they pass a re-inspection. The Riverside Mall failed its fire safety inspection last month and has not yet been re-inspected.
questionStem: Which of the following must be true?

options.text: The Riverside Mall is currently closed.
The Riverside Mall is a safety hazard.
The Riverside Mall will pass its re-inspection.
The owner of the Riverside Mall will be fined.
The Riverside Mall has a history of safety violations.

explanation: Fail inspection → closed until re-inspection. Mall failed + not re-inspected → currently closed (A). B is an inference too far. C is prediction. D and E are unsupported.

passage: A pharmaceutical company reports that its research and development spending has increased by 50% over the past five years, while the number of new drugs receiving regulatory approval has decreased from eight to three per year. During the same period, the average time from initial research to approval has increased from 8 years to 12 years.
questionStem: Which of the following is best supported by the information above?

options.text: The pharmaceutical company is becoming less innovative.
Getting new drugs approved is becoming more costly and time-consuming for the company.
The company should reduce its R&D spending.
Regulatory agencies have become more corrupt.
The company's existing drugs are becoming less effective.

explanation: R&D up 50%, approvals down (8→3), time to approval up (8→12 years) → more costly and time-consuming per approved drug (B). A is judgmental. C is a recommendation. D is unsupported. E is unrelated.

passage: An airline's data shows that its fuel costs per flight mile decreased by 10% after it upgraded its fleet to more fuel-efficient aircraft. However, the airline's total fuel expenditure increased by 5% over the same period. During this time, the average ticket price on the airline remained unchanged.
questionStem: Which of the following can be most reasonably inferred from the statements above?

options.text: The airline is less profitable than before the fleet upgrade.
The airline increased its total number of flight miles during the period.
The fleet upgrade was a poor financial decision.
Fuel prices rose during the period in question.
The airline's passengers are flying longer routes.

explanation: Cost per mile down 10% but total fuel cost up 5% → the airline must be flying significantly more total miles to produce higher total costs despite lower per-mile costs (B). A conflates fuel cost with total profit. C is a judgment. D is possible but not the only explanation. E is about route length specifically, not total miles.

passage: Horizon Airlines offers only nonstop flights. All nonstop flights from Midville to Laketown take exactly 2 hours. Emma booked a Horizon Airlines flight from Midville to Laketown departing at 3:00 PM.
questionStem: If the statements above are true, which of the following must be true?

options.text: Emma will arrive in Laketown at 5:00 PM.
Emma prefers nonstop flights.
Horizon Airlines is the only airline offering Midville-to-Laketown flights.
Emma has flown with Horizon Airlines before.
The flight will be delayed.

explanation: Horizon = nonstop only. Nonstop Midville→Laketown = 2 hours. Depart 3 PM → arrive 5 PM (A). B-E are unsupported.

passage: All graduate students in the biology department must complete a thesis to receive their degree. No student who has not received their degree is eligible for the department's alumni mentorship program. Zhang is a graduate student in the biology department who has not yet completed a thesis.
questionStem: Which of the following must be true?

options.text: Zhang is not currently eligible for the alumni mentorship program.
Zhang will not complete a thesis.
Zhang is a poor student.
Zhang has not started working on a thesis.
Zhang is ineligible to receive any academic honors.

explanation: No thesis → no degree. No degree → no alumni program. Zhang has no thesis → no degree → not eligible for alumni program (A). B is prediction. C-E are unsupported.

passage: A survey of 2,000 adults found that 60% use social media daily, and among daily social media users, 75% report experiencing at least occasional anxiety related to social media use. Among adults who do not use social media daily, only 20% report similar anxiety.
questionStem: Which of the following is best supported by the survey results?

options.text: Social media causes anxiety in most adults.
A greater absolute number of daily social media users experience social-media-related anxiety than non-daily users.
Adults should reduce their social media usage to improve mental health.
Social media companies are responsible for user anxiety.
All daily social media users will eventually develop anxiety disorders.

explanation: 60% daily users × 75% anxious = 45% of total. 40% non-daily × 20% anxious = 8% of total. 45% > 8% → more daily users experience anxiety in absolute terms (B). A conflates correlation with causation. C is a recommendation. D assigns blame. E is extreme.

passage: A company's records show that 90% of customers who purchased the premium warranty also purchased the product installation service. However, only 30% of all customers purchased the premium warranty. The installation service is available to all customers regardless of warranty purchase.
questionStem: Which of the following can be properly inferred?

options.text: The installation service is only useful for customers with the premium warranty.
Most customers who purchased the installation service also purchased the premium warranty.
The premium warranty is overpriced.
The company should bundle the warranty and installation service together.
Customers who purchase the premium warranty tend to also purchase the installation service.

explanation: 90% of premium warranty buyers also buy installation → strong tendency for warranty buyers to also buy installation (E). B cannot be determined — we don't know total installation buyers. A, C, D are unsupported opinions.

passage: In Country Z, any corporation with annual profits exceeding $50 million must allocate at least 2% of profits to community development programs. Corporations that allocate more than 5% of profits to such programs receive a tax credit equal to 1% of their total profits. MegaIndustries, a corporation in Country Z, allocated exactly 6% of its profits to community development last year, and its annual profits were $80 million.
questionStem: Which of the following must be true?

options.text: MegaIndustries allocated $4.8 million to community development and received a tax credit of $800,000.
MegaIndustries is the most generous corporation in Country Z.
MegaIndustries allocated more than required because it wanted to improve its public image.
MegaIndustries' tax credit exceeded its community development spending.
All corporations in Country Z allocate at least 6% to community development.

explanation: Profits $80M. 6% to community = $4.8M. Profits > $50M → must allocate ≥2% (met). Allocated >5% → tax credit of 1% of profits = $800K. So $4.8M spent and $800K credit (A). B is comparative. C assumes motive. D is false ($800K < $4.8M). E overgeneralizes.

passage: NovaTech's employee manual states that all software engineers must be proficient in at least two programming languages. Alex was just hired as a software engineer at NovaTech.
questionStem: Which of the following must be true?

options.text: Alex is proficient in at least two programming languages.
Alex has a computer science degree.
Alex is NovaTech's newest employee.
Alex will receive training in two programming languages.
Alex has more experience than other applicants.

explanation: All NovaTech software engineers → proficient in ≥2 languages. Alex = NovaTech software engineer → proficient in ≥2 languages (A). B-E unsupported.

passage: A county's recycling report shows that neighborhoods with curbside recycling pickup recycle an average of 45% of their waste, while neighborhoods with drop-off centers only recycle 15% of their waste. Currently, 60% of the county's neighborhoods have curbside pickup and 40% have only drop-off centers. The county plans to convert all drop-off-only neighborhoods to curbside pickup within two years.
questionStem: Based on the statements above, which of the following is most strongly supported?

options.text: If the conversion plan succeeds and recycling rates remain consistent, the county's overall recycling rate will increase.
The county will eliminate all drop-off centers.
Residents prefer curbside pickup over drop-off centers.
The county's waste output will decrease after the conversion.
The conversion will cost less than building new drop-off centers.

explanation: Current: 60%×45% + 40%×15% = 27%+6% = 33%. After: 100%×45% = 45%. 45% > 33% → overall recycling increases (A). B assumes elimination rather than addition. C is about preference. D conflates recycling rate with waste volume. E is about cost.

passage: A research university reports that its faculty members who secured external grants published an average of 8 papers per year, while faculty without grants published an average of 3 papers. Faculty with grants constitute 40% of the total faculty. The university has 500 faculty members in total. The university's strategic plan targets increasing total annual publications by 25%.
questionStem: Which of the following is best supported by the information above?

options.text: The university currently produces approximately 2,500 papers per year, and achieving the target requires approximately 625 additional publications.
Faculty without grants are less productive than those with grants.
The university should require all faculty to apply for grants.
Grant-funded research is of higher quality than non-grant-funded research.
The university will achieve its publication target within one year.

explanation: Current: (200×8)+(300×3) = 1600+900 = 2500. 25% increase = 625 more needed. Total target = 3125 (A). B equates publications with productivity. C is a recommendation. D is about quality. E is prediction.

passage: A city council is debating whether to convert a downtown parking lot into a public park. Proponents argue that the park will increase property values in the surrounding area. Opponents contend that eliminating parking will drive customers away from downtown businesses.
questionStem: Which of the following would be most useful to evaluate in order to assess the likely economic impact of the proposed conversion?

options.text: Whether the city has other parks in different neighborhoods.
Whether nearby parking garages and street parking have sufficient unused capacity to absorb the vehicles currently using the lot.
Whether the mayor supports the park conversion.
Whether parks in general are popular with city residents.
Whether the parking lot is currently profitable for the city.

explanation: The debate hinges on whether removing the parking lot will actually hurt businesses. B is the key question — if other parking can absorb demand, the opponents' concern is moot. If not, businesses may suffer. A, C, D don't address the tradeoff. E is about current revenue, not impact.

passage: A company is considering implementing a four-day workweek to improve employee satisfaction. Managers worry that reducing work hours will decrease output.
questionStem: Which of the following would it be most useful to determine in order to evaluate whether the four-day workweek will achieve its goal without reducing output?

options.text: Whether employees at the company currently work overtime on weekdays.
Whether employees can maintain or increase their hourly productivity enough during four days to match their five-day output.
Whether other companies in the same industry have tried four-day workweeks.
Whether the company's CEO supports work-life balance initiatives.
Whether employees would prefer a four-day workweek to a salary increase.

explanation: The key question: Can productivity per hour increase enough to compensate for lost hours? B directly tests whether the goal (satisfaction) can be achieved without the cost (output loss). A is relevant but secondary. C is about precedent. D and E are tangential.

passage: A hospital administrator proposes that requiring doctors to use a checklist before every surgery will reduce surgical errors. A senior surgeon objects, arguing that experienced surgeons do not need checklists and that the requirement will slow down the surgical process.
questionStem: Which of the following would be most useful to evaluate in order to resolve the disagreement?

options.text: Whether the checklists are used at other hospitals.
Whether data shows that surgical error rates differ significantly between experienced surgeons who use checklists and those who do not.
Whether patients prefer surgeons who use checklists.
Whether the hospital's surgical error rate is currently above the national average.
Whether the checklist takes more than five minutes to complete.

explanation: The disagreement is whether experienced surgeons benefit from checklists. B directly provides the relevant comparison — if experienced surgeons WITH checklists still have fewer errors, the surgeon's objection is weak. A is about adoption. C is about preference. D is about current rates but not the checklist effect. E is about time.

passage: A school superintendent proposes eliminating homework for elementary school students, arguing that homework adds stress without improving academic outcomes. Teachers argue that homework reinforces classroom learning and builds study habits.
questionStem: Which of the following would be most useful to determine in order to evaluate the superintendent's proposal?

options.text: Whether parents help their children with homework.
Whether controlled studies show a measurable difference in academic achievement and skill development between elementary students who do homework and those who do not.
Whether homework is assigned in other countries' elementary schools.
Whether the superintendent has children in the school district.
Whether students enjoy doing homework.

explanation: The disagreement is about whether homework benefits elementary students. B directly provides empirical evidence to resolve this — if studies show no benefit, the superintendent's proposal is supported; if they show benefit, the teachers' argument is supported.

passage: A pharmaceutical company has developed a new drug that is 20% more effective at treating migraines than the current leading medication. However, the new drug costs three times as much as the existing medication. The company argues that insurance companies should cover the new drug.
questionStem: Which of the following would be most useful in evaluating whether insurance companies should cover the new drug?

options.text: Whether the pharmaceutical company spent significant resources developing the drug.
Whether the 20% improvement in effectiveness translates into a meaningful reduction in migraine frequency and severity that justifies the threefold cost increase from a cost-benefit perspective.
Whether the company has other drugs on the market.
Whether the current medication has any side effects.
Whether migraines are a common condition.

explanation: The question is whether the improvement justifies the cost. B directly evaluates the cost-benefit tradeoff — does 20% more effective mean meaningfully fewer/milder migraines? If the improvement is marginal in practical terms, the cost increase isn't justified.

passage: A retail company plans to replace its experienced full-time cashiers with self-checkout kiosks to reduce labor costs. The head of customer service warns that customer satisfaction may decline if human interaction is removed.
questionStem: Which of the following would be most useful to evaluate in order to decide whether to proceed with the plan?

options.text: Whether self-checkout kiosks are used by competitors.

Whether pilot data from stores that have partially implemented self-checkout shows how customer satisfaction and net cost savings are affected after accounting for kiosk maintenance, theft rates, and customer assistance needs.
Whether the company's current labor costs are above industry average.
Whether the kiosks are manufactured domestically.
Whether the company plans to offer severance packages to displaced cashiers.

explanation: The decision requires knowing both the real cost savings and the satisfaction impact. B provides pilot data addressing both — actual savings (after hidden costs) and actual satisfaction impact.

passage: A government report shows that states with stricter gun control laws have lower rates of gun violence. Gun-control advocates cite this as evidence that strict laws reduce gun violence. Gun-rights groups counter that states with stricter laws already had lower violence rates before the laws were enacted.
