questionStem: Which of the following would be most useful to evaluate in order to determine whether strict gun laws reduce gun violence?

options.text: Whether gun ownership rates differ between states with strict and lenient laws.
Whether the rates of gun violence in states that enacted stricter laws decreased more after enactment than the rates in comparable states that did not change their laws during the same period.
Whether strict gun laws are popular with the general public.
Whether gun violence affects property values in affected neighborhoods.
Whether other forms of violent crime also differ between states with strict and lenient gun laws.

explanation: The debate is causation vs. pre-existing differences. B provides a difference-in-differences analysis — comparing change before/after in states that changed laws versus those that didn't. This controls for pre-existing levels.

passage: A university is considering whether to make standardized test scores optional for admissions. Proponents argue that test scores are biased against underrepresented students. Opponents argue that without test scores, the university cannot fairly compare applicants from different schools with varying grading standards.
questionStem: Which of the following would be most useful to evaluate in order to make this decision?

options.text: Whether other universities have adopted test-optional policies.

Whether universities that have gone test-optional saw changes in both the diversity of admitted classes and the academic performance of enrolled students, compared to their own prior cohorts and to similar universities that maintained testing requirements.
Whether standardized tests are expensive for students to take.
Whether high school grades are a better predictor of college success than test scores.
Whether applicants prefer test-optional policies.

explanation: The decision balances diversity (proponents) vs. predictive quality (opponents). B provides empirical evidence on both dimensions from institutions that have already tried it, including controls.

passage: A factory manager wants to determine whether switching to automated assembly lines will be beneficial. Currently, the factory uses manual labor. Proponents say automation will increase output; skeptics worry it will increase defects.
questionStem: Which of the following would be most useful to determine in order to evaluate whether the switch to automation is beneficial?

options.text: Whether automated factories have been featured in industry magazines.
Whether comparable factories that switched to automation saw increases in output that outweighed any changes in defect rates and the costs of implementation.
Whether the factory's current workers are experienced.
Whether automation technology has improved in recent years.
Whether the factory produces a single product or multiple products.

explanation: The debate is about net benefit (output vs. defects vs. cost). B directly provides evidence from comparable factories covering all three dimensions.

passage: A nonprofit proposes a job training program for unemployed young adults in the inner city. Supporters say it will reduce unemployment; critics say participants will leave for better opportunities outside the community, providing no local benefit.
questionStem: Which of the following would be most useful to evaluate in order to determine whether the program will benefit the local community?

options.text: Whether the program has received media coverage.
Whether data from similar programs elsewhere shows what percentage of trained participants found and retained employment within their local community versus relocating.
Whether the program's instructors have relevant industry experience.
Whether unemployment is higher in the inner city than in other parts of the metropolitan area.
Whether the program offers training in technology or manual skills.

explanation: The debate is about local benefit — will trained people stay? B provides the most relevant evidence by showing outcomes from comparable programs.

passage: A large office building installed motion-sensor lights to reduce energy costs. However, in the first year after installation, the building's electricity bill actually increased by 8%.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, best explains the discrepancy described above?

options.text: Motion-sensor lights are commonly used in commercial buildings.
During the same year, the building added a new server room and data center that operates 24 hours a day.
The motion-sensor lights were installed on all floors.
Some employees initially found the motion-sensor lights distracting.
The building's lease includes a fixed utility rate.

explanation: Expected: motion sensors → lower electricity. Actual: bill increased. B explains it — a new 24/7 data center's massive energy draw more than offset the lighting savings.

passage: A gym lowered its monthly membership fee by 30% to attract more members. Although membership numbers increased significantly, the gym's total monthly revenue from memberships actually decreased.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, best explains the apparent discrepancy?

options.text: The gym invested in new equipment after lowering fees.
The increase in new members was not large enough to offset the 30% price reduction applied to all existing members' fees.
The gym is located in a competitive market with many fitness options.
New members typically visit the gym more frequently than long-term members.
The gym also offers personal training services at an additional cost.

explanation: Expected: more members → more revenue. Actual: revenue fell. B explains: the price cut applied to ALL members (existing + new), and the new member gains didn't compensate for the lost revenue from reduced fees on the larger existing base.

passage: A city doubled its recycling bin distribution, placing bins on every block. Surprisingly, the total amount of material collected for recycling decreased over the following year.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, best explains the decrease in recycled material?

options.text: Recycling is considered environmentally important by most city residents.
The additional bins were labeled with confusing sorting instructions, causing residents to put recyclables in the trash instead for fear of contaminating the recycling stream.
The city did not change its recycling collection schedule.
Some of the new bins were placed in areas with low foot traffic.
The city also launched a campaign encouraging waste reduction.

explanation: Expected: more bins → more recycling. Actual: less recycling. B explains: confusing labels made people avoid recycling bins and throw recyclables in the trash.

passage: A hospital introduced a new rapid diagnostic test that identifies bacterial infections in two hours instead of the previous 48-hour culture method. Despite the faster diagnosis, the average length of patient hospital stays for bacterial infections increased.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, best explains the discrepancy?

options.text: The new diagnostic test is more expensive than the culture method.

The faster test detected many mild infections that previously went undiagnosed and were resolved without treatment, causing doctors to treat these cases with hospital-administered antibiotics as a precaution.
Some bacterial infections are resistant to common antibiotics.
The hospital also treated more patients during the same period.
Nurses received training on the new diagnostic equipment.

explanation: Expected: faster diagnosis → shorter stays. Actual: longer stays. B explains: the better test found previously undetected mild cases, adding patients who wouldn't have been hospitalized before, increasing average stay length.

passage: A coffee company increased the size of its standard cup by 20% while keeping prices the same, expecting to attract more customers. Instead, the company's profit margins decreased significantly while customer numbers remained unchanged.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, best explains the discrepancy?

options.text: The coffee company uses premium coffee beans from South America.
The larger cups required 20% more coffee per serving, increasing ingredient costs proportionally, but the unchanged prices meant the additional cost came entirely out of the profit margin.
The company has several competitors in the area.
Customer surveys showed satisfaction with the larger cup size.
The company also sells pastries and sandwiches.

explanation: Expected: bigger cups → more customers → more revenue. Actual: same customers + lower margins. B explains: 20% more coffee per cup = 20% higher costs per serving with no price increase, directly cutting margins.

passage: A country that significantly increased funding for its public education system saw no improvement in average student test scores over a five-year period.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, best explains why increased funding did not improve test scores?

options.text: The country's education minister is a former school principal.
The additional funding was primarily allocated to administrative salaries and facility construction rather than to classroom instruction, teacher training, or educational materials.
Student enrollment increased during the five-year period.
Some schools in the country perform well above the national average.
The country uses standardized tests to measure student achievement.

explanation: Expected: more funding → better scores. Actual: no improvement. B explains: money went to administration and buildings, not to factors that directly improve teaching and learning.

passage: A car rental company replaced its entire fleet with fuel-efficient hybrid vehicles. Customers reported that their fuel costs while renting were actually higher than with the previous conventional fleet.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, best explains the customers' experience?

options.text: Hybrid vehicles are generally quieter than conventional vehicles.
The hybrid vehicles required premium gasoline, which costs 40% more per gallon than regular gasoline, more than offsetting the hybrids' 25% better fuel economy.
The company's rental prices remained the same after the fleet change.
Hybrid vehicles have higher resale values than conventional vehicles.
The previous fleet was less than three years old.

explanation: Expected: hybrids → lower fuel costs. Actual: higher costs. B explains: premium fuel requirement (+40% cost) outweighed fuel efficiency gain (25% better economy).

passage: A tech company offered a 50% salary increase to employees who relocated from its suburban campus to its new urban headquarters. Despite the generous raise, 70% of eligible employees declined to relocate.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, best explains why most employees declined the relocation despite the salary increase?

options.text: The new urban headquarters has a modern open floor plan.

Housing costs near the urban headquarters are more than double those near the suburban campus, and most employees calculated that the 50% salary increase would not cover the increased housing, commuting, and cost-of-living expenses.
The company provides free shuttle service to the suburban campus.
Some employees had concerns about the urban area's school quality.
The company set a deadline of three months for employees to decide.

explanation: Expected: 50% raise → most relocate. Actual: 70% declined. B explains: the real cost of living in the urban area exceeded the raise, making the move a net financial loss for most employees.

passage: A government mandated that all gasoline sold in the country contain 10% ethanol to reduce greenhouse gas emissions. Five years after implementation, the country's total greenhouse gas emissions from the transportation sector increased.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, best explains the increase in emissions despite the ethanol mandate?

options.text: Ethanol is produced from renewable crops such as corn and sugarcane.

The production of ethanol at scale required massive deforestation for farmland and energy-intensive industrial processing that generated more greenhouse gases than the ethanol saved when burned as fuel.
The price of gasoline decreased slightly after the mandate was implemented.
Some car manufacturers opposed the ethanol mandate.
The country also invested in public transportation during the same period.

explanation: Expected: ethanol blend → lower emissions. Actual: emissions increased. B explains: lifecycle emissions from ethanol production (deforestation + industrial processing) exceeded the combustion savings.

passage: A bookstore added a popular coffee shop inside its premises to increase foot traffic and book sales. Foot traffic did increase by 40%, but book sales actually declined by 10%.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, best explains the discrepancy?

options.text: The coffee shop sells a variety of specialty drinks.

Most new visitors came specifically for the coffee and spent their time in the café area without browsing or purchasing books, and the coffee shop's noise and crowding deterred the bookstore's traditional customers.
The bookstore expanded its operating hours after adding the coffee shop.
Online book sales have been growing in the wider market.
The bookstore did not change its book inventory.

explanation: Expected: more foot traffic → more book sales. Actual: sales declined. B explains: new visitors came for coffee only, and the café environment drove away actual book buyers.

passage: A company introduced generous parental leave policies expecting to improve employee retention. However, turnover rates among employees who had recently become parents increased in the year following the policy change.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, best explains the unexpected increase in turnover?

options.text: The parental leave policy provides up to six months of paid leave.

The extended time away from work gave new parents an opportunity to reassess their priorities, and many discovered they preferred to stay home with their children or pursue more family-friendly career options.
The company's competitors also offer parental leave.
Some employees without children expressed frustration about the policy.
The company's overall turnover rate decreased during the same period.

explanation: Expected: better parental leave → parents stay. Actual: parent turnover increased. B explains: the leave itself gave parents time to reconsider, and many chose to leave the workforce or find different jobs.

passage: A country enacted a law requiring all food products to display calorie counts prominently on their packaging. However, a nationwide study conducted two years after the law took effect found that average daily caloric intake among citizens had actually increased.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, best explains the increase in caloric intake despite the labeling law?

options.text: The calorie labels were displayed in a standardized format across all products.

Food manufacturers, anticipating that consumers would choose lower-calorie options, reformulated products to reduce calories per serving but simultaneously reduced serving sizes, leading consumers to eat more servings per sitting and ultimately consume more total calories.
Some consumers reported ignoring the calorie labels when shopping.
The law did not apply to restaurant meals.
Calorie awareness campaigns accompanied the labeling law.

explanation: Expected: calorie labels → consumers eat less. Actual: intake increased. B explains the paradox: smaller servings with lower per-serving calories led people to eat more servings, increasing total intake.

passage: Anyone who exercises regularly is healthy. John is healthy. Therefore, John must exercise regularly.
questionStem: The reasoning in the argument above is flawed because it:

options.text: Fails to consider that John might have other health conditions.
Confuses a sufficient condition with a necessary condition — exercising regularly guarantees health, but health can result from other factors as well.
Assumes that exercise is the only way to stay healthy.
Relies on John's personal testimony about his health.
Ignores the fact that some people exercise irregularly.

explanation: Regular exercise → healthy (sufficient). But healthy doesn't guarantee regular exercise — other things can produce health. The argument reverses the conditional, treating a sufficient condition as if it were the only cause.

passage: A politician argues: 'My opponent has no experience in business, so she cannot possibly understand economic policy.'
questionStem: Which of the following best describes the flaw in the politician's reasoning?

options.text: It assumes that the opponent will not gain business experience in the future.
It assumes that business experience is the only way to understand economic policy, ignoring other routes such as academic study, policy research, or governmental experience.
It attacks the opponent's character rather than her policy positions.
It relies on an unverified claim about the opponent's background.
It fails to provide evidence that the politician has business experience.

explanation: The flaw is a false dichotomy / overlooking alternatives. Business experience is treated as the ONLY path to understanding economic policy, but academic training, research, or government work could also provide understanding.

passage: Since the introduction of energy drinks ten years ago, rates of anxiety among teenagers have increased. Therefore, energy drinks are causing increased anxiety in teenagers.
questionStem: Which of the following best describes the flaw in the reasoning above?

options.text: It ignores the possibility that some teenagers do not drink energy drinks.
It assumes a causal relationship based solely on a temporal correlation — two trends occurring simultaneously does not establish that one caused the other.
It does not define what qualifies as an energy drink.
It fails to consider that anxiety can be treated with medication.
It assumes all teenagers drink the same amount of energy drinks.

explanation: Classic post hoc ergo propter hoc fallacy. Two simultaneous trends (energy drinks + teen anxiety) don't prove causation. Many other factors (social media, academic pressure) could explain anxiety increases.

passage: A company executive argues: 'We should not invest in solar energy. The last company in our industry that invested heavily in solar energy went bankrupt within three years.'
questionStem: Which of the following best describes the flaw in the executive's reasoning?

options.text: It does not consider the environmental benefits of solar energy.
It generalizes from a single case — one company's failure does not prove that all solar investments will fail, as the bankruptcy may have been due to factors unrelated to the solar investment.
It assumes that solar technology has not improved since the other company's investment.
It fails to account for government incentives for solar energy.
It ignores the long-term cost savings of solar energy.

explanation: Hasty generalization from one example. One company's bankruptcy doesn't prove solar investment is bad — the failure could have been due to management, market conditions, or other factors entirely unrelated to solar.

passage: A nutritionist argues: 'Country X has the highest per capita consumption of olive oil and one of the lowest rates of heart disease. Therefore, if people in Country Y increase their olive oil consumption, they will reduce their rates of heart disease.'
questionStem: Which of the following best identifies the flaw in the nutritionist's reasoning?

options.text: It fails to note that olive oil is expensive in many countries.

It assumes that olive oil is the cause of Country X's low heart disease rate, ignoring other factors such as overall diet, lifestyle, genetics, and healthcare, and assumes Country Y's population would respond identically.
It does not specify how much olive oil people in Country Y should consume.
It overlooks the possibility that Country X's low heart disease rate has been increasing.
It ignores that some people are allergic to olive oil.

explanation: Two flaws: (1) Assumes olive oil causes low heart disease rather than considering confounding factors. (2) Assumes what works in one population transfers to another with potentially different genetics, diets, and lifestyles.

passage: A manager argues: 'Employee surveys show that 80% of our workers are satisfied with their jobs. Therefore, we do not need to make any changes to our workplace policies.'
questionStem: Which of the following best describes the flaw in the manager's reasoning?

options.text: It does not specify which workplace policies are being discussed.
It ignores the 20% who are dissatisfied, whose concerns could still indicate significant problems, and assumes that current satisfaction precludes the possibility of improvement.
It relies on potentially biased survey data.
It does not compare satisfaction rates with other companies.
It assumes that workplace policies are the only factor affecting job satisfaction.

explanation: Two flaws: (1) Dismisses 20% dissatisfied — this could represent hundreds of employees with valid concerns. (2) Assumes no need for improvement just because a majority is satisfied — policies could still be improved.

passage: Editorial: The government's new regulation requiring restaurants to list allergen information on menus is unnecessary. Customers with allergies can simply ask their server about ingredients. Servers are trained in food safety and can provide accurate information.
questionStem: Which of the following best describes the flaw in the editorial's reasoning?

options.text: It ignores that some restaurants have menus without descriptions.
It assumes servers will always have complete and accurate knowledge of every ingredient in every dish and that customers will always feel comfortable asking, neither of which is reliably the case.
It does not consider that some allergies are more severe than others.
It fails to mention the cost of menu printing.
It overlooks the fact that some customers dine alone.

explanation: The argument relies on two unreliable assumptions: servers always know all ingredients (they may not), and customers will always ask (some may feel embarrassed or forget). The regulation provides a reliable backup.

passage: A social commentator argues: 'Studies show that married people live longer than unmarried people. Therefore, getting married will help you live longer.'
questionStem: Which of the following best identifies the flaw in the commentator's reasoning?

options.text: It does not distinguish between happy and unhappy marriages.

It confuses correlation with causation and fails to consider that healthier, wealthier, or more socially connected people may be more likely both to marry and to live longer, without marriage itself being the cause of longevity.
It does not specify what age people should get married.
It assumes that all marriages are equally beneficial.
It ignores the stress that marriage can sometimes cause.

explanation: Classic correlation-causation confusion. Marriage is associated with longer life, but the causal mechanism is unclear — healthier people may simply be more likely to marry. Selection effects, not marriage itself, may explain the correlation.

passage: A school board member argues: 'Last year we cut the arts budget by 20%, and graduation rates didn't change. This year we should cut the arts budget by another 20%, since clearly the arts have no impact on student outcomes.'
questionStem: Which of the following best identifies the flaw in the board member's reasoning?

options.text: It does not consider students' opinions about arts education.

It assumes that because one year's cut didn't affect graduation rates, further cuts won't either, and it uses a single narrow metric (graduation rates) to measure the broad impact of arts education on student outcomes.
It ignores the potential long-term effects of budget cuts.
It does not compare the school's arts budget with those of other districts.
It assumes that all students participate in arts programs.

explanation: Two flaws: (1) Extrapolation — one cut with no measured effect doesn't mean further cuts are harmless (cumulative effects, threshold effects). (2) Narrow metric — graduation rate doesn't capture creativity, engagement, or other outcomes arts education affects.

passage: An economist argues: 'Countries with higher levels of immigration tend to have stronger economies. Therefore, increasing immigration will strengthen our country's economy.'
questionStem: Which of the following best describes the flaw in the economist's reasoning?

options.text: It fails to consider the social costs of immigration.

It mistakes correlation for causation, and specifically fails to consider that strong economies attract more immigrants rather than immigrants causing economic strength, while also assuming that any additional immigration will produce the same association observed in the data.
It does not specify what types of immigration should be increased.
It assumes that all immigrants contribute equally to the economy.
It ignores the political challenges of changing immigration policy.

explanation: Two related flaws: (1) Direction of causation — strong economies may attract immigrants (reverse causation). (2) Scope shift — the observed correlation at different levels doesn't prove that increasing immigration from current levels will produce economic gains.

passage: Professor: 'Students who use laptops in my class score lower on exams than students who take notes by hand. Therefore, laptops impair learning.'
questionStem: Which of the following best identifies the flaw in the professor's reasoning?

options.text: It does not consider that some students may use both laptops and handwritten notes.

It assumes that the laptop itself causes lower scores, rather than considering that students who use laptops may be more likely to engage in distracting activities like browsing the internet during class.
It fails to account for differences in exam difficulty.
It ignores that some students are better at typing than writing by hand.
It does not consider the professor's teaching style.

explanation: The flaw is mistaking what's actually causing the effect. It may not be the laptop as a note-taking tool that's harmful, but rather the distraction opportunities (internet, social media) that come with having a laptop open.

passage: Critics of a proposed minimum wage increase argue: 'A small business owner we interviewed said the increase would force her to lay off half her staff. Therefore, the minimum wage increase will cause widespread unemployment.'
questionStem: Which of the following best describes the flaw in the critics' reasoning?

options.text: It fails to consider that the minimum wage may need to increase due to inflation.

It generalizes from one anecdotal case to a broad economic outcome, and takes the business owner's prediction at face value without considering that businesses may adapt through other means such as modest price increases or efficiency improvements.
It does not consider the benefits of higher wages for workers.
It assumes that all businesses are small businesses.
It ignores the views of employees.

explanation: Two flaws: (1) Anecdotal evidence — one business owner's prediction doesn't represent all businesses. (2) Uncritical acceptance — the owner may be exaggerating, and businesses often adapt through methods other than layoffs.

passage: **Solar energy costs have decreased by 89% over the past decade.** Some energy analysts argue that this trend means solar will inevitably replace fossil fuels as the primary energy source. However, this conclusion is premature. **Solar energy currently accounts for only 4% of global electricity generation, and the intermittent nature of solar power requires expensive battery storage solutions that have not yet scaled economically.**
questionStem: In the argument above, the two boldface portions play which of the following roles?

options.text: The first is a premise supporting the analysts' claim; the second provides evidence that undermines that claim.
The first is the main conclusion of the argument; the second supports that conclusion.
The first and second are both premises supporting the same conclusion.
The first is an assumption made by the author; the second challenges that assumption.
The first is evidence the author rejects; the second is the author's main conclusion.

explanation: The first boldface (cost decrease) is cited as the basis for the analysts' conclusion. The second boldface (4% share + storage problems) is the author's evidence countering that conclusion. So: first = premise for analysts' claim, second = evidence undermining it.

passage: **Revenue from streaming services has grown 25% annually for five consecutive years.** Media executives predict that traditional broadcast television will become obsolete within a decade. But this prediction underestimates the resilience of broadcast TV. **Live events, particularly sports and news, still attract broadcast audiences that streaming platforms have struggled to capture consistently.**
questionStem: In the argument above, the two boldface portions play which of the following roles?

options.text: The first is background evidence cited by those whose view the author opposes; the second supports the author's own position.
The first is the author's main conclusion; the second is a premise supporting it.
The first and second both support the executives' prediction.
The first is irrelevant to the argument; the second is the author's conclusion.
The first challenges the author's conclusion; the second restates it.

explanation: First boldface = evidence cited by executives who predict broadcast TV's demise. Second boldface = evidence the author uses to argue against that prediction (broadcast still strong for live events).

passage: Some educators argue that standardized testing should be eliminated because it creates excessive stress for students. **However, standardized tests provide an objective, comparable measure of student achievement that cannot be replicated by subjective teacher assessments alone.** Without such measures, there would be no way to identify achievement gaps across schools and districts. **Therefore, rather than eliminating standardized testing, policymakers should focus on reducing the stakes attached to test scores while preserving the diagnostic value of the assessments.**
questionStem: In the argument above, the two boldface portions play which of the following roles?

options.text: The first is a counterargument to the educators' position; the second is the author's main conclusion.
The first is the author's main conclusion; the second provides supporting evidence.
The first is a premise of the educators' argument; the second is the author's objection.
The first and second are both premises leading to an unstated conclusion.
The first is evidence the author rejects; the second is an alternative explanation.

explanation: First boldface = the author's counter to the educators' elimination argument (tests have diagnostic value). Second boldface = the author's main conclusion (reduce stakes, don't eliminate). Structure: counter-premise → conclusion.

passage: **Research has shown that companies with diverse leadership teams outperform less diverse competitors by an average of 35%.** Diversity advocates use this data to argue that companies should prioritize diversity in hiring to improve financial performance. While the correlation is real, the causal mechanism is unclear — it may be that innovative, well-managed companies both attract diverse talent and outperform, without diversity itself being the direct cause. **The data alone does not establish that imposing diversity quotas will replicate the financial gains observed in naturally diverse companies.**
questionStem: In the argument above, the two boldface portions play which of the following roles?

options.text: The first is evidence that the author accepts as factually accurate but considers insufficient to support the advocates' conclusion; the second is the author's main conclusion.
The first is evidence the author rejects; the second is an alternative explanation.
The first supports the author's conclusion; the second weakens it.
The first is the author's conclusion; the second restates it in different terms.
The first and second both support the diversity advocates' position.

explanation: First boldface = research data the author acknowledges as real but questions as causal evidence. Second boldface = the author's conclusion that this data doesn't prove quotas will work. The author accepts the correlation but challenges the causal inference.

passage: **The city's crime rate dropped 15% last year.** The mayor claims this proves his policing strategy is working. Yet several factors beyond policing could explain the decline. **The city's unemployment rate also fell significantly last year, and research consistently links lower unemployment to lower crime rates.**
questionStem: In the argument above, the two boldface portions play which of the following roles?

options.text: The first is evidence the mayor uses to support his claim; the second is an alternative explanation that undermines the mayor's claim.
The first is the author's main conclusion; the second supports it.
The first is a claim the author rejects entirely; the second is the author's own claim.
The first and second are both premises supporting the mayor's position.
The first contradicts the second.

explanation: First boldface = the crime drop evidence the mayor cites. Second boldface = an alternative explanation (lower unemployment) that weakens the mayor's attribution to policing.

passage: Opponents of remote work argue that it reduces collaboration and innovation. **However, a meta-analysis of 47 studies found no statistically significant decrease in innovation output among remote teams compared to co-located teams.** Some critics question the meta-analysis, noting that innovation is difficult to measure. **While measurement challenges exist, the burden of proof should fall on those claiming remote work harms innovation, and the available evidence does not support that claim.**
questionStem: In the argument above, the two boldface portions play which of the following roles?

options.text: The first provides empirical evidence supporting the author's position; the second is the author's main conclusion, which incorporates an acknowledgment of limitations.
The first is evidence the author questions; the second is the critics' response.
The first is the main conclusion; the second is a supporting premise.
The first and second are both objections to remote work.
The first is an assumption; the second challenges that assumption.

explanation: First boldface = meta-analysis evidence the author cites to counter the opponents. Second boldface = the author's conclusion — acknowledges measurement limits but argues evidence doesn't support the anti-remote claim.

passage: **Global average temperatures have risen 1.1°C since pre-industrial times.** Climate scientists warn that exceeding 1.5°C could trigger irreversible environmental damage. Some policymakers counter that economic growth should take priority over emission cuts. **This prioritization is shortsighted, as the economic costs of unchecked climate change — including extreme weather, agricultural disruption, and coastal flooding — are projected to far exceed the costs of emission reduction.**
questionStem: In the argument above, the two boldface portions play which of the following roles?

options.text: The first provides factual context for the debate; the second is the author's main conclusion rejecting the policymakers' position.
The first is the author's main conclusion; the second is supporting evidence.
The first is a claim the author disagrees with; the second restates it.
The first and the second are both premises leading to an unstated conclusion.
The first is irrelevant background; the second is the policymakers' argument.

explanation: First boldface = factual background that frames the urgency. Second boldface = the author's conclusion — the economic-growth-first argument is shortsighted because climate costs will exceed reduction costs.

passage: **Studies consistently show that students from smaller class sizes perform better on standardized tests than students from larger classes.** Education reformers cite this evidence to advocate for reducing class sizes nationwide. However, class-size reduction is extraordinarily expensive, and the performance gains, while statistically significant, are modest compared to the gains achievable through other interventions such as high-quality tutoring programs. **The evidence therefore supports not a blanket reduction in class sizes but rather a targeted approach that invests in the most cost-effective interventions first.**
questionStem: In the argument above, the two boldface portions play which of the following roles?

options.text: The first is evidence the author accepts but considers an insufficient basis for the reformers' conclusion; the second is the author's own conclusion.
The first is the author's main conclusion; the second provides an alternative recommendation.
The first is evidence the author rejects; the second explains why.
The first and second are premises supporting the reformers' position.
The first is a premise the author uses to support the second, which is also a premise for an unstated conclusion.

explanation: First boldface = evidence the author acknowledges (class size helps) but considers insufficient to justify the reformers' expensive proposal. Second boldface = the author's conclusion — invest in cost-effective interventions first, not blanket class-size reduction.

passage: **Coffee consumption has been linked to a 15% reduction in the risk of liver disease.** Some health advocates recommend that everyone drink at least three cups of coffee per day. **However, excessive coffee consumption can cause insomnia, anxiety, and elevated heart rate, particularly in individuals sensitive to caffeine.**
questionStem: In the argument above, the two boldface portions play which of the following roles?

options.text: The first supports the health advocates' recommendation; the second provides evidence that complicates that recommendation.
The first is the main conclusion; the second is a supporting premise.
The first and second support the same conclusion.
The first is irrelevant background; the second is the main conclusion.
The first is an objection the author raises; the second defends against it.

explanation: First boldface = evidence supporting the advocates' pro-coffee recommendation. Second boldface = evidence of coffee's downsides that complicates the universal recommendation.

passage: Critics allege that artificial intelligence will eliminate more jobs than it creates, pointing to automation of routine tasks. **Yet historical precedent from previous technological revolutions — the Industrial Revolution, electrification, and computerization — shows that while specific jobs are displaced, new industries and job categories emerge that ultimately increase total employment.** Admittedly, transition periods can cause significant hardship for displaced workers. **The key policy challenge, therefore, is not preventing AI adoption but rather ensuring that workers have access to retraining programs that equip them for the new roles AI will create.**
questionStem: In the argument above, the two boldface portions play which of the following roles?

options.text: The first is a historical premise supporting the author's optimistic view of AI's employment impact; the second is the author's main conclusion about what policy should focus on.
The first is the critics' main argument; the second is the author's rebuttal.
The first and second both support the critics' position.
The first is an assumption the author questions; the second provides the author's alternative assumption.
The first is evidence the author rejects; the second is the author's prediction.

explanation: First boldface = historical evidence (tech revolutions created net jobs) supporting the author's counter to the critics. Second boldface = the author's policy conclusion — focus on retraining, not blocking AI.

passage: A study found that employees who work remotely at least three days per week report higher job satisfaction than those who work entirely in the office. However, these employees also report feeling more isolated from their colleagues. For remote work to be sustainable in the long term, companies must therefore __________.
questionStem: Which of the following most logically completes the argument?

options.text: Require all employees to work from the office full time.
Find ways to reduce the isolation that remote workers experience while maintaining the flexibility that contributes to their satisfaction.
Increase remote workers' salaries to compensate for isolation.
Monitor remote employees more closely to ensure productivity.
Eliminate remote work options for employees who report feeling isolated.

explanation: The argument identifies a tension: remote work → satisfaction but also isolation. For sustainability, the solution must address the downside (isolation) while preserving the upside (flexibility/satisfaction). B does exactly this.

passage: Experts predict that electric vehicle sales will surpass gasoline vehicle sales within the next decade. However, the current electric grid in many countries cannot support the additional demand that widespread EV adoption would create. Unless __________. widespread EV adoption will likely cause significant power grid disruptions.
questionStem: Which of the following most logically completes the argument?

options.text: Electric vehicles become less popular with consumers.
Significant investments are made to upgrade and expand the electrical grid infrastructure before EV adoption reaches critical mass.
Gasoline prices decrease substantially.
Governments ban the sale of gasoline vehicles.
Electric vehicles become more fuel-efficient.

explanation: Logic: Grid can't handle mass EV demand → disruptions UNLESS grid is upgraded in time. B provides the necessary condition to prevent the disruption.

passage: Supporters of year-round schooling argue that it prevents the 'summer slide' — the loss of academic skills during the traditional summer break. Opponents counter that students need extended breaks to rest and pursue non-academic interests. The evidence suggests that the summer slide primarily affects students from lower-income families who lack access to enrichment activities. Therefore, the most efficient approach to addressing the summer slide would be to __________.
questionStem: Which of the following most logically completes the argument?

options.text: Implement year-round schooling for all students regardless of income level.
Eliminate summer break entirely.
Provide targeted summer academic programs for lower-income students rather than restructuring the entire school calendar.
Extend the school day during the academic year to cover more material.
Allow parents to choose whether their children attend school during the summer.

explanation: The evidence narrows the problem to lower-income students. The most efficient solution targets the affected group rather than changing the entire system. C provides targeted intervention.

passage: A fitness company markets its product by claiming that its supplement increases muscle growth by 50%. However, the company's study compared supplement users who followed a structured weight training program to non-users who did not exercise. The study's results therefore demonstrate not that the supplement increases muscle growth, but rather that __________.
questionStem: Which of the following most logically completes the argument?

options.text: The supplement is ineffective at promoting muscle growth.
The combination of supplementation and exercise produces more muscle growth than no exercise, which does not isolate the supplement's independent effect.
Weight training alone is sufficient for muscle growth.
The supplement may cause health problems.
The company's marketing practices are deceptive.

explanation: The study conflates two variables (supplement + exercise vs. neither). The result shows the combined effect, not the supplement's independent contribution. B correctly identifies what was actually demonstrated.

passage: Many cities have invested in bike-sharing programs to reduce traffic congestion. However, studies show that the majority of bike-share users are people who previously commuted by public transit or walking, not by car. This finding suggests that bike-sharing programs, as currently designed, __________.
questionStem: Which of the following most logically completes the argument?

options.text: Should be expanded to include more stations.
Are unlikely to significantly reduce traffic congestion, since they primarily shift riders from other non-car modes rather than removing cars from the road.
Are too expensive to maintain.
Benefit only a small segment of the population.
Increase safety risks for cyclists.

explanation: If bike-share draws from transit/walking, not driving, then cars aren't being removed from roads → no congestion reduction. B follows logically from the evidence.

passage: Proponents of organic farming argue that it is better for the environment because it avoids synthetic pesticides. However, organic farming typically produces 20-25% lower crop yields per acre than conventional farming. If the world's food supply were produced entirely through organic methods, significantly more land would need to be converted to farmland. This conversion would likely involve __________.
questionStem: Which of the following most logically completes the argument?

options.text: Higher food prices for consumers.
Destruction of forests and natural habitats on a scale that could cause more environmental damage than the synthetic pesticides that organic farming avoids.
A shortage of organic seeds.
Increased water usage for irrigation.
Greater demand for farm labor.

explanation: Logic: lower yields → more farmland needed → habitat/forest destruction. B completes by connecting the land-use consequence to an environmental harm potentially worse than what organic farming prevents.

passage: A new study found that students who type their notes on laptops record more words per lecture than students who write notes by hand. However, students who write by hand scored higher on conceptual understanding tests. This suggests that __________.
questionStem: Which of the following most logically completes the argument?

options.text: Laptops should be banned from all classrooms.
Recording more information does not necessarily lead to deeper learning, and the process of handwriting may encourage more active cognitive processing of the material.
Typing is an inefficient way to learn.
Students should use both methods equally.
Handwritten notes are easier to review.

explanation: More words (typing) but lower understanding vs. fewer words (handwriting) but higher understanding → volume ≠ depth. B captures this insight about the relationship between note-taking method and cognitive processing.

passage: The government provides subsidies to domestic solar panel manufacturers to help them compete with cheaper imports. However, these subsidies make solar panels more expensive for consumers than they would be if the cheaper imports were allowed without restriction. Ironically, by making solar energy more costly for consumers, the subsidies may actually __________.
questionStem: Which of the following most logically completes the argument?

options.text: Increase domestic employment in the solar industry.
Slow the adoption of solar energy by making it less financially attractive compared to fossil fuels, undermining the environmental goals that solar subsidies are meant to advance.
Reduce the quality of domestically produced solar panels.
Encourage consumers to install wind turbines instead.
Lead to trade disputes with foreign governments.

explanation: The irony: subsidies meant to promote solar actually make it more expensive → slower adoption → less environmental benefit. B captures the self-defeating consequence.

passage: Artificial intelligence systems trained to make lending decisions have been shown to approve loans for a higher percentage of qualified applicants from minority groups than human loan officers do. Paradoxically, the deployment of AI lending systems has intensified scrutiny from civil rights organizations. This reaction may be explained by the fact that __________.
questionStem: Which of the following most logically completes the argument?

options.text: AI systems are expensive to implement.

Even if AI performs better on average, the opacity of algorithmic decision-making makes it impossible for denied applicants to understand or challenge the basis for rejection, creating accountability concerns that did not arise with human decision-makers whose reasoning could be examined.
Some AI systems have been found to have biases in other domains.
Civil rights organizations are resistant to new technology.
AI systems can process loan applications faster than human officers.

explanation: The paradox: AI is more fair statistically but draws more scrutiny. B explains why — the 'black box' nature of AI makes individual denials unaccountable, even if aggregate outcomes are better.

passage: Behavioral economists have documented that people consistently prefer smaller immediate rewards over larger delayed rewards, a phenomenon known as hyperbolic discounting. Some policymakers propose using this insight to design retirement savings programs that automatically enroll employees and gradually increase contribution rates. Critics argue that such programs are paternalistic because they override individual choice. However, this criticism misconceives the nature of the intervention, because __________.
questionStem: Which of the following most logically completes the argument?

options.text: Most people lack financial literacy.

Auto-enrollment programs allow individuals to opt out at any time, so they preserve individual choice while simply changing the default from non-participation to participation — correcting a cognitive bias without removing freedom.
Retirement savings are important for financial security.
Some countries have already implemented similar programs.
Hyperbolic discounting affects all people equally.

explanation: The critics say: paternalistic = overrides choice. B rebuts by showing the design preserves choice (opt-out is available) — it only changes the default, nudging rather than mandating.

passage: A school district is considering eliminating its art program to redirect funds to STEM education. Supporters say STEM skills are more relevant to the job market. Opponents argue that arts education develops creativity and emotional intelligence.
questionStem: Which of the following would be most useful to evaluate in order to decide whether to eliminate the art program?

options.text: Whether art teachers at the school are certified.
Whether students who receive arts education alongside STEM perform better in STEM careers and demonstrate skills valued by employers that STEM-only students lack.
Whether other school districts have eliminated their art programs.
Whether the school district has a budget surplus.
Whether students prefer art classes to STEM classes.

explanation: The decision hinges on whether arts add value beyond STEM. B directly tests this by comparing outcomes of arts+STEM vs. STEM-only students.

passage: A country is debating whether to legalize a currently prohibited recreational substance. Proponents cite economic benefits from taxation. Opponents warn of increased public health costs.
questionStem: Which of the following would be most useful to evaluate in order to assess the net economic impact of legalization?

options.text: Whether the substance is legal in neighboring countries.

Whether comprehensive data from jurisdictions that have already legalized the substance shows that tax revenues consistently exceed the additional public health and law enforcement costs, and whether those jurisdictions are economically comparable.
Whether public opinion favors legalization.
Whether the substance is addictive.
Whether current prohibition effectively prevents use of the substance.

explanation: The debate is about net economic impact (tax revenue vs. health costs). B provides the most relevant evidence — real data from comparable jurisdictions covering both sides of the equation.

passage: A company is considering switching from annual bonuses to monthly performance-based rewards. Proponents say frequent rewards motivate employees more consistently. Skeptics worry that it will encourage short-term thinking.
questionStem: Which of the following would be most useful to determine in order to evaluate this proposal?

options.text: Whether the company's competitors use annual or monthly bonus structures.
Whether pilot programs testing monthly rewards at similar companies showed sustained or declining employee engagement and whether they observed changes in the quality of long-term strategic work.
Whether employees prefer monthly bonuses over annual ones.
Whether the administrative cost of monthly bonuses is higher.
Whether some employees are more motivated by financial rewards than others.

explanation: The debate is about whether frequent rewards sustain motivation without encouraging short-termism. B directly tests both through pilot data from comparable companies.

passage: A farmer is deciding whether to switch from traditional irrigation to drip irrigation. A consultant says drip irrigation saves 40% of water. The farmer worries about the high upfront installation cost.
questionStem: Which of the following would be most useful to determine in order to evaluate whether the switch is worthwhile?

options.text: Whether other farmers in the area use drip irrigation.
Whether the water cost savings over a reasonable period will exceed the installation cost, taking into account the farmer's specific crop type and water usage.
Whether drip irrigation is better for the environment.
Whether the farmer can get a loan for the installation.
Whether drip irrigation requires more maintenance.

explanation: The farmer's concern is cost. B directly evaluates whether the investment pays for itself through savings — the key financial question.

passage: Researchers are debating whether artificial sweeteners are a safe alternative to sugar for weight management. Some studies show people who consume artificial sweeteners actually gain more weight than sugar consumers.
questionStem: Which of the following would be most useful to evaluate in order to determine whether artificial sweeteners contribute to weight gain?

options.text: Whether artificial sweeteners taste different from sugar.

Whether randomized controlled trials — where participants are assigned to artificial sweeteners or sugar rather than choosing — show weight differences, since observational studies may suffer from reverse causation where heavier individuals choose sweeteners.
Whether artificial sweeteners are approved by food safety agencies.
Whether sugar consumption has been increasing in the general population.
Whether there are different types of artificial sweeteners.

explanation: The key issue is causation vs. selection. B proposes the gold standard (RCTs) to eliminate the reverse causation problem — overweight people may choose sweeteners rather than sweeteners causing weight gain.

passage: A company provided all employees with standing desks to reduce sedentary behavior. After six months, employee surveys revealed that average daily standing time had not increased compared to before the standing desks were provided.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, best explains the discrepancy?

options.text: Standing desks are adjustable to multiple heights.
Most employees found standing uncomfortable for long periods and lowered their desks to sitting position within the first week, using them identically to their previous desks.
The company also offered ergonomic chairs.
Standing desks were available in three colors.
Employee productivity remained unchanged.

explanation: Expected: standing desks → more standing. Actual: no change. B explains: employees tried standing but found it uncomfortable, so they just sat at the 'standing' desks — behavior didn't change.

passage: A country introduced a generous tax deduction for charitable donations, expecting total charitable giving to increase. In the first year, the total dollar amount donated to charities remained virtually unchanged despite widespread use of the new deduction.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, best explains why charitable giving did not increase?

options.text: The tax deduction was widely publicized by the government.

Donors who took advantage of the deduction redirected money they were already planning to donate, and some donors reduced cash giving because they donated appreciated securities instead, which had the same tax value but lower out-of-pocket cost.
Some charities reported an increase in the number of individual donors.
The tax deduction applied to all types of charitable organizations.
Charitable giving in neighboring countries increased during the same period.

explanation: Expected: tax deduction → more giving. Actual: same total amount. B explains: existing donors restructured their giving (cash → securities) rather than giving more. The deduction changed the form but not the total.

passage: A hospital implemented a new electronic health records (EHR) system promising to save doctors 30 minutes per day on paperwork. However, six months after full implementation, doctors reported spending more time on documentation than before the system was introduced.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, best explains the increase in documentation time?

options.text: The EHR system was purchased from a reputable vendor.

The EHR system's interface required doctors to navigate through many more screens and dropdown menus than the old paper system, and it automatically generated follow-up documentation requirements that paper records did not trigger.
Nurses adapted to the new system faster than doctors.
The hospital also hired additional administrative staff.
Some doctors had limited experience with computers.

explanation: Expected: EHR → 30 min saved daily. Actual: more time on documentation. B explains: the digital system introduced new complexity (more screens, auto-generated requirements) that exceeded the time saved by going paperless.

passage: A recent survey found that 90% of consumers say they prefer to buy products from environmentally responsible companies. However, market data shows that eco-friendly products account for only 15% of total sales in their categories. This discrepancy suggests that __________.
questionStem: Which of the following most logically completes the argument?

options.text: Environmental marketing is ineffective.

Consumers' stated preferences do not reliably predict their actual purchasing behavior, likely because factors such as price, convenience, and brand loyalty outweigh environmental considerations at the point of purchase.
The survey was poorly designed.
Eco-friendly products are of lower quality.
Companies should stop investing in environmental initiatives.

explanation: 90% say they prefer eco → but only 15% buy eco. B explains the stated-preference/revealed-preference gap: people say one thing but do another when cost and convenience enter the equation.

passage: Studies show that diverse teams generate more creative solutions than homogeneous teams. However, diverse teams also experience more interpersonal conflict and take longer to reach consensus. For organizations to capture the creativity benefits of diversity, they must therefore __________.
questionStem: Which of the following most logically completes the argument?

options.text: Avoid hiring diverse teams.
Invest in conflict resolution training and structured collaboration processes that help diverse teams manage their differences productively.
Only assemble diverse teams for creative projects.
Reduce team sizes to minimize conflict.
Prioritize homogeneity for efficiency.

explanation: Diversity = more creativity but more conflict. To get creativity without being destroyed by conflict, invest in managing the conflict productively (B).

passage: Opponents of universal healthcare argue that government-run systems lead to long wait times for medical procedures. However, data from countries with universal healthcare shows that while elective procedure wait times are longer, wait times for urgent and emergency care are comparable to or shorter than those in the United States. This evidence suggests that long wait times in universal systems are not a fundamental flaw but rather __________.
questionStem: Which of the following most logically completes the argument?

options.text: A result of inadequate funding.
A deliberate resource allocation choice that prioritizes urgent medical needs over elective procedures, a tradeoff that can be adjusted through policy without abandoning universal coverage.
An inevitable consequence of government bureaucracy.
A problem that only affects certain countries.
A minor inconvenience for patients.

explanation: The data shows universal systems prioritize urgent over elective care. B frames this as a policy choice (not a fundamental flaw), implying it can be optimized without abandoning the system.

passage: **Violent crime rates in the United States have declined steadily for three decades.** Despite this trend, surveys show that a majority of Americans believe crime is increasing. This disconnect between perception and reality is likely driven by media coverage that disproportionately focuses on violent incidents. **Therefore, Americans' fear of crime is based more on media exposure than on actual risk.**
questionStem: In the argument above, the two boldface portions play which of the following roles?

options.text: The first is a factual premise; the second is the main conclusion drawn from the evidence presented.
The first is the main conclusion; the second is a supporting premise.
The first and second are both conclusions.
The first is a claim the author disputes; the second supports the dispute.
The first is an assumption; the second is a consequence of that assumption.

explanation: First boldface = factual premise (crime is actually declining). Second boldface = conclusion (fear is media-driven, not reality-driven).

passage: Some nutritionists claim that organic food is significantly healthier than conventionally grown food. **However, a comprehensive review of over 200 studies found no consistent evidence that organic foods provide meaningful nutritional advantages over their conventional counterparts.** While organic farming may have environmental benefits, the health claims are not supported by current evidence. **Consumers who choose organic solely for health reasons may therefore be paying a premium for a benefit that does not exist.**
questionStem: In the argument above, the two boldface portions play which of the following roles?

options.text: The first provides evidence that undermines the nutritionists' claim; the second is the author's conclusion based on that evidence.
The first is the nutritionists' claim; the second is the author's rebuttal.
The first is the author's conclusion; the second provides supporting evidence.
The first and second both support the nutritionists' claim.
The first is an assumption the author makes; the second challenges it.

explanation: First boldface = evidence against health claims (200-study review found no advantage). Second boldface = conclusion (paying premium for non-existent benefit).

passage: **Historical data shows that every U.S. recession in the past 50 years was preceded by an inverted yield curve.** Some investors use this pattern to predict recessions and adjust their portfolios accordingly. However, an inverted yield curve has also occurred without a subsequent recession on several occasions. **Therefore, while an inverted yield curve may be a necessary precursor to recent recessions, it is not a sufficient condition for predicting that a recession will occur, and investment strategies based solely on this indicator are unreliable.**
questionStem: In the argument above, the two boldface portions play which of the following roles?

options.text: The first is evidence the author considers relevant but incomplete; the second is the author's conclusion about the limitations of that evidence as a predictive tool.
The first is the investors' premise that the author rejects; the second is the author's alternative theory.
The first is the author's main conclusion; the second is a supporting premise.
The first and second are both premises supporting the investors' strategy.
The first is an assumption; the second challenges the assumption.

explanation: First boldface = historical correlation the author accepts but considers incomplete (necessary but not sufficient). Second boldface = conclusion about its predictive limitations.

passage: A company spokesperson states: 'Our product has been on the market for 20 years with no lawsuits filed against it. This proves that our product is completely safe.'
questionStem: Which of the following best identifies the flaw in the spokesperson's reasoning?

options.text: It does not compare the product to competitors' products.
It equates the absence of lawsuits with the absence of safety problems, ignoring that harmed individuals may not sue for various reasons including cost, awareness, or statute of limitations.
It does not mention the product's price.
It assumes the product will remain safe in the future.
It fails to describe the product's features.

explanation: Absence of evidence is not evidence of absence. No lawsuits ≠ no problems. People may not know they're harmed, can't afford lawyers, or face legal barriers.

passage: The school board president argues: 'Test scores at our schools have improved every year for the past five years. Therefore, our teaching quality is improving.'
questionStem: Which of the following best describes the flaw in the president's reasoning?

options.text: It does not compare scores to other districts.

It assumes test scores are a reliable indicator of teaching quality, ignoring that score increases could result from easier tests, teaching to the test, changes in student demographics, or other factors unrelated to actual teaching improvement.
It does not consider student satisfaction.
It assumes all teachers are equally effective.
It ignores the role of technology in education.

explanation: Test scores can improve for many reasons unrelated to teaching quality. The argument conflates one measure (scores) with the broader concept (teaching quality) without considering alternative explanations.

passage: A politician argues: 'Countries that have implemented universal basic income have not seen a decrease in workforce participation. Therefore, concerns that UBI will reduce people's motivation to work are entirely unfounded.'
questionStem: Which of the following best identifies the flaw in the politician's reasoning?

options.text: It ignores that UBI programs cost money to implement.

It generalizes from limited pilot programs that may not reflect long-term permanent effects, and it treats the absence of observed negative effects as proof that no negative effects will ever occur under different conditions or at different scales.
It does not consider the amount of the basic income payment.
It fails to address concerns about inflation.
It assumes all countries have similar labor markets.

explanation: Two flaws: (1) Limited data — pilots may not predict permanent program effects. (2) Absence of evidence treated as evidence of absence — 'not seen decrease' doesn't prove 'concerns are entirely unfounded.'

passage: A business analyst argues: 'Our company's stock price has increased every quarter for the past three years. This trend demonstrates that our management team's strategy is sound, and we should therefore continue the same strategic approach indefinitely.'
questionStem: Which of the following best describes the flaw in the analyst's reasoning?

options.text: It fails to consider competitors' stock performance.

It attributes the stock price increase solely to management strategy while ignoring market-wide conditions such as a bull market, industry tailwinds, or monetary policy, and it commits the fallacy of assuming that a past trend will continue indefinitely in potentially changing conditions.
It does not consider employee morale.
It assumes stockholders agree with the strategy.
It ignores the company's debt levels.

explanation: Multiple flaws: (1) Misattribution — stock gains may reflect market conditions, not strategy. (2) Induction error — past performance extrapolated to 'indefinitely.' (3) Survivorship — focuses on outcome (stock price) not process quality.

passage: A consumer advocate argues: 'Brand X and Brand Y laundry detergents contain the same active ingredients in the same concentrations. Therefore, there is absolutely no difference between the two products.'
questionStem: Which of the following best identifies the flaw in the advocate's reasoning?

options.text: It does not consider consumer preferences.

It assumes that active ingredients are the only relevant factor, ignoring that differences in inactive ingredients, formulation, fragrance, packaging, and manufacturing quality could create meaningful differences between the products.
It fails to mention the prices of the two products.
It does not consider the environmental impact of the products.
It assumes all consumers use the same amount of detergent.

explanation: Same active ingredients ≠ identical products. Inactive ingredients, formulation, and manufacturing can affect performance, shelf life, and user experience. The argument is too absolute ('absolutely no difference').

passage: My grandfather smoked cigarettes his entire life and lived to be 95. Therefore, smoking is not harmful to health.
questionStem: Which of the following best identifies the flaw in this reasoning?

options.text: It does not consider the cost of cigarettes.
It draws a general conclusion from a single anecdotal case, ignoring the overwhelming statistical evidence that smoking significantly increases health risks for the vast majority of people.
It does not mention other risk factors for health problems.
It assumes the grandfather was in good health throughout his life.
It ignores the effects of secondhand smoke.

explanation: Classic anecdotal fallacy. One person's experience doesn't override population-level data. The grandfather may have been an extreme genetic outlier.

passage: A pet food company switched to organic ingredients and saw customer complaints drop by 30%. The company concluded that organic ingredients produce healthier pet food.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the conclusion?

options.text: Organic ingredients are more expensive than conventional ones.
The company also changed its manufacturing process and quality control procedures when it switched to organic ingredients.
Some pets are allergic to certain organic ingredients.
The company's competitor also uses organic ingredients.
Customer complaints can be submitted online.

explanation: Alternative cause: manufacturing changes could explain fewer complaints.

passage: A survey found that employees who telecommute are 20% more productive than office workers. The CEO concluded that allowing all employees to telecommute would boost company productivity.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the CEO's conclusion?

options.text: Telecommuting reduces commuting stress.
Employees who telecommute were selected for the privilege because they had already demonstrated above-average self-discipline and productivity.
The company uses cloud-based collaboration tools.
Telecommuting saves office space costs.
Some employees prefer working from the office.

explanation: Selection bias: already productive workers got telecommuting privileges.

passage: After a city installed speed cameras on Oak Avenue, speeding violations on that street dropped by 60%. The city council argues speed cameras are the best tool for reducing speeding citywide.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the council's argument?

options.text: The cameras operate 24 hours a day.
Speeding violations on streets adjacent to Oak Avenue increased by 50% during the same period as drivers shifted routes.
Speed cameras are relatively inexpensive to maintain.
Some drivers received multiple speeding tickets.
The cameras were installed at the most dangerous intersections.

explanation: Crime displacement: speeders moved to other streets rather than stopping.

passage: A food delivery company found that restaurants using its premium listing service received 45% more orders. The company claims the premium service directly increases restaurant business.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the company's claim?

options.text: The premium service includes professional food photography.
Restaurants that purchase the premium listing are already among the most popular and highest-rated establishments in their area.
The company offers three price tiers for its services.
Some restaurants without premium listings also saw order increases.
The premium listing includes a featured badge visible to customers.

explanation: Selection bias: already popular restaurants buy premium listings.

passage: A hospital found that patients treated in private rooms recovered 15% faster than those in shared wards. The hospital board recommended converting all shared wards to private rooms.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the board's recommendation?

options.text: Private rooms cost more to build and maintain.
Patients assigned to private rooms were predominantly those with better insurance coverage, higher incomes, and less severe conditions.
Some patients prefer shared wards for social interaction.
Private rooms reduce the risk of hospital-acquired infections.
The hospital has limited construction budget.

explanation: Confound: private-room patients were healthier and wealthier to begin with.

passage: A marketing study found that consumers who watched a 30-second ad recalled the product 40% better than those who saw a 15-second ad. The advertising firm recommended all clients switch to 30-second ads.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the firm's recommendation?

options.text: 30-second ads cost more than twice as much as 15-second ads to produce and air.

The study participants watched only one ad during the session, whereas in real-world settings consumers see dozens of ads consecutively and recall differences between ad lengths diminish to near zero.
Some consumers fast-forward through ads on streaming platforms.
The study was conducted with a sample of 500 participants.
Product recall does not always translate to purchase intent.

explanation: Lab conditions differ from reality: with ad clutter, the length advantage disappears.

passage: Researchers found that children who attend summer academic camps score higher on fall standardized tests. A parents' association argued that sending all children to summer camps would eliminate achievement gaps.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the association's argument?

options.text: Summer camps offer a variety of academic and recreational activities.
Parents who send children to summer academic camps tend to be highly educated and provide extensive academic support at home year-round.
The camps operate for six weeks during the summer.
Achievement gaps exist across socioeconomic lines.
Some children who attend camp still score below average on fall tests.

explanation: Confound: educated parents provide year-round support; camp attendance correlates with but doesn't cause higher scores.

passage: A wine magazine rated wines from Region X as superior based on blind taste tests with professional sommeliers. The magazine concluded that Region X produces the world's best wines.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the magazine's conclusion?

options.text: Region X has a long winemaking tradition.

The wines from Region X that were submitted for the tasting were pre-selected by the region's wine council as their top examples, while wines from other regions were randomly selected without curation.
Professional sommeliers undergo extensive training.
Wine quality can be subjective.
Region X's climate is well-suited to viticulture.

explanation: Unfair comparison: X submitted its best; others were random selections.

passage: A tech company reports that its employees who use the company gym during lunch breaks take 30% fewer sick days. The company plans to build larger gym facilities to improve employee health across the organization.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the plan's rationale?

options.text: The gym offers modern exercise equipment.
Employees who use the gym during lunch are already the healthiest employees, and expanding facilities is unlikely to persuade less healthy employees to exercise.
The gym is open from 6 AM to 9 PM.
Sick days cost the company an average of $500 per day per employee.
The company provides free gym memberships to all employees.

explanation: Self-selection: healthy employees choose the gym. Bigger facilities won't change less-healthy employees' behavior.

passage: A government study found that regions where public libraries offer free internet access have lower rates of digital illiteracy. The government concluded that expanding free library internet access will reduce digital illiteracy nationwide.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the government's conclusion?

options.text: Public libraries have been community institutions for over a century.

Regions with free library internet are wealthier urban areas where residents already have home internet access and computer skills, while digitally illiterate populations in rural areas lack transportation to reach libraries.
Digital literacy programs have been effective in some schools.
The study measured digital literacy using a standardized assessment.
Some libraries offer computer training classes.

explanation: Confound: library-internet regions are already digitally literate. Target populations can't even reach libraries.

passage: A pharmaceutical company's study showed patients taking its new sleep medication fell asleep 20 minutes faster and slept 45 minutes longer than a placebo group. The company markets the drug as significantly improving sleep quality.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the company's marketing claim?

options.text: The medication is available by prescription only.
