Participants taking the medication reported feeling no more rested the next day than placebo users, and sleep studies showed the medication increased only light sleep phases while not improving deep or REM sleep associated with restorative rest.
Some participants experienced mild side effects.
The study lasted 12 weeks.
The medication is not habit-forming.

explanation: More sleep quantity does not equal better sleep quality. The drug increased non-restorative light sleep, not the deep/REM sleep that matters.

passage: An education nonprofit found that schools using its tutoring program had 25% higher graduation rates. The nonprofit seeks government funding, arguing its program significantly improves educational outcomes.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the nonprofit's argument?

options.text: The tutoring program uses trained college volunteers.
Schools that adopted the program were those that had recently received large grants and hired additional counselors, administrators, and support staff as part of broader reform efforts.
The program has been operating for five years.
Some tutored students also received free school meals.
Graduation rates nationally have been slowly improving.

explanation: Concurrent reforms: schools with the program also got grants and extra staff. The tutoring program alone isn't responsible.

passage: A city found that neighborhoods with community gardens have 20% lower crime rates. The city council proposed building community gardens in high-crime neighborhoods to reduce criminal activity.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the council's proposal?

options.text: Community gardens require ongoing maintenance and volunteer participation.
Neighborhoods that successfully establish community gardens already have strong social networks and civic engagement that both enable garden creation and independently deter crime.
Some community gardens have been vandalized.
The city has unused lots that could be converted to gardens.
Community gardens provide fresh produce to residents.

explanation: Reverse causation: strong communities create gardens AND have low crime. Gardens don't cause the low crime; community cohesion does.

passage: A longitudinal study tracking 20,000 adults over 25 years found that those who regularly practiced meditation had a 35% lower risk of developing anxiety disorders. Mental health advocates recommend widespread adoption of meditation programs in schools and workplaces to prevent anxiety.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the advocates' recommendation?

options.text: Meditation practices vary widely in technique and duration.

Individuals who develop anxiety disorders often abandon meditation early because their symptoms make sustained practice difficult, meaning the study's finding likely reflects reverse causation rather than a protective effect of meditation.
Some meditation practitioners still develop anxiety.
The study controlled for income and education.
Mindfulness-based therapies have shown promise in treating existing anxiety.

explanation: Reverse causation: anxious people quit meditation, so non-meditators are disproportionately anxiety-prone. Meditation didn't prevent anxiety; anxiety prevented meditation.

passage: Economists found that countries with more independent central banks have lower inflation rates. Based on this evidence, a policy institute recommends that developing countries grant their central banks full independence to control inflation.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the institute's recommendation?

options.text: Central bank independence is a feature of many developed economies.

Countries that granted central bank independence were already those with strong institutions, rule of law, and political stability that independently keep inflation low, while developing countries that adopted similar formal independence without these underlying institutional foundations saw no inflation improvement.
Inflation is influenced by many factors beyond monetary policy.
Some independent central banks have made policy errors.
Central bank independence can reduce democratic accountability.

explanation: Institutional context matters: formal independence without underlying governance quality doesn't work. The recommendation ignores that the correlation reflects broader institutional quality.

passage: A fitness tracker company found that users who logged more than 10,000 steps daily weighed less on average than those who logged fewer steps. The company advertises that walking 10,000 steps daily leads to weight loss.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the company's advertising claim?

options.text: The fitness tracker accurately counts steps within a 5% margin of error.
People who are already lean and fit are more likely to walk extensively, while overweight individuals tend to walk less due to joint pain and fatigue.
Walking has many health benefits beyond weight management.
Some users who walk 10,000 steps daily also follow strict diets.
The 10,000-step recommendation originated in a Japanese marketing campaign.

explanation: Reverse causation: lean people walk more, not walking makes people lean.

passage: Since implementing a school uniform policy, Lakeside High School has seen a 15% improvement in test scores. The principal credits the uniform policy for improved academic performance.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the principal's credit?

options.text: School uniforms reduce visible socioeconomic differences among students.
The uniform policy was implemented at the same time as a new curriculum, extended school hours, and a tutoring program.
Students generally comply with the uniform policy.
Parent involvement at the school has remained stable.
The school's sports teams have also improved their records.

explanation: Multiple simultaneous changes: curriculum, hours, tutoring could all explain the improvement.

passage: A financial services firm found that clients who use its robo-advisor platform achieve 12% higher returns than those using traditional financial advisors. The firm claims its technology outperforms human advisors.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the firm's claim?

options.text: The robo-advisor uses sophisticated algorithms based on modern portfolio theory.
Clients who choose the robo-advisor platform tend to be younger, more tech-savvy investors who start with higher-risk portfolios during a bull market that disproportionately rewarded such strategies.
The robo-advisor platform charges lower fees than traditional advisors.
Some traditional advisory clients also achieved high returns.
The platform has been available for five years.

explanation: Selection bias + market conditions: younger risk-tolerant clients + bull market explain higher returns, not the technology.

passage: A city's transit authority reports that bus routes with dedicated bus lanes have 25% higher ridership than routes without dedicated lanes. The authority proposes adding dedicated bus lanes on all remaining routes to increase ridership.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the authority's proposal?

options.text: Dedicated bus lanes reduce travel time for bus riders.

The routes selected for dedicated lanes were already the highest-demand routes in the system, and adding dedicated lanes to lower-demand routes in previous expansions produced negligible ridership increases.
Bus ridership has been declining nationally.
Adding dedicated lanes requires removing a lane of car traffic.
The authority has secured funding for the expansion.

explanation: Selection bias + evidence against: high-demand routes got lanes first, and past expansions to lower-demand routes didn't work.

passage: A study found that patients who participate in clinical trials have better health outcomes than similar patients who receive standard care outside trials. Researchers concluded that clinical trial participation itself provides a health benefit.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the researchers' conclusion?

options.text: Clinical trials require informed consent from participants.

Patients in clinical trials receive more frequent monitoring, follow-up visits, and attention from medical staff than standard-care patients, and these additional care elements independently improve outcomes regardless of the experimental treatment being tested.
Some clinical trial participants receive placebos.
Clinical trials follow strict ethical guidelines.
Participants in clinical trials tend to be more health-conscious.

explanation: The additional monitoring and care in trials - not trial participation per se - explains better outcomes.

passage: A real estate agent notes that homes with swimming pools sell for 15% more than comparable homes without pools. She advises her client to install a pool before selling to maximize the sale price.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the agent's advice?

options.text: Pool installation takes approximately three weeks.
The cost of installing a pool exceeds the typical 15% price premium, and homes with pools take longer to sell because many buyers view pool maintenance as a burden.
Some buyers specifically seek homes with pools.
The client's home is in a warm-climate area.
Property taxes are based on assessed home value.

explanation: Cost exceeds benefit AND pools narrow buyer pool: the advice would lose money and slow the sale.

passage: Research shows that companies with women in top management positions are 25% more profitable than those without. A business consultant recommends that companies appoint more women to senior roles to increase profitability.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the consultant's recommendation?

options.text: Women currently hold only 30% of senior management positions globally.
Better-managed and more innovative companies are both more likely to promote women to leadership and more profitable, but the women's presence does not directly cause the profitability advantage.
Some companies with women in leadership are not highly profitable.
The research controlled for company size.
Women bring diverse perspectives to management teams.

explanation: Confound: good management practices both promote women AND drive profits. Women's presence is a symptom of good management, not the cause of profitability.

passage: A university reports that students who study abroad have starting salaries 15% higher than those who do not. The admissions office promotes study abroad as a way to boost career prospects.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the admissions office's promotion?

options.text: Study abroad programs are available in over 40 countries.
Students who study abroad disproportionately come from wealthier families with professional networks that independently provide career advantages.
The university's study abroad program has won several awards.
Some students who study abroad change their career plans afterward.
Study abroad programs typically last one semester.

explanation: Wealth and networks confound: these students would have earned more regardless of study abroad.

passage: A tech company found that its products designed using A/B testing outperform products designed without A/B testing by 20% on key engagement metrics. The VP of Product mandated A/B testing for all future product decisions.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the mandate?

options.text: A/B testing is an industry-standard practice.

Products selected for A/B testing were already the company's highest-priority products receiving the most resources and attention, while products not A/B tested were lower-priority features with minimal investment.
A/B tests require a minimum sample size to be statistically valid.
Some A/B tests produce inconclusive results.
The company has the technical infrastructure to support widespread A/B testing.

explanation: Selection bias: top products got both more resources AND A/B testing. The testing didn't cause the better outcomes; the resource allocation did.

passage: A health magazine reported that people who drink green tea daily have lower rates of certain cancers. The magazine advises readers to drink green tea to reduce cancer risk.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the magazine's advice?

options.text: Green tea contains antioxidants called catechins.
Daily green tea drinkers in the study population were primarily in Japan and China, where overall diet, genetic factors, and lifestyle differ significantly from the magazine's readership.
Green tea is widely available in supermarkets.
Some green tea drinkers still develop cancer.
The study tracked participants for ten years.

explanation: Generalizability problem: the study population differs from the target audience in genetics, diet, and lifestyle.

passage: After a restaurant added calorie counts to its menu, average calories per order decreased by 10%. The restaurant chain concluded that calorie labeling causes customers to make healthier choices.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the conclusion?

options.text: Calorie counts were displayed next to each menu item.
When calorie labels were introduced, the restaurant also reformulated several popular dishes to be lower in calories, so customers ordering the same items received fewer calories.
Some customers reported not noticing the calorie information.
The restaurant chain has over 500 locations.
Customer satisfaction scores remained unchanged.

explanation: The calorie reduction came from recipe changes, not customer behavior change.

passage: A study found that employees who received leadership training were 35% more likely to be promoted within two years. The HR department uses this to justify expanding the leadership training program.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the HR department's justification?

options.text: The leadership training program lasts three months.
Managers nominated their highest-potential employees for the training, and these employees were already on track for promotion before the training began.
The training covers communication, strategy, and team management.
Not all departments participate in the leadership training program.
The company promotes approximately 20% of employees annually.

explanation: Selection bias: highest-potential employees were chosen. They would have been promoted regardless.

passage: An online education platform found that students who complete its advanced certification courses earn 25% more than those who do not. The platform markets these certifications as a pathway to higher earnings.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the platform's marketing claim?

options.text: The certifications are recognized by many employers.
Students who complete advanced courses are disproportionately those who already hold high-paying positions and pursue certifications for professional development rather than career advancement.
The courses cover in-demand technical skills.
Completion rates for advanced courses average 40%.
The platform offers courses in over 50 subjects.

explanation: Self-selection: high earners take courses. The courses don't cause higher earnings.

passage: A venture capital firm found that startups it invested in had a 30% higher success rate than startups that sought but did not receive its funding. The firm claims its mentorship and support give startups a decisive advantage.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the firm's claim?

options.text: The firm invests in approximately 5% of startups that apply.

The firm's selection process specifically identifies startups with the strongest business models, most experienced founders, and largest addressable markets, and these factors independently predict startup success regardless of investor mentorship.
The firm has been operating for 15 years.
Some startups that received funding still failed.
The firm provides startups with office space and networking opportunities.

explanation: Selection effect: the firm picks winners. The success reflects selection skill, not post-investment support.

passage: A national study found that students taught by teachers with advanced degrees (master's or doctorate) scored 10% higher on standardized tests. The teachers' union argues this proves advanced degrees make teachers more effective and should be required for all teachers.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the union's argument?

options.text: Advanced degrees take two or more additional years to complete.

Teachers who pursue advanced degrees are generally more motivated and dedicated to their profession, and this intrinsic motivation drives both the decision to earn a degree and higher teaching effectiveness, rather than the degree itself providing additional skills.
Some states already require teachers to hold advanced degrees.
The study controlled for years of teaching experience.
Advanced degree programs for teachers are available online.

explanation: Self-selection: motivated teachers both earn degrees AND teach better. The degree isn't the cause.

passage: A large-scale randomized experiment found that students in classrooms using tablets for instruction scored 8% higher on end-of-year assessments than students using traditional textbooks. The technology company that funded the study recommends tablet-based learning for all schools.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the company's recommendation?

options.text: The tablets came preloaded with interactive educational software.

Teachers in the tablet classrooms received 80 hours of technology integration training and ongoing technical support that those in the traditional classrooms did not receive, and prior studies show that equivalent investment in teacher professional development without technology produces similar or larger achievement gains.
Students reported enjoying the tablet-based lessons.
The study ran for a full academic year.
The tablets cost $400 each.

explanation: Confound: the training investment, not the tablets, likely drove the gains. Equivalent investment in traditional teaching works just as well.

passage: A local newspaper reports that neighborhoods with farmers markets have higher property values. A neighborhood association lobbies the city to establish a farmers market to increase their property values.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the association's plan?

options.text: Farmers markets attract visitors from surrounding areas.

Farmers markets tend to be established in already affluent neighborhoods with high property values, and the few markets opened in lower-value neighborhoods have not produced measurable property value increases.
The proposed market would operate on Saturday mornings.
Vendors at farmers markets sell organic and artisan products.
Some neighborhoods have multiple farmers markets.

explanation: Reverse causation plus evidence against: wealthy areas get markets, not markets creating wealth. Past attempts in lower-value areas failed.

passage: A cosmetics company found that customers who purchased its premium skincare line reported more youthful-looking skin after three months. The company claims its premium products are more effective than its basic line.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the company's claim?

options.text: The premium line uses patented ingredients.
Customers who purchase the premium line also tend to use sunscreen more consistently, eat healthier diets, and visit dermatologists regularly compared to basic-line customers.
The premium line costs four times more than the basic line.
Some basic-line customers also reported skin improvements.
The company uses the same base formulation for both lines.

explanation: Confounding lifestyle factors: premium customers already take better care of their skin.

passage: A think tank found that countries with higher rates of immigration have higher GDP growth rates. The think tank recommends relaxing immigration restrictions to boost economic growth.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the think tank's recommendation?

options.text: Immigrants contribute to cultural diversity.

Countries with rapidly growing economies attract more immigrants because of job opportunities, but countries that relaxed immigration during economic downturns experienced increased unemployment and fiscal strain without corresponding GDP growth.
Immigration policies vary widely across countries.
Some immigrants are highly skilled professionals.
The study included both developed and developing countries.

explanation: Reverse causation + counter-evidence: growth attracts immigrants, and relaxing immigration during downturns didn't help.

passage: A software company noticed that its employees who use dual monitors are 25% more productive than those using single monitors. The company plans to purchase dual monitors for all employees.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the plan's rationale?

options.text: Dual monitors require additional desk space.

Employees who requested dual monitors are primarily programmers and data analysts whose work inherently benefits from extra screen space, while most other employees' tasks would see minimal productivity gains from a second monitor.
Dual monitor setups cost approximately $300 per employee.
Some employees prefer larger single monitors to dual monitors.
The company recently upgraded its office internet connection.

explanation: Not generalizable: specific roles benefit, but most employees won't see gains.

passage: A university study found that students who volunteered during college had higher GPAs than those who did not. The university dean argued that community service improves academic performance.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the dean's argument?

options.text: Many volunteer opportunities are available through the university.
Students with higher GPAs have more schedule flexibility and time management skills, which enable them to volunteer, rather than volunteering improving their academic performance.
Volunteering develops leadership and communication skills.
The study tracked students over four years.
Some students volunteer for resume-building purposes.

explanation: Reverse causation: good students have time to volunteer, not volunteering improving grades.

passage: A juice company claims its new vitamin-fortified juice boosts immune function because sales of the juice are highest during flu season.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the company's claim?

options.text: The juice contains vitamins C and D.
High flu-season sales reflect consumers' desire to feel proactive about health rather than evidence that the juice actually prevents illness.
The juice tastes better than competitors' products.
Sales of many health products increase during flu season.
The company also sells non-fortified juice.

explanation: Sales reflect consumer psychology, not product efficacy. Buying it doesn't prove it works.

passage: A car manufacturer's data shows that drivers of its vehicles who use the advanced driver-assistance system have 40% fewer accidents. The company claims its system makes driving significantly safer.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the company's claim?

options.text: The system includes lane departure warning and automatic emergency braking.

The assistance system is available only on the manufacturer's most expensive models, whose buyers tend to be older, more experienced drivers with clean driving records who would have fewer accidents regardless.
The system has received positive reviews from automotive journalists.
Some accidents cannot be prevented by technology.
The company invested heavily in developing the system.

explanation: Confound: expensive-car buyers are already safer drivers.

passage: A government nutrition program provides free healthy school lunches to all students in low-income districts. After five years, childhood obesity rates in participating districts have not decreased. Program supporters argue that the program needs more time to show results.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the supporters' defense?

options.text: Childhood obesity is influenced by genetics.

Students in the program consume the majority of their daily calories outside school hours from high-calorie convenience foods, and one healthy meal per day is insufficient to offset the caloric surplus from meals and snacks consumed at home and in the community.
The program costs $500 million annually.
Some students do not eat the school lunch provided.
Physical education classes have been reduced in many schools.

explanation: Mechanism failure: one healthy meal can't overcome the rest of the day's unhealthy eating. More time won't help if the fundamental problem is out-of-school eating.

passage: Behavioral economists found that employees enrolled in automatic 401(k) savings plans accumulate 80% more retirement savings over 20 years than those who must actively opt in. Policymakers propose mandating auto-enrollment for all employers to address the retirement savings crisis.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the policymakers' proposal?

options.text: Auto-enrollment typically starts at a 3% contribution rate.

Workers who benefited most from auto-enrollment in existing plans are salaried professionals at large corporations, while the workers most affected by the retirement savings crisis are hourly and gig economy workers whose irregular incomes make consistent automated deductions impractical and financially destabilizing.
Some employees opt out of auto-enrollment plans.
Auto-enrollment programs increase administrative costs for employers.
Financial literacy programs have had limited success in changing savings behavior.

explanation: Target population mismatch: the solution worked for stable-income professionals but won't help the people who actually need it most.

passage: A city installed new water filtration systems in all public schools and claims the systems provide cleaner drinking water for students.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the city's claim?

options.text: Independent testing showed lead levels in school water dropped from 15 parts per billion to below 1 part per billion after the filtration systems were installed.
The filtration systems were manufactured by a reputable company.
Some students bring water from home.
The city also tests water at residential homes.
The filtration systems require annual filter replacement.

explanation: Direct empirical evidence: independent testing confirms dramatic reduction in contaminants.

passage: A wildlife preserve claims its breeding program has helped increase the local population of endangered hawks.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the preserve's claim?

options.text: Before the breeding program began, the hawk population had been declining steadily for a decade, and since the program started, 70% of newly observed hawks in the area have identification bands from the breeding program.
Hawks are apex predators in the local ecosystem.
The preserve employs experienced wildlife biologists.
Other hawk species in the region are not endangered.
The preserve has been in operation for 25 years.

explanation: Direct evidence: banded breeding-program hawks account for most new birds, and the previous declining trend reversed.

passage: Researchers claim that a new therapy reduces chronic pain more effectively than standard treatment. Critics point out that the study was small.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the researchers' claim despite the small study size?

options.text: A subsequent large-scale multi-center trial with 2,000 patients replicated the original findings, showing statistically significant pain reduction in the therapy group compared to standard treatment.
The therapy uses a novel mechanism of action.
Chronic pain affects millions of people worldwide.
The researchers have published extensively on pain management.
The original study had a well-designed methodology.

explanation: Replication in a large trial addresses the small-sample concern directly.

passage: A company claims its employee wellness program reduces healthcare costs. Healthcare costs for participating employees are 20% lower than for non-participants.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the company's claim?

options.text: A randomized internal pilot assigned employees to the program by lottery, and those assigned to participate had 18% lower healthcare costs than those not assigned, even though both groups had identical health profiles at the start.
The wellness program includes gym access, nutrition counseling, and stress management.
Healthcare costs have been rising nationwide.
The company pays a significant portion of employee healthcare premiums.
Employee satisfaction surveys mention the wellness program positively.

explanation: Randomized assignment eliminates self-selection bias, supporting the causal claim.

passage: A new tutoring method claims to improve math skills in struggling students. Test scores improved 15% for students in the program.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the claim that the tutoring method works?

options.text: Students were randomly assigned to the new method or a control group receiving traditional tutoring with equal contact hours, and the new-method group improved 15% while the control group improved only 3%.
The tutoring method uses visual and kinesthetic learning techniques.
Students attended tutoring sessions twice per week.
The improvement was measured using state standardized tests.
Parents reported that students seemed more engaged with math.

explanation: Controlled comparison with equal contact hours isolates the method's specific effect.

passage: A pharmaceutical company claims its new blood pressure medication is effective based on clinical trial results. Critics note that the trial was conducted only at the company's own research facilities.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the company's claim despite the critics' concern?

options.text: Subsequent independent trials conducted at 15 university hospitals across 8 countries reproduced the company's findings, with consistent blood pressure reductions across diverse patient populations.
The company's research facilities are state-of-the-art.
Blood pressure medication is one of the most commonly prescribed drug types.
The FDA reviewed the trial data.
The company has produced other successful medications.

explanation: Independent replication across multiple countries and hospitals directly addresses the concern about company-only testing.

passage: A farmer claims that rotating crops between corn and soybeans improves soil health compared to planting corn continuously.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the farmer's claim?

options.text: Soil tests on the farmer's rotated fields show 40% higher nitrogen levels and 25% more organic matter than on neighboring farms that plant continuous corn on similar soil types.
Crop rotation has been practiced for centuries.
Soybeans are legumes that fix nitrogen from the atmosphere.
The farmer has been rotating crops for ten years.
Corn requires significant nitrogen fertilizer.

explanation: Direct comparative soil test data from similar conditions supports the claim.

passage: An airline claims its new pilot fatigue management program improves flight safety. Since implementing the program, the airline has had zero fatigue-related incidents.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the claim that the program specifically caused the improvement?

options.text: Before the program, the airline averaged four fatigue-related incidents per year for the previous decade, and no other changes to scheduling, aircraft, or crew policies were made during the period.
The program includes mandatory rest periods between flights.
The airline operates over 1,000 flights daily.
Other airlines have implemented similar programs.
Pilot fatigue is a recognized safety concern in aviation.

explanation: Historical baseline (4/year) + no other changes + zero incidents = strong causal evidence.

passage: Researchers claim that exposure to nature improves mental health outcomes. Critics argue that people who spend time in nature are already healthier.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the researchers' claim against the critics' objection?

options.text: A randomized experiment assigned sedentary office workers to either daily 30-minute walks in a park or equivalent walks on indoor treadmills; the park-walking group showed significantly greater reductions in anxiety and cortisol levels despite identical exercise.
Many cultures have traditions of spending time in nature.
Nature exposure has been studied for decades.
Some mental health professionals recommend spending time outdoors.
Urban parks have been shown to increase nearby property values.

explanation: Randomized + exercise controlled: the only variable is nature exposure, directly isolating its mental health effect.

passage: A school claims that its anti-bullying program has reduced bullying incidents. Reported bullying dropped 50% in the year after the program was implemented.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the claim that the program actually reduced bullying rather than just reducing reporting?

options.text: Anonymous student surveys, which track actual experiences of bullying independent of official reporting, also showed a 45% decline in bullying experiences during the same period.
The program includes peer mediation and teacher training.
The school has 500 students.
Some students may still not report bullying.
Other schools in the district did not implement anti-bullying programs.

explanation: Anonymous surveys bypass reporting biases, confirming that actual bullying decreased, not just reports.

passage: An environmental agency claims that its wetland restoration project has improved water quality in the adjacent river. Downstream pollution levels have decreased by 30%.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the agency's claim?

options.text: Upstream pollution sources have not changed, no new water treatment facilities were built, and scientific monitoring shows that the specific pollutants that decreased are precisely those that wetlands are known to filter.
Wetlands are sometimes called nature's kidneys.
The restoration project took three years to complete.
The river supports several fish species.
The agency has restored other wetlands in the region.

explanation: Rules out alternatives + pollutant-specific match with wetland filtration = strong causal evidence.

passage: A controversial study claims that a four-day workweek increases productivity. The study tracked five companies over two years.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the study's conclusion?

options.text: A subsequent government-funded trial randomly assigned 100 companies of various sizes and industries to either a four-day or five-day workweek for one year; the four-day companies showed equal or higher output per employee, lower absenteeism, and sustained results through the full year without a novelty-effect decline.
The original five companies represented different sectors.
Employee satisfaction increased at all five companies.
The four-day workweek saved companies money on utilities.
Several countries have expressed interest in four-day workweek policies.

explanation: Large-scale randomized trial, diverse companies, sustained results, no novelty decline — addresses every common objection to the original small study.

passage: A nonprofit claims its after-school reading program improves children's reading skills.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the nonprofit's claim?

options.text: Children in the program improved their reading levels by an average of 1.5 grade levels in one year, while a matched comparison group of similar children who did not participate improved by only 0.3 grade levels.
The program uses certified reading specialists.
The program operates in ten states.
Parents report that their children enjoy the program.
The program serves children in grades 1 through 5.

explanation: Controlled comparison with matched groups supports causal claim.

passage: A hospital claims that its new hand hygiene monitoring system has reduced hospital-acquired infections.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the hospital's claim?

options.text: Infection rates dropped 40% after the system was implemented, the decline began immediately after implementation and was sustained for two years, and no other infection control measures were introduced during that period.
Hand hygiene is the most important factor in preventing hospital infections.
The monitoring system uses electronic sensors.
Staff compliance with hand hygiene protocols increased from 40% to 90%.
Hospital-acquired infections are costly to treat.

explanation: Immediate and sustained decline with no other changes = strong causal evidence.

passage: Researchers claim that a new gene therapy can cure a rare blood disorder. Initial trials showed 90% of patients achieved complete remission.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the claim that the therapy is genuinely curative?

options.text: Five-year follow-up data shows that 85% of treated patients remain in complete remission with normal blood values, and genetic analysis confirms the corrected gene is stably integrated and functioning in patients' bone marrow stem cells.
The therapy was developed at a leading research university.
The blood disorder affects approximately 1 in 100,000 people.
Patients in the trial reported improved quality of life.
The therapy requires only a single treatment.

explanation: Long-term data + confirmed mechanism (stable gene integration) supports a genuine cure rather than temporary remission.

passage: A tech company claims its AI hiring tool reduces bias in recruitment by focusing only on skills and experience.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens this claim?

options.text: An independent audit comparing candidates selected by the AI tool versus human recruiters found that the AI tool selected demographically diverse candidates at statistically equal rates across all groups, while human recruiters showed measurable disparities favoring certain demographic groups.
The AI tool was trained on a large dataset.
The company employs a diverse workforce.
The tool analyzes resumes in under 10 seconds.
Several Fortune 500 companies use similar tools.

explanation: Independent audit with comparative data directly confirms reduced bias versus the human baseline.

passage: A gardener claims that adding compost to her vegetable garden increases yield compared to using chemical fertilizer alone.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the gardener's claim?

options.text: She divided her garden into identical plots, applied compost to half and chemical fertilizer to the other half, and the compost plots produced 30% more vegetables over three growing seasons.
Compost is made from decomposed organic matter.
Many gardening books recommend using compost.
Chemical fertilizers can be harmful to soil organisms.
Compost improves soil structure and water retention.

explanation: Controlled comparison on identical plots over multiple seasons directly supports the causal claim.

passage: A driving school claims that its graduates have fewer accidents in their first year of driving than the general population of new drivers.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the school's claim?

options.text: When compared to new drivers of the same age, gender, and region who attended other driving schools, the school's graduates had 35% fewer accidents in their first year.
The driving school has been in business for 20 years.
The school's instructors are certified by the state.
New drivers generally have higher accident rates than experienced drivers.
The school offers both classroom and behind-the-wheel instruction.

explanation: Controlled comparison against similar new drivers from other schools isolates the school's specific contribution.

passage: A city claims that its new traffic signal coordination system has reduced commute times on Main Street.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the city's claim?

options.text: Average commute times on Main Street decreased by 18% after the system was activated, while commute times on parallel streets without the system remained unchanged, and no road construction or lane changes occurred during the measurement period.
The system uses advanced AI algorithms.
Commuters have expressed appreciation for the shorter travel times.
Traffic signal coordination has been used in other cities.
The system cost $2 million to implement.

explanation: Decline on Main Street + no change on parallel streets + no other changes = the system caused the improvement.

passage: A coffee chain claims that switching to ethically sourced beans has not affected the taste quality of its beverages, despite skeptics who say ethical sourcing requires compromising on flavor.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the coffee chain's claim?

options.text: Double-blind taste tests with professional coffee tasters and regular consumers found no statistically significant difference in flavor ratings between the old and new bean sources.
The company pays farmers a premium for ethically sourced beans.
Sales have remained stable since the switch.
The company displays its ethical sourcing certification in stores.
Ethical sourcing is becoming an industry standard.

explanation: Blind taste tests with both experts and consumers directly test the taste quality claim.

passage: A prison reform organization claims that its education program reduces recidivism among inmates.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the organization's claim?

options.text: A randomized study assigned eligible inmates to the education program or a waitlist control group with identical sentence lengths and offense types, and three-year follow-up showed the education group had a recidivism rate of 20% compared to 45% for the control group.
The program offers GED preparation and vocational training.
Participants in the program reported feeling more optimistic about their futures.
The organization has been operating for 15 years.
Other states have implemented similar programs.

explanation: Randomized controlled study with matched groups and long follow-up is the gold standard for causal evidence.

passage: A shoe company claims its new running shoe reduces knee strain during exercise.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the company's claim?

options.text: Biomechanical studies using motion capture and force plates showed that runners wearing the new shoe experienced 25% less impact force on the knee joint compared to runners wearing the previous model, with no changes to running form or speed.
The shoe features a new cushioning material.
Professional athletes have endorsed the shoe.
The shoe is available in multiple colors.
Running is one of the most popular forms of exercise.

explanation: Objective biomechanical measurement directly quantifies the claimed benefit under controlled conditions.

passage: Researchers claim that a new water purification technology removes 99.5% of microplastics from drinking water.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the researchers' claim?

options.text: Laboratory tests by three independent universities using water samples contaminated with known concentrations of various microplastic types confirmed removal rates between 99.3% and 99.7% across all tests.
Microplastics have been found in drinking water worldwide.
The technology uses a novel filtration membrane.
Current water treatment plants do not effectively remove microplastics.
The World Health Organization has expressed concern about microplastics in water.

explanation: Independent replication by multiple labs with diverse microplastic types confirms the claim robustly.

passage: A tech company claims that its new battery technology can safely power electric vehicles for 500,000 miles without significant degradation.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the company's claim?

options.text: Accelerated aging tests simulating 500,000 miles of real-world driving conditions, validated by three independent automotive testing organizations, showed the battery retaining 85% of its original capacity, consistent with the company's claims.
The battery uses a solid-state electrolyte.
Current EV batteries typically last 200,000 to 300,000 miles.
The company holds 50 patents related to the technology.
Several major automakers have expressed interest in the technology.

explanation: Independent validation of accelerated aging tests under realistic conditions directly supports the durability claim.

passage: A local bakery claims that its sourdough bread stays fresh longer than commercial bread.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the bakery's claim?

options.text: Side-by-side tests showed that the bakery's sourdough remained mold-free and palatable for 7 days at room temperature, while commercial bread from three major brands showed mold growth by day 4 under identical conditions.
Sourdough bread has been made for thousands of years.
The bakery uses a natural starter culture.
Commercial bread contains preservatives.
Customers report enjoying the taste of the sourdough.

explanation: Controlled comparison under identical conditions directly tests the freshness claim.

passage: A city's parks department claims that its new playground design reduces childhood injuries compared to the old design.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the department's claim?

options.text: Injury reports at parks with the new playground design show a 55% reduction in injuries per 1,000 visits compared to parks that still have the old design, and the parks have similar visit volumes, demographics, and supervision levels.
The new design uses rubberized surfacing instead of wood chips.
Parents prefer the look of the new playgrounds.
The new playgrounds cost 30% more to build.
The department consulted child safety experts during the design process.

explanation: Controlled comparison with similar usage patterns and demographics isolates the design's effect on injuries.

passage: A biotech company claims its genetically modified crop variety requires 50% less water than conventional varieties while maintaining equivalent yields.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the company's claim?

options.text: Multi-year field trials across diverse climate zones, conducted by both company and independent agricultural researchers, consistently showed the GM variety producing equivalent yields at 50% irrigation levels where conventional varieties experienced significant yield losses.
The modification targets a gene involved in drought tolerance.
Water scarcity is a growing concern for global agriculture.
The GM variety has received regulatory approval in multiple countries.
Some farmers have expressed interest in the new variety.

explanation: Multi-year, multi-site, independent replication across diverse conditions provides the strongest possible field evidence.

passage: A paint company claims its new exterior paint lasts 15 years without peeling or fading.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the company's claim?

options.text: Accelerated weathering tests equivalent to 15 years of sun, rain, and temperature extremes showed no significant peeling or fading, and homes painted with the product 12 years ago in a real-world pilot show paint that remains intact and vibrant.
The paint uses a new polymer formulation.
The paint is available in 200 colors.
Professional painters recommend the product.
The paint costs more than standard exterior paints.

explanation: Both lab simulation and real-world long-term evidence support the 15-year claim.

passage: A hospital claims that its new surgical technique for knee replacement results in faster patient recovery.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the hospital's claim?

options.text: Patients who underwent the new technique were walking independently an average of 2 days after surgery compared to 5 days for the traditional technique, in a study where patients were randomly assigned to either technique and both groups had similar pre-operative health profiles.
The new technique is minimally invasive.
Patient satisfaction scores are high for both techniques.
The surgeon who developed the technique has 20 years of experience.
Recovery time is an important factor for patients.

explanation: Randomized assignment with similar health profiles directly supports the claim that the technique, not patient characteristics, caused faster recovery.

passage: A controversial study claims that intermittent fasting produces metabolic benefits beyond those achievable through equivalent caloric restriction alone. Critics argue that any benefits are simply due to eating less.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the study's claim against the critics' argument?

options.text: A follow-up trial gave one group an intermittent fasting schedule and another group the same total daily calories spread across regular meals; the fasting group showed significantly greater improvements in insulin sensitivity, cellular autophagy markers, and inflammatory biomarkers despite identical caloric intake.
Intermittent fasting has been practiced in various cultures for centuries.
The original study was published in a top-tier journal.
Some participants found intermittent fasting difficult to maintain.
Animal studies have shown longevity benefits from caloric restriction.

explanation: Same calories, different eating pattern, different metabolic outcomes — directly isolates the fasting pattern's independent effect beyond calorie reduction.

passage: A charity claims that providing mosquito nets to families in malaria-endemic regions saves lives.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the charity's claim?

options.text: A randomized distribution program in 200 villages showed that villages receiving nets had 44% fewer malaria deaths among children under five compared to villages that did not, and the effect was consistent across different geographic and demographic conditions.
Mosquito nets are inexpensive to produce.
Malaria kills over 400,000 people annually.
The nets are treated with insecticide.
International health organizations support net distribution.

explanation: Randomized village-level study with consistent results across conditions provides strong causal evidence.

passage: A logistics company claims its route optimization software reduces fuel consumption for delivery fleets.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the company's claim?

options.text: Delivery fleets that adopted the software saw a 22% reduction in fuel consumption in the first six months, while control fleets with the same vehicle types, delivery volumes, and geographic areas that did not adopt the software showed no change in fuel consumption.
The software uses machine learning algorithms.
Fuel costs represent a significant portion of logistics expenses.
The company has won industry innovation awards.
GPS tracking has become standard in the logistics industry.

explanation: Controlled comparison with matched fleets shows the software's specific effect on fuel consumption.

passage: A sleep researcher claims that blue-light-blocking glasses improve sleep quality when worn in the evening.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the researcher's claim?

options.text: A double-blind crossover study had participants wear either genuine blue-light-blocking glasses or visually identical sham glasses with no blue-light filtering for two weeks each; sleep quality metrics including time to fall asleep, sleep duration, and melatonin levels were significantly better during the genuine-glasses period.
Blue light from screens suppresses melatonin production.
Many technology companies now offer blue-light filtering settings on devices.
Participants reported subjective improvement in sleep quality.
Poor sleep quality affects millions of adults worldwide.

explanation: Double-blind crossover with sham control is the gold standard: eliminates placebo effect, individual differences, and expectation bias.

passage: A nonprofit claims that its financial literacy program helps low-income families build savings.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the nonprofit's claim?

options.text: Families who completed the program increased their savings by an average of $1,200 over one year, compared to $150 for matched families who did not participate, and the program did not provide any direct financial assistance.
The program covers budgeting, debt management, and investing.
Low-income families often lack access to financial education.
The program operates in 20 cities.
Participants receive a certificate upon completion.

explanation: Matched comparison + no direct financial aid = the education itself drove the savings increase.

passage: A company claims its new air purifier effectively removes allergens from indoor air.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the company's claim?

options.text: Independent testing in sealed rooms showed the purifier reduced airborne allergen particles by 98% within two hours, and allergy sufferers in a clinical trial reported a 60% reduction in symptoms when using the purifier compared to a sham device.
The purifier uses HEPA filtration technology.
Indoor air quality is a growing health concern.
The purifier is Energy Star certified.
Allergies affect approximately 50 million Americans.

explanation: Both lab performance data and clinical symptom improvement from a blinded trial provide strong converging evidence.

passage: A restaurant owner plans to boost weekend revenue by offering live music on Friday and Saturday nights, since a nearby restaurant that offers live music has long wait times on weekends.
questionStem: Which of the following is an assumption on which the owner's plan depends?

options.text: Live musicians are available for hire on weekends.
The nearby restaurant's popularity on weekends is at least partly attributable to its live music rather than to other factors such as its food quality, location, or reputation.
The owner has a personal interest in music.
Weekend evenings are the busiest time for restaurants.
The restaurant has adequate space for a music setup.

explanation: The plan assumes the neighbor's success is caused by the music, not other factors.

passage: A company plans to reduce employee turnover by increasing salaries by 10%, reasoning that employees leave primarily because of inadequate pay.
questionStem: Which of the following is an assumption necessary for the plan to succeed?

options.text: The company can afford the 10% salary increase.
Inadequate pay is indeed a primary reason employees leave, rather than factors like poor management, lack of growth opportunities, or work-life balance issues that a pay increase would not address.
Employee turnover is costly for the company.
The company's competitors pay higher salaries.
A 10% increase is significant enough to affect retention.

explanation: If employees leave for non-monetary reasons, raising pay won't help.

passage: A city installs new LED traffic signals expecting to reduce accidents at intersections, since LED signals are brighter and more visible than the old incandescent ones.
questionStem: Which of the following is an assumption on which the city's expectation depends?

options.text: LED signals use less electricity than incandescent signals.

A significant number of intersection accidents are caused by drivers failing to see the traffic signals, rather than by drivers who see the signals but deliberately run them or by other factors unrelated to signal visibility.
LED technology has improved significantly in recent years.
The city has many busy intersections.
The old incandescent signals were functioning properly.

explanation: If accidents aren't caused by poor visibility, brighter signals won't help.

passage: An airline plans to improve on-time performance by purchasing newer aircraft, since newer planes require less maintenance and experience fewer mechanical delays.
questionStem: Which of the following is an assumption required by the airline's plan?

options.text: Newer aircraft are more fuel-efficient than older ones.

Mechanical issues are a significant cause of the airline's current delays, rather than delays being caused primarily by factors such as air traffic congestion, weather, crew scheduling, or gate availability.
The airline can afford to purchase new aircraft.
Passengers prefer flying on newer aircraft.
The airline's competitors use newer fleets.

explanation: If delays are mostly from ATC, weather, or scheduling, new planes won't improve on-time performance.

passage: A publisher plans to increase book sales by releasing all new titles simultaneously in print and audiobook formats, since the audiobook market has been growing 25% annually.
questionStem: Which of the following is an assumption on which the publisher's plan depends?

options.text: Audiobook production costs are manageable for the publisher.
Audiobook sales will represent genuinely new purchases rather than simply cannibalizing sales from readers who would have purchased the print version.
The publisher has the technical capability to produce audiobooks.
Audiobooks are popular among commuters.
The publisher currently releases only print editions.

explanation: If audiobook buyers would have bought print anyway, total sales don't increase.

passage: A school board proposes reducing class sizes from 30 to 20 students to improve learning outcomes, based on research showing that smaller classes lead to better student performance.
questionStem: Which of the following is an assumption necessary for the plan to achieve its goal?

options.text: Smaller class sizes are preferred by both teachers and parents.
Sufficient qualified teachers are available to staff the additional classrooms needed, and these teachers will be of comparable quality to the existing teaching staff.
The school district has adequate classroom space.
Students in smaller classes have fewer behavioral issues.
Research on class size has been conducted in various educational contexts.

explanation: If you can't hire enough good teachers, the additional classes may have inexperienced or unqualified instructors, negating the benefits.

passage: A tech startup argues that its product will succeed in the Asian market because it has been successful in the European market.
questionStem: Which of the following is an assumption required by the startup's argument?

options.text: The Asian market is larger than the European market.

Consumer preferences, technology infrastructure, and competitive landscapes in Asian target markets are similar enough to European markets that the product's European success is predictive of Asian success.
The startup has employees who speak Asian languages.
Asian consumers are interested in technology products.
The European launch was well-funded.

explanation: Market transferability assumption: what works in Europe may not work in Asia if conditions differ.

passage: A policy institute argues that raising the legal smoking age from 18 to 21 will significantly reduce the number of people who become lifelong smokers, since most lifelong smokers begin before age 21.
questionStem: Which of the following is an assumption on which the institute's argument depends?

options.text: Smoking is the leading cause of preventable death.

Raising the legal age will actually prevent a significant number of 18-20-year-olds from obtaining and using cigarettes, rather than those individuals simply acquiring cigarettes through illegal channels or from older friends.
The policy has been implemented in some jurisdictions.
Young adults are particularly susceptible to nicotine addiction.
Public support for raising the smoking age is growing.

explanation: If underage individuals easily circumvent the law, raising the legal age won't reduce actual smoking.

passage: A financial advisor argues that her client should invest in index funds rather than actively managed funds because index funds have outperformed 85% of actively managed funds over the past 20 years.
questionStem: Which of the following is an assumption necessary for the advisor's argument?

options.text: Index funds have lower management fees than actively managed funds.

The historical pattern of index funds outperforming most actively managed funds will continue in the future, and the client's specific financial situation, risk tolerance, and investment timeline are compatible with index fund investing.
The client has sufficient savings to invest.
Index fund investing requires less knowledge than active management.
Past performance of investments is well-documented.

explanation: Two required assumptions: (1) past patterns predict future results and (2) index funds suit this client's specific needs.

passage: A city transit authority plans to increase bus ridership by making all bus routes free of charge, citing a study showing that price is the primary barrier to transit use in the city.
questionStem: Which of the following is an assumption required by the authority's plan?

options.text: The city has sufficient budget to absorb the lost fare revenue.
Eliminating fares will not cause overcrowding, service degradation, or safety concerns that would deter current and potential riders from using the bus system.
Free public transit has been tried in other cities.
Bus routes cover most of the city's neighborhoods.
Many residents currently drive to work.

explanation: If free buses become overcrowded or unsafe, ridership may not increase or may even decrease. The plan assumes service quality will be maintained.

passage: A pet store owner decides to stock only grain-free dog food because several customers have requested it, concluding that grain-free food is what most customers want.
questionStem: Which of the following is an assumption on which the owner's conclusion depends?

options.text: Grain-free dog food is more expensive than regular dog food.
The few customers who made requests are representative of the broader customer base rather than being a vocal minority with unusual preferences.
Grain-free diets are recommended by some veterinarians.
The store currently stocks both grain-free and regular dog food.
Grain-free dog food is available from multiple manufacturers.

explanation: A few vocal requests may not represent the majority. Stocking only grain-free could alienate most customers.

passage: A company plans to improve cybersecurity by requiring all employees to change their passwords every 30 days, since security experts have traditionally recommended frequent password changes.
questionStem: Which of the following is an assumption on which the plan depends?

options.text: Employees can remember new passwords every 30 days.
Frequent password changes will actually improve security rather than causing employees to choose weaker, easier-to-remember passwords or write passwords down where they can be discovered.
Cyber attacks are a growing threat to businesses.
The company has experienced a security breach in the past.
Password management software is available.

explanation: If frequent changes lead to weaker passwords, the policy could actually worsen security.

passage: A university president argues that eliminating the standardized test requirement for admissions will increase socioeconomic diversity in the student body, since standardized test scores correlate strongly with family income.
questionStem: Which of the following is an assumption required by the president's argument?

options.text: Standardized tests are biased against lower-income students.

Removing the test requirement will not cause admissions decisions to rely more heavily on other criteria that are equally or more correlated with socioeconomic status, such as extracurricular activities, legacy status, or personal essays reflecting privileged experiences.
The university currently has limited socioeconomic diversity.
Many universities have adopted test-optional policies.
Lower-income students are less likely to apply to the university.

explanation: If other admission criteria are equally income-dependent, removing tests may not help diversity at all.

passage: A climate scientist argues that planting one trillion trees globally would sequester enough carbon dioxide to significantly offset human emissions for the next century, based on calculations of carbon absorption rates per tree.
questionStem: Which of the following is an assumption required by the scientist's argument?

options.text: Reforestation projects have been successful in many countries.

The planted trees will survive, grow to maturity, and remain standing for the century-long period, and the land used for planting will not be needed for food production, urban development, or other purposes that would require removing the trees.
Trees absorb carbon dioxide through photosynthesis.
One trillion trees would cover a vast area.
Climate change is caused primarily by carbon dioxide emissions.

explanation: Long-term survival + land availability: if trees die, burn, or are cleared, the sequestration fails. Land-use competition is critical.

passage: A music streaming service plans to attract more subscribers by offering an exclusive podcast library, since podcast listening has grown 50% over the past two years.
questionStem: Which of the following is an assumption on which the plan depends?

options.text: Podcasts are popular among younger demographics.
People who want to listen to podcasts will choose to pay for this streaming service rather than accessing the abundant free podcasts available on competing platforms.
The streaming service currently has a large music library.
Podcast production costs are manageable.
The streaming market is competitive.

explanation: If free podcasts are widely available elsewhere, exclusive content on a paid platform may not attract subscribers.

passage: A city council plans to reduce traffic congestion by converting two major roads into one-way streets. Traffic engineers estimate that average commute times on these roads will decrease by 15 percent. The council therefore concludes that overall traffic congestion in the city will be reduced.
questionStem: The council's conclusion depends on which of the following assumptions?

options.text: The roads being converted currently carry the majority of the city's traffic.
Drivers who currently use the two roads will not shift to alternative routes in numbers sufficient to create new congestion elsewhere.
One-way streets are generally safer than two-way streets for both drivers and pedestrians.
The cost of converting the roads will be recovered within five years through increased economic productivity.
Public transportation ridership will not decrease as a result of the changes.

explanation: The argument assumes that reducing congestion on two roads leads to reduced overall congestion. If displaced drivers clog other routes, total congestion may not decrease. (B) identifies this necessary assumption. (A) is not required—even minority-traffic roads could help if no displacement occurs. (C), (D), and (E) address safety, cost, and transit use, which are irrelevant to the congestion conclusion.

passage: A restaurant chain claims that switching from plastic straws to paper straws will significantly reduce its environmental impact. The chain serves approximately 50 million drinks per year.
questionStem: The argument above assumes which of the following?

options.text: Paper straws cost more than plastic straws to manufacture.
The environmental benefit of using paper straws is not offset by greater environmental costs in their production or disposal.
Customers prefer paper straws to plastic straws.
Other restaurant chains have already made a similar switch.
The chain will not reduce the total number of drinks it serves.

explanation: If paper straws cause equal or greater environmental harm during production or disposal, the switch would not reduce impact. The argument assumes (B). (A) addresses cost, not environmental impact. (C) addresses preference, not impact. (D) and (E) are irrelevant to the environmental claim.

passage: Researchers found that students who participate in extracurricular debate programs score, on average, 12 percent higher on standardized reading comprehension tests than students who do not. The school board cited this finding in proposing mandatory debate participation for all students as a means of improving reading scores district-wide.
questionStem: The school board's proposal depends on which of the following assumptions?

options.text: Debate participation causes improvement in reading comprehension rather than merely correlating with pre-existing reading ability.
Students in debate programs spend less time on other extracurricular activities.
The standardized tests accurately measure all aspects of reading ability.
Debate coaches receive specialized training in reading instruction.
Students who currently participate in debate are representative of the student body as a whole in terms of socioeconomic background.

explanation: The board infers causation from correlation. If stronger readers simply choose debate, mandatory participation would not boost scores. (A) is the necessary assumption. (B) is irrelevant to reading improvement. (C) questions test validity but the board relies on these tests regardless. (D) is not required. (E) addresses demographics but not the causal mechanism.

passage: A software company found that employees who work remotely at least three days a week report 20 percent higher job satisfaction than those who work entirely in the office. To improve overall employee satisfaction, the company plans to require all employees to work remotely at least three days per week.
questionStem: Which of the following is an assumption on which the plan depends?

options.text: Remote work does not reduce collaboration among team members.
The higher satisfaction of current remote workers is not primarily due to the voluntary nature of their remote arrangement.
All employees have adequate home office setups for remote work.
The company's competitors also offer remote work options.
Job satisfaction is the most important factor in employee retention.

explanation: If satisfaction stems from having chosen to work remotely rather than from remote work itself, making it mandatory would not replicate the effect. (B) is essential. (A) addresses collaboration, not satisfaction. (C) is a practical concern but not an assumption about satisfaction outcomes. (D) and (E) are irrelevant to the plan's logic.

passage: Pharmaceutical company executives argue that high drug prices in the United States are necessary to fund research and development of new treatments. They note that R&D spending has increased by 40 percent over the past decade while the number of new drugs approved annually has remained roughly constant.
questionStem: The executives' argument assumes which of the following?

options.text: The cost of conducting clinical trials has not increased over the past decade.
Drug prices in countries other than the United States have not increased.
Without revenue from high U.S. drug prices, the current level of R&D spending could not be maintained through other means.
The drugs approved in recent years are not more complex or costly to develop than those approved a decade ago.
Consumers in the United States use more prescription drugs per capita than consumers in other countries.

explanation: The executives claim high prices are 'necessary' for R&D. This requires assuming no alternative funding source exists. (C) captures this. (A) and (D) address R&D cost trends but the argument is about funding sources, not efficiency. (B) and (E) concern international comparisons irrelevant to the necessity claim.

passage: A survey of 1,000 households found that 60 percent own at least one pet. Of pet-owning households, 70 percent own a dog, 45 percent own a cat, and 20 percent own both a dog and a cat.
questionStem: If the survey results are accurate, which of the following must be true?

options.text: More households own only a dog than own only a cat.
At least 100 households own neither a dog nor a cat but own some other pet.
Some pet-owning households own a pet that is neither a dog nor a cat.
Most households that own a cat also own a dog.
The number of households owning a dog exceeds the total number of households not owning any pet.

explanation: Of 600 pet-owning households: dog-only = 70% - 20% = 50% (300 households), cat-only = 45% - 20% = 25% (150 households). Dog-only (300) exceeds cat-only (150), so (A) must be true. (B) cannot be confirmed—the 5% remainder could own only both-category overlaps. (C) is likely but not proven: dog+cat coverage is 95%, the other 5% (30 households) could own other pets or be a rounding artifact. (D) is false: only 20/45 of cat owners also own dogs, less than half. (E) compares 420 dog owners to 400 non-pet owners—close but true; however (A) is more directly supported.

passage: A factory produces widgets in three shifts. The morning shift produces 40 percent of daily output with a 2 percent defect rate. The afternoon shift produces 35 percent with a 3 percent defect rate. The night shift produces the remaining output with a 5 percent defect rate.
questionStem: If the above information is accurate, which of the following can be properly inferred?

options.text: The night shift produces fewer widgets than either of the other shifts.
The majority of defective widgets are produced during the night shift.
If a randomly selected widget is defective, it is most likely from the night shift.
The overall defect rate for the factory is less than 4 percent.
Reducing the night shift defect rate would have the greatest impact on overall quality.

explanation: Night shift = 25% of output. Overall defect rate = 0.40(0.02) + 0.35(0.03) + 0.25(0.05) = 0.008 + 0.0105 + 0.0125 = 0.031, or 3.1%, which is less than 4%. (D) must be true. (A) is true but (D) is also valid. (B) is false: night produces 0.0125 of defects vs morning 0.008 and afternoon 0.0105—night is highest but not a majority. (C) requires Bayesian calculation; night contributes 0.0125/0.031 = ~40%, not a majority. (E) is not necessarily true without comparing marginal impacts.

passage: All of the company's senior managers have MBAs. Some senior managers also hold engineering degrees. No employee without an MBA has been promoted to senior management in the past five years. Recently, three engineers without MBAs were hired into middle management positions.
questionStem: If the statements above are true, which of the following must also be true?

options.text: The three recently hired engineers will not be promoted to senior management.
No current senior manager was originally hired as an engineer.
If the three engineers obtain MBAs, they will be promoted to senior management.
The three recently hired engineers cannot be promoted to senior management without first obtaining MBAs.
Most senior managers were promoted from middle management.

explanation: The passage states no one without an MBA has been promoted to senior management in five years, and all senior managers hold MBAs. The three engineers lack MBAs. (D) follows: they cannot be promoted without MBAs. (A) is too strong—they could obtain MBAs. (B) is unsupported—some senior managers hold engineering degrees. (C) is too strong—an MBA is necessary but not sufficient. (E) is not stated.

passage: A bookstore's records show that in January, mystery novels accounted for 30 percent of all sales, romance novels for 25 percent, and science fiction for 15 percent. The remaining sales were distributed among all other genres.
questionStem: Which of the following can be properly concluded from the information above?

options.text: Mystery novels are the most popular genre at the bookstore year-round.
In January, more than half of the bookstore's sales were not from mystery, romance, or science fiction.
The combined sales of mystery and romance novels exceeded the sales of all other genres combined in January.
Science fiction novels are the least popular genre at the bookstore.
In January, the three listed genres together accounted for more than two-thirds of all sales.

explanation: Mystery + romance = 55%, all other genres = 45%. So (C) is true. (A) extrapolates beyond January. (B) is false—other genres = 30% which is less than half. (D) cannot be concluded; 15% is the total for sci-fi but individual other genres could be smaller. (E) states >66.7%, but actual is 70%, so (E) is also true. However (C) is the most directly supported answer.

passage: A pharmaceutical company argues that its new drug for migraines is more effective than existing treatments because in a clinical trial, 65 percent of patients receiving the new drug reported significant pain reduction within two hours, compared to 45 percent of patients receiving a placebo.
questionStem: Which of the following would be most useful to know in evaluating the company's claim that its drug is more effective than existing treatments?

options.text: Whether the clinical trial was funded by the pharmaceutical company itself.
What percentage of patients using existing migraine treatments report significant pain reduction within two hours.
Whether patients in the trial had previously tried other migraine medications.
How many total patients participated in the clinical trial.
Whether the new drug has any side effects not present in existing treatments.

explanation: The company claims superiority over existing treatments but only compared to placebo. To evaluate this claim, we need to know how existing treatments perform. (B) provides this crucial comparison. (A) addresses bias but not effectiveness comparison. (C) is relevant background but does not directly evaluate relative effectiveness. (D) addresses statistical power but the result is already stated. (E) concerns safety, not effectiveness.
