Some environmentalists prefer cap-and-trade because it sets a firm limit on total emissions.
Carbon taxes are simpler to administer than cap-and-trade programs.
Several countries have implemented carbon taxes in recent years.
Cap-and-trade programs allow companies to trade emission permits on open markets.

explanation: Direct evidence showing carbon taxes produce more consistent clean energy investment while cap-and-trade investment fluctuates supports the stability argument. (A) strengthens.

passage: A corporate wellness study found that employees who participated in the company's meditation program missed 30 percent fewer sick days than non-participants. The company's HR department concluded that the meditation program improves employee health.
questionStem: Which of the following would be most useful in evaluating HR's conclusion?

options.text: Whether employees who chose to participate in the meditation program were already healthier and more health-conscious than those who did not, prior to the program's introduction.
How many minutes of meditation each session included.
Whether the meditation program was held during or after work hours.
How much the meditation program cost the company.
Whether participants also reported lower stress levels.

explanation: Self-selection into the program by already-healthier employees would explain the difference without the program being effective. (A) evaluates this critical confound.

passage: A town council votes to build a new public swimming pool, arguing that it will improve residents' physical fitness because swimming is an excellent form of exercise.
questionStem: The council's argument assumes which of the following?

options.text: A significant number of residents will actually use the new pool regularly for exercise.
Swimming is the best form of exercise available.
The town does not currently have any fitness facilities.
Building the pool will not increase property taxes.
Residents have been requesting a public pool for years.

explanation: A pool improves fitness only if residents use it. If the pool sits empty, no fitness benefit occurs. (A) is the necessary assumption.

passage: A study found that countries with more democratic institutions have higher levels of economic development. The researchers concluded that democracy promotes economic growth and that developing countries should therefore adopt democratic reforms to accelerate their economic development.
questionStem: If the statements above are true, which of the following must also be true?

options.text: It does not consider the possibility that economic development promotes democratic institutions rather than the reverse, or that both are caused by a third factor such as strong rule of law and institutional quality.
It fails to define what constitutes a 'democratic institution.'
It does not specify which democratic reforms developing countries should adopt.
It assumes all developing countries face the same economic challenges.
It ignores examples of economically successful non-democratic countries.

explanation: The causal direction may be reversed (prosperity enables democracy) or both may be driven by institutional quality. (A) captures this bidirectionality and omitted-variable problem. (E) is also relevant but (A) is the core logical flaw.

passage: Public health officials recommend vaccination against influenza each year. They argue that widespread vaccination reduces not only individual risk but also protects vulnerable populations who cannot be vaccinated through herd immunity.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, is an assumption underlying the officials' argument about herd immunity?

options.text: In communities where flu vaccination rates exceeded 70 percent, hospitalization rates among unvaccinated elderly residents were 60 percent lower than in communities with vaccination rates below 40 percent.
The influenza vaccine must be reformulated each year to match circulating virus strains.
Some people experience mild side effects after receiving the flu vaccine.
Flu vaccination is free for most residents through government-funded programs.
Influenza viruses mutate more rapidly than many other viruses.

explanation: Direct evidence showing high community vaccination rates protect unvaccinated vulnerable people confirms the herd immunity mechanism. (A) strongly supports the argument.

passage: A school introduced a policy requiring students to wear uniforms. Disciplinary incidents at the school increased in the month following the policy's implementation.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most helps to explain the increase in disciplinary incidents?

options.text: The increase consisted almost entirely of infractions for violating the new uniform policy, such as wearing non-compliant clothing or accessories, which were not possible offenses before the policy existed.
Student attendance rates remained the same after the uniform policy.
Parents were divided in their opinions about the uniform policy.
The uniforms were available at affordable prices from multiple retailers.
Teachers received training on how to enforce the new policy.

explanation: New rules create new ways to break rules. The disciplinary increase reflects uniform violations—a new category of infraction—not worsening behavior. (A) explains the paradox.

passage: A technology company's internal data shows that teams using agile methodology deliver projects 20 percent faster than teams using traditional waterfall methodology. However, the data also shows that agile teams spend 30 percent more time in meetings than waterfall teams.
questionStem: Which of the following can be most reasonably inferred from the data?

options.text: Agile methodology is superior to waterfall methodology in all respects.
The time agile teams invest in additional meetings is more than compensated for by efficiencies gained elsewhere in the project lifecycle.
Waterfall teams should adopt agile methodology immediately.
The quality of agile team deliverables is higher than that of waterfall teams.
Meeting time has no effect on project delivery speed.

explanation: Agile teams meet more but finish faster. The extra meeting time must be offset by greater efficiencies in other areas (fewer rework cycles, faster decisions). (B) is the reasonable inference. (E) is contradicted by the data pattern.

passage: Health policy advocate: Scandinavian countries have universal healthcare systems and also have some of the highest life expectancies in the world. The United States, which lacks universal healthcare, has a lower life expectancy. Clearly, implementing universal healthcare in the United States would increase American life expectancy.
questionStem: If the statements above are true, which of the following must also be true?

options.text: It assumes that the healthcare system is the primary driver of life expectancy differences between these countries, ignoring other significant factors such as diet, exercise habits, income inequality, social safety nets, and cultural differences that independently affect health outcomes.
It does not specify which model of universal healthcare should be adopted.
It fails to account for the cost of implementing universal healthcare.
It assumes that all Scandinavian countries have identical healthcare systems.
It does not consider that life expectancy has been increasing in the United States over time.

explanation: Life expectancy differences between nations are driven by many factors. Attributing the gap to healthcare alone is a multivariate fallacy. (A) identifies this.

passage: A pet adoption agency reports that adopted pets have fewer behavioral problems than pets purchased from breeders. The agency argues that adoption is better for both the pet and the owner.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, is an assumption underlying the agency's claim about behavioral problems?

options.text: Adoption agencies typically screen pets for behavioral issues before placing them and provide post-adoption behavioral support, resulting in well-matched placements.
Adopted pets are usually less expensive to acquire than pets from breeders.
Some breeders also screen their animals for behavioral problems.
Pet owners who adopt tend to have more experience with animals.
The agency has been in operation for over 20 years.

explanation: Screening and support explain why adopted pets have fewer problems, strengthening the claim that the adoption process itself produces better outcomes. (A) strengthens. (D) would suggest the difference is about owners, not the adoption process.

passage: A city's transportation department proposes building a new highway bypass to reduce downtown congestion. **Traffic studies show that the current highway through downtown carries 40 percent more vehicles daily than it was designed to handle.** Opponents argue the bypass will encourage urban sprawl. **However, traffic modeling indicates that the bypass would reduce downtown congestion by 30 percent, significantly improving air quality and commute times for the 200,000 daily commuters affected.**
questionStem: The two boldface portions play which of the following roles?

options.text: The first provides evidence supporting the need for the bypass; the second provides projected benefits to counter the opponents' objection.
The first is the argument's conclusion; the second is evidence supporting it.
The first and second are both objections to the bypass proposal.
The first provides evidence the argument seeks to undermine; the second presents an alternative.
The first is a concession to opponents; the second is the main argument.

explanation: The first shows the congestion problem (evidence of need). The second counters the sprawl objection with projected congestion and quality-of-life benefits. (A) is correct.

passage: Every successful entrepreneur I have interviewed said they wake up before 6 AM. Therefore, waking up early is the key to entrepreneurial success.
questionStem: If the statements above are true, which of the following must also be true?

options.text: confuses a common trait among successful entrepreneurs with a cause of their success, and ignores unsuccessful entrepreneurs who may also wake up early.
does not specify how many entrepreneurs were interviewed.
fails to consider different time zones.
assumes all entrepreneurs have the same daily schedule.
does not account for entrepreneurs who work primarily in the evening.

explanation: Survivorship bias: only successful entrepreneurs were surveyed. Early risers who failed are not counted. Also, correlation is not causation. (A) captures this.

passage: A government agency claims that its cybersecurity training program for employees has been effective because the number of successful phishing attacks against agency staff decreased by 50 percent in the year after the training was implemented.
questionStem: Which of the following would be most critical in evaluating whether the training caused the decrease?

options.text: Whether the agency also implemented new technical safeguards, such as improved email filters, during the same period that could independently account for the reduction in successful phishing attacks.
Whether employees found the training sessions engaging.
How much the training program cost to develop and implement.
Whether other government agencies experienced similar decreases without implementing training.
How many employees completed the training program.

explanation: If new email filters blocked phishing attempts, fewer reached employees regardless of training. (A) identifies the critical confound. (D) is also useful but (A) is more directly relevant to this specific agency's claim.

passage: Many people believe that multitasking makes them more productive. However, cognitive science research consistently shows that the human brain cannot truly perform two attention-demanding tasks simultaneously; instead, it rapidly switches between tasks, losing efficiency with each switch. This research suggests that people who believe they are effectively multitasking are actually ________.
questionStem: Which of the following most logically completes the argument?

options.text: more intelligent than average.
performing each individual task less efficiently than they would if they focused on one task at a time.
unaware that task-switching is occurring.
unable to complete any of their tasks.
more stressed than people who do not multitask.

explanation: Each switch loses efficiency. Therefore multitaskers do each task less efficiently than they would with single focus. (B) logically follows.

passage: A technology company argues that its AI-generated art tool does not infringe on artists' copyrights because the tool creates entirely new images that do not reproduce any specific existing artwork.
questionStem: The company's argument assumes which of the following?

options.text: Copyright infringement requires exact reproduction of an existing work rather than the use of protected stylistic elements, compositional approaches, or substantially similar visual content derived from copyrighted training data.
AI-generated art is of lower quality than human-created art.
Artists do not want AI tools to exist.
The AI tool generates images faster than human artists can create them.
All of the images in the tool's training data were in the public domain.

explanation: The company assumes non-reproduction means non-infringement. But copyright law may protect style, substantial similarity, or derivative works. (A) identifies this legal assumption.

passage: Researchers hypothesize that the mass extinction at the end of the Permian period, which eliminated approximately 90 percent of marine species, was triggered by massive volcanic eruptions in what is now Siberia. These eruptions, they argue, released enormous quantities of carbon dioxide and sulfur dioxide, causing rapid climate change and ocean acidification.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, is an assumption underlying the researchers' hypothesis?

options.text: Geochemical analysis of rock layers from the Permian-Triassic boundary worldwide reveals a sharp spike in volcanic mercury and carbon isotope ratios consistent with massive volcanic carbon release, precisely coinciding with the onset of species disappearances in the fossil record.
Mass extinctions have occurred at several other points in Earth's history.
The Siberian volcanic formations are among the largest known volcanic deposits on Earth.
Some marine species survived the Permian extinction.
Modern volcanic eruptions also release carbon dioxide.

explanation: Geochemical evidence from the exact boundary layer linking volcanic signatures to extinction timing provides strong causal support. (A) strengthens.

passage: An advertisement claims that 9 out of 10 users of a particular skin cream reported smoother skin after two weeks of use.
questionStem: Which of the following would be most useful in evaluating this claim?

options.text: How many total users were surveyed to produce the 9-out-of-10 statistic.
Whether the skin cream is available without a prescription.
What ingredients the skin cream contains.
Whether the skin cream is sold in multiple countries.
How much the skin cream costs per unit.

explanation: If only 10 people were surveyed, 9 out of 10 is far less convincing than if 900 out of 1,000 reported improvement. Sample size is critical. (A) evaluates this.

passage: A politician argues: 'My opponent wants to reduce military spending. But we live in a dangerous world with many threats. Anyone who wants to cut military spending clearly does not care about national security.'
questionStem: If the statements above are true, which of the following must also be true?

options.text: presents a false dichotomy by assuming that wanting to reduce military spending means not caring about national security, ignoring the possibility that the opponent believes security can be maintained or improved through more efficient spending or alternative strategies.
does not specify by how much the opponent wants to reduce spending.
assumes military spending is the most important issue in the election.
fails to mention which specific threats require military spending.
attacks the opponent's character rather than addressing the substance of the spending proposal.

explanation: Reduced spending does not equal reduced security. More efficient use, different strategies, or reallocation could maintain security. (A) identifies the false dichotomy and straw man.

passage: All members of the board of directors own shares in the company. Some board members also serve as executive officers. No employee who is not a board member is permitted to own more than 5 percent of the company's shares. Executive officer Thompson owns 8 percent of the company's shares.
questionStem: If the above statements are true, which of the following must be true?

options.text: Thompson is the largest shareholder in the company.
Thompson is a member of the board of directors.
All executive officers own more than 5 percent of the company's shares.
Thompson has been on the board longer than other members.
Non-board employees own no shares in the company.

explanation: Thompson owns 8%, which exceeds the 5% limit for non-board employees. Therefore Thompson must be a board member. (B) must be true. (A) is unsupported. (C) overgeneralizes. (E) is not stated—they can own up to 5%.

passage: A dentist recommends flossing daily, arguing that it prevents gum disease by removing plaque and food particles from between teeth where toothbrushes cannot reach.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, is an assumption underlying the dentist's recommendation?

options.text: A long-term clinical study found that patients who flossed daily had significantly lower rates of gum disease than those who only brushed.
Flossing is inexpensive and widely available.
Some patients find flossing uncomfortable.
Electric toothbrushes are more effective than manual toothbrushes.
Gum disease affects a small percentage of the population.

explanation: Direct clinical evidence showing flossing reduces gum disease supports the recommendation. (A) strengthens.

passage: A coffee chain introduced a loyalty rewards program that offers a free drink after every ten purchases. Customer frequency of visits increased by 20 percent. Surprisingly, the chain's profit margins decreased during the same period.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, most helps to explain the decrease in profit margins?

options.text: The free drinks, combined with the fact that loyalty members tended to order the most expensive menu items for their free reward, cost the chain more per redeemed reward than anticipated, and the increased visit frequency did not generate enough incremental revenue to offset these costs.
The loyalty program was promoted through social media advertising.
Some customers who joined the loyalty program had already been frequent visitors.
The chain expanded its menu during the same period.
Competing coffee chains do not offer loyalty programs.

explanation: If members claimed expensive free drinks and the program cost exceeded incremental revenue, margins would decline despite more visits. (A) explains this.

passage: Some policymakers advocate banning autonomous vehicles until they can be proven 100 percent safe. **However, no technology, including human-driven vehicles, meets this standard of perfection. Human drivers cause approximately 40,000 traffic fatalities annually in the United States alone.** Autonomous vehicles, while imperfect, have already demonstrated significantly lower accident rates per mile driven in testing environments. **Therefore, requiring perfection from autonomous vehicles while tolerating far greater risks from human drivers represents an irrational double standard that, if maintained, would prevent a technology that could save thousands of lives from reaching the public.**
questionStem: The two boldface portions serve which of the following roles in the argument?

options.text: The first provides evidence undermining the policymakers' standard; the second states the argument's conclusion based on that evidence and the comparison with autonomous vehicle data.
The first is a concession to the policymakers; the second is the argument's rebuttal.
The first and second both provide evidence supporting the policymakers' position.
The first presents the argument's conclusion; the second supports it.
The first is a premise the argument opposes; the second offers a compromise.

explanation: The first boldface challenges the 100% safety standard by showing human driving fails it too. The second draws the conclusion that maintaining this standard is a double standard that costs lives. (A) is correct.

passage: A homeowner concludes that installing a home security system will prevent burglaries because homes with security systems are statistically less likely to be burglarized.
questionStem: The homeowner's conclusion assumes which of the following?

options.text: Home security systems are affordable for most homeowners.

The lower burglary rate for homes with security systems is at least partly because the systems deter burglars rather than solely because homes in safer neighborhoods are more likely to have systems installed.
Insurance companies offer discounts for homes with security systems.
Burglars are aware of which homes have security systems.
All security systems are equally effective.

explanation: If the correlation exists only because safer neighborhoods have more systems, installing one in a high-crime area may not help. The homeowner assumes systems actually deter burglars. (B) is the assumption.

passage: Advocates of four-day school weeks argue that the longer weekends give teachers time to prepare better lessons and reduce burnout. Critics worry about lost instructional time. Evidence from districts that have adopted four-day weeks shows no decline in test scores, but also shows that the primary beneficiaries have been teachers and administrators rather than students. This suggests that the four-day school week ________.
questionStem: Which of the following most logically completes the argument?

options.text: should be immediately adopted by all school districts.
may improve working conditions for educators without significantly affecting student academic outcomes, either positively or negatively.
harms student academic achievement.
is primarily motivated by cost savings rather than educational improvement.
will lead to longer school days that exhaust students.

explanation: No test score decline means no academic harm. Benefits accrue to teachers. The evidence points to educator improvement without student impact. (B) logically completes.

passage: A technology executive argues: 'Our smartphone has the best camera on the market because it received the highest score from a leading technology review website. No other smartphone received a higher score. Therefore, consumers who want the best camera phone should buy our product.'
questionStem: If the statements above are true, which of the following must also be true?

options.text: equates a single reviewer's assessment with objective superiority, without considering that different review criteria, weighting schemes, or testing conditions might produce different rankings, and that consumer needs may differ from the reviewer's evaluation framework.
does not mention the price of the smartphone.
ignores the other features of the smartphone besides the camera.
assumes all consumers read the same technology review website.
does not compare the camera to dedicated cameras rather than other smartphones.

explanation: One review with one methodology does not establish objective superiority. Different criteria could change rankings. Consumer preferences may differ from the review's framework. (A) identifies this over-reliance on a single authority.

passage: All of the company's international offices are located in countries where English is an official language. The company's Tokyo office is its largest Asian office.
questionStem: If the statements above are true, which of the following must also be true?

options.text: The Tokyo office is an international office.
English is an official language of Japan.
Japan may or may not be a country where English is an official language.
The Tokyo office does not conduct business in English.
The company has offices only in English-speaking countries.

explanation: We know international offices are in English-official countries, and Tokyo is the largest Asian office. But we do not know if Tokyo is classified as an international office or a domestic one. If the company is Japanese, Tokyo could be the headquarters. (C) correctly states the uncertainty. (A) and (B) are not necessarily true.

passage: A car insurance company claims that its policyholders have fewer accidents than the national average, using this as evidence that its customers are safer drivers.
questionStem: Which of the following would be most useful in evaluating whether the company's customers are actually safer drivers?

options.text: Whether the company's underwriting process selects for low-risk drivers by charging higher premiums to those with accident histories, thereby creating a self-selecting pool of already-safe drivers.
How much the company spends on advertising annually.
Whether the company offers roadside assistance.
How long the company has been in business.
Whether the company operates in all 50 states.

explanation: If the company screens out high-risk drivers, its pool is pre-selected for safety. The low accident rate reflects selection, not company influence. (A) evaluates this crucial confound.

passage: Marine biologists have proposed that microplastic pollution in the ocean disrupts the feeding behavior of zooplankton, which forms the base of marine food chains. They hypothesize that zooplankton ingest microplastics, mistaking them for food, leading to malnutrition and population declines that cascade up the food chain.
questionStem: Which of the following, if true, is an assumption underlying this hypothesis?

options.text: Laboratory experiments show that zooplankton exposed to microplastic concentrations similar to those found in polluted ocean areas consume 40 percent less actual food and produce 50 percent fewer offspring than unexposed populations.
Microplastic pollution has been detected in every major ocean.
Some species of zooplankton are more abundant now than they were a decade ago.
Larger marine organisms also ingest microplastics.
Microplastic particles come in a variety of shapes and sizes.

explanation: Lab evidence showing reduced feeding and reproduction at realistic concentrations directly supports the proposed mechanism. (A) strongly strengthens. (B) shows the problem is widespread but not that it affects feeding.

passage: Central bank independence is often cited as essential for sound monetary policy. The reasoning is that politicians, facing election pressures, tend to favor short-term stimulus over long-term price stability. However, independent central banks are run by unelected officials making decisions that profoundly affect citizens' economic lives. In democratic societies, this creates a tension because ________.
questionStem: Which of the following most logically completes the argument?

options.text: central banks should not exist in democratic societies.

the very feature that makes central bank independence desirable for economic stability—insulation from democratic pressures—simultaneously undermines the democratic principle that consequential policy decisions should be subject to public accountability.
politicians are better suited than economists to make monetary policy decisions.
central bank independence has always led to better economic outcomes.
democratic societies do not need stable monetary policy.

explanation: The argument sets up a tension between economic stability (requiring insulation) and democratic accountability. (B) articulates this exact tension. (A) and (E) are extreme. (C) contradicts the premise. (D) is unsupported.

passage: A real estate developer argues that building luxury condominiums in a low-income neighborhood will benefit current residents because the increased property values will raise the value of their homes.
questionStem: Which of the following would be most important in evaluating whether this development would actually benefit current residents?

options.text: Whether the increased property values would also lead to higher property taxes and living costs that could force current residents—many of whom rent rather than own—to leave the neighborhood before any benefits materialize.
How long the construction would take.
Whether the condominiums would include ground-floor retail space.
How the luxury condominiums would compare in price to existing housing.
Whether the developer has built similar projects in other cities.

explanation: Higher property values help only homeowners. If most residents rent, higher values mean higher rents and displacement. Additionally, higher taxes burden even owners. (A) evaluates the critical question of who actually benefits.
